blocks:
Ayahs

ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَلَمْ يَجْعَل لَّهُۥ عِوَجَا ۜ (١)
All praise belongs to Allah, who has sent down the Book to His servant and did not let any crookedness be in it, (1)
قَيِّمًا لِّيُنذِرَ بَأْسًا شَدِيدًا مِّن لَّدُنْهُ وَيُبَشِّرَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ أَجْرًا حَسَنًا (٢)
[a Book] upright, to warn of a severe punishment from Him, and to give good news to the faithful who do righteous deeds, that there shall be for them a good reward, (2)
مَّـٰكِثِينَ فِيهِ أَبَدًا (٣)
to abide in it forever, (3)
وَيُنذِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَدًا (٤)
and to warn those who say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ (4)
مَّا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ وَلَا لِـَٔابَآئِهِمْ ۚ كَبُرَتْ كَلِمَةً تَخْرُجُ مِنْ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ ۚ إِن يَقُولُونَ إِلَّا كَذِبًا (٥)
They do not have any knowledge of that, nor did their fathers. Monstrous is the utterance that comes out of their mouths, and they say nothing but a lie. (5)
فَلَعَلَّكَ بَـٰخِعٌ نَّفْسَكَ عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِمْ إِن لَّمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ أَسَفًا (٦)
You are liable to imperil your life out of grief for their sake, if they should not believe this discourse. (6)
إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا مَا عَلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَّهَا لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا (٧)
Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct. (7)
وَإِنَّا لَجَـٰعِلُونَ مَا عَلَيْهَا صَعِيدًا جُرُزًا (٨)
And indeed We will turn whatever is on it into a barren plain. (8)

(Commentary)

The Surah encompasses the call to true belief (الاعتقاد الحق) and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) through warning (الإنذار) and the bearing of good news (التبشير), as indicated by the two opening verses and its conclusion in His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنْ كَانَ يَرْجُو لِقَاءَ رَبِّهِ فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلًا صَالِحًا وَلَا يُشْرِكْ بِعِبَادَةِ رَبِّهِ أَحَدًا "So whoever expects to encounter his Lord—let him act righteously, and not associate anyone with the worship of his Lord." (18:110)

Furthermore, it contains a profound emphasis on negating the concept of offspring [attributed to Allah], as evidenced by the specific mention of warning those who claim He has a son, following the general mention of warning. I refer to the occurrence of His saying: وَيُنْذِرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَدًا "and to warn those who say, ‘Allah has taken a son’" (18:4) after His saying: لِيُنْذِرَ بَأْسًا شَدِيدًا مِنْ لَدُنْهُ "to warn of a severe punishment from Him" (18:2).

Thus, the discourse is directed toward the polytheists (الوثنيين) who claimed the angels, the jinn, and the righteous among men were the offspring of Allah; and toward the Christians who claimed the sonship of the Messiah (عليه السلام); and perhaps the Jews share in this, as the Quran mentions their saying that Ezra (عزير) is the son of Allah.

It is not far-fetched to suggest that the primary purpose of the Surah’s revelation was to recount the three wondrous stories not mentioned in the Noble Quran except in this Surah: the story of the People of the Cave (أصحاب الكهف), the story of Moses and his servant on their journey to the junction of the two seas, and the story of Dhu’l-Qarnayn. From these, the discourse then extracted the negation of partners (الشريك) and the exhortation toward godwariness (تقوى) of Allah, the Glorified.

The Surah is Meccan (مكية), as inferred from the context of its verses. An exception has been made for His saying: وَاصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ "And content yourself with those who pray to their Lord" (18:28); the discussion regarding this will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ عَلَى عَبْدِهِ الْكِتَابَ وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ عِوَجًا قَيِّمًا "All praise belongs to Allah, who has sent down the Book to His servant and did not let any crookedness be in it, [making it] upright" (18:1-2). The term ‘iwaj (العوج), with either a fatha or kasra on the ‘ayn, means deviation (الانحراف). It is stated in al-Majma‘: "‘Awaj (with fatha) refers to that which is perceived by the eye, such as a spear or a piece of wood; while ‘iwaj (with kasra) refers to that which does not have a physical standing, such as religion or speech." End quote.

Perhaps what is meant by "what is seen" and "what is not seen" is that which is easily perceived versus that which is difficult, as mentioned by al-Raghib in al-Mufradat: "‘Awaj (with fatha) is used for what is easily perceived by sight, like an upright piece of wood and the like; while ‘iwaj (with kasra) is used for what is perceived by thought and insight (البصيرة), such as a level land whose unevenness is known only through insight, or like religion and livelihood." End quote. Thus, there is no contradiction with His saying: لَا تَرَى فِيهَا عِوَجًا وَلَا أَمْتًا "wherein you will see no crookedness (عِوَجًا) or unevenness" (20:107). Reflect on this.

The Exalted opened the discourse in this Surah by praising Himself for what He revealed to His servant—a Quran in which there is no deviation (انحراف) from the truth in any respect, and which is upright (قيم) over the interests of His servants in their worldly life and the Hereafter. To Him belongs all praise for the goodness and blessings resulting from its revelation, from the day it descended until the Day of Resurrection. A critical researcher should not doubt that the righteousness and integrity found in human society are among the blessings of what the noble Prophets spread in their call to the true word, virtuous character, and righteous deeds. Furthermore, the distinction of the era of the Quran throughout its fourteen centuries over preceding eras—in terms of the elevation of human society and its progress in beneficial knowledge or righteous action—is an area where the Quran has its specific impact and the Prophetic call has its beautiful favors. For all of that, praise belongs entirely to Allah.

From this, it becomes clear that the view of some commentators—that the verse means "Say: All praise belongs to Allah who revealed..."—is not as it should be.

His saying: وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ عِوَجًا "and did not let any crookedness be in it." The pronoun refers to the Book, and the clause is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the Book. His saying: قَيِّمًا "upright" is a second circumstantial qualifier, as suggested by the context. For the Exalted is in the station of praising Himself for revealing a Book described as having no crookedness and being upright over the interests of human society. The focus is directed toward both attributes equally, which is the implication of them being circumstantial qualifiers of the Book.

It has been said that the clause وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ عِوَجًا is coordinated (معطوفة) with the relative clause, and قَيِّمًا is a circumstantial qualifier for the pronoun in لِلَّهِ. The meaning would then be: "He who did not make crookedness for the Book while it was upright." Alternatively, قَيِّمًا is governed by an elided verb, meaning: "He who did not make crookedness for it and made it upright." Both views necessitate a division of focus between the act of revelation itself and the Book being upright without crookedness. You have already seen that this contradicts what is inferred from the context.

It has also been said that there is a transposition (تقديم وتأخير) in the verse, the intended meaning being: "He revealed the Book upright and did not make any crookedness for it." This is the weakest of the views.

The negation of crookedness was mentioned before the affirmation of uprightness because the former is the perfection (كمال) of the Book in itself, while the latter is its perfecting (تكميل) of others; perfection naturally precedes perfecting.

The occurrence of "crookedness" (عِوَجًا) as an indefinite noun in the context of negation implies generality (العموم). Thus, the Quran is straight in all its aspects: eloquent in its wording, profound in its meaning, accurate in its guidance, vital in its proofs and demonstrations, sincere in its commands and prohibitions, truthful in the stories and reports it narrates, and decisive in its judgments. It is protected from the interference of devils; there is no inconsistency in it, and falsehood (الباطل) — that which is contrary to reality and truth — cannot approach it from before it or from behind it.

The "upright" (القيم) is one who maintains the welfare of a thing and manages its affairs, like the "overseer of a house" (قيم الدار) who manages its interests and to whom its affairs are referred. The Book is only upright by virtue of the meanings it contains. What the Quran encompasses is true belief and righteous deeds, as He, the Exalted, said: يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ وَإِلَى طَرِيقٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ "it guides to the truth and to a straight path" (46:30). This is the religion (الدين), and the Exalted has described His religion in several places in His Book as being upright (قيم), saying: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ الْقَيِّمِ "So set your heart on the upright religion" (30:43). Accordingly, the description of the Book as "upright" is due to the upright religion it contains, which maintains the interests of the human world in this life and the next.

Sometimes the matter is reversed, where uprightness is taken as a description of the Book and then of the religion because of it, as in His saying: وَذَلِكَ دِينُ الْقَيِّمَةِ "and that is the upright religion" (98:5). The apparent meaning is "the religion of the upright books," which is a form of metaphorical usage (تجوز).

It has been said that "upright" (القيم) means straight and balanced, without excess or deficiency. It has also been said that "upright" means the regulator of all other heavenly books, confirming them, preserving them, and abrogating their laws. The follow-up of the word with His saying: لِيُنْذِرَ بَأْسًا شَدِيدًا مِنْ لَدُنْهُ وَيُبَشِّرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "to warn of a severe punishment from Him and to give the good news to the believers..." supports what we have previously stated.

His saying, the Exalted: لِيُنْذِرَ بَأْسًا شَدِيدًا مِنْ لَدُنْهُ وَيُبَشِّرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ الصَّالِحَاتِ "to warn of a severe punishment from Him and to give the good news to the believers who do righteous deeds" (18:2). That is, to warn the disbelievers of a severe punishment originating from Allah. So it has been said. However, it appears from the context of qualifying the believers who receive good news as الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ الصَّالِحَاتِ "those who do righteous deeds," that the elided object of the warning is "those who do not do righteous deeds," which is more general than those who do not believe at all or those who believe but act wickedly.

In any case, the sentence is an exposition (بيان) of His revealing the Book to His servant as straight and upright; for if it were not straight in itself and upright over others, neither warning nor the bearing of good news would be sound. This is evident.

The "goodly reward" (الأجر الحسن) refers to Paradise (الجنة), as evidenced by His saying in the following verse: مَاكِثِينَ فِيهِ أَبَدًا "to remain in it forever" (18:3). The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيُنْذِرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَدًا "and to warn those who say, ‘Allah has taken a son’" (18:4). These are the general polytheists who claimed that the angels were His sons or daughters, and sometimes said the same of the jinn and the righteous among men; and the Christians who claimed that the Messiah is the son of Allah. The Quran also attributed to the Jews the saying that Ezra is the son of Allah.

The specific mention of their warning a second time, after mentioning it generally the first time in His saying: لِيُنْذِرَ بَأْسًا شَدِيدًا مِنْ لَدُنْهُ, is due to the increased importance (الاهتمام) of their matter.

His saying, the Exalted: مَا لَهُمْ بِهِ مِنْ عِلْمٍ وَلَا لِآبَائِهِمْ كَبُرَتْ كَلِمَةً تَخْرُجُ مِنْ أَفْوَاهِهِمْ "They do not have any knowledge of it, nor did their fathers. Monstrous is the utterance that comes out of their mouths" (18:5). Most of them intended by their saying "Allah has taken a son" the reality of procreation (التوليد), as indicated by His saying: أَنَّى يَكُونُ لَهُ وَلَدٌ وَلَمْ تَكُنْ لَهُ صَاحِبَةٌ وَخَلَقَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ "How could He have a child when He has no mate? He created all things" (6:101).

The Glorified refuted their claim, first by stating it was a saying born of ignorance without knowledge, and second by His saying at the end of the verse: إِنْ يَقُولُونَ إِلَّا كَذِبًا "they say nothing but a lie" (18:5).

His saying: مَا لَهُمْ بِهِ مِنْ عِلْمٍ "They do not have any knowledge of it" included all of them, both fathers and sons. However, since they used to defer the knowledge of it to their fathers, saying, "This is the creed of our fathers and they are more knowledgeable than us, and we have only to follow and emulate them," the Exalted distinguished between them and their fathers. He negated knowledge from them first, and from their fathers upon whom they relied second, so as to invalidate both their claim and their argument entirely.

His saying: كَبُرَتْ كَلِمَةً تَخْرُجُ مِنْ أَفْوَاهِهِمْ "Monstrous is the utterance that comes out of their mouths" is a condemnation of them and an expression of the gravity of their saying "Allah has taken a son," due to the immense audacity against Allah, the Glorified, by attributing to Him a partner, corporeality (التجسم), composition (التركب), and the need for a helper or a successor. Exalted is He far above that.

It may be that in the speech of their predecessors, the term "son" was applied to some of His creation as an honorific (تشريف) to indicate proximity to Him and special status, similar to the saying of the Jews recounted by the Quran: "Ezra is the son of Allah," and their saying: "We are the sons of Allah and His beloved ones." Likewise, in the speech of several of their ancients, the term "son" was applied to some of the first creations in the sense of proceeding from Him as a son proceeds from a father, and the terms "spouse" or "mate" were applied to the intermediaries of emanation and creation, just as a man's spouse is the intermediary for the proceeding of a child from him. Thus, the term "spouse" was applied to some angels of the first creation, and "son" or "daughter" to others.

These two usages, even if they do not contain exactly what the first usage contained—being metaphorical for the sake of honor and the like—are nonetheless prohibited by law (شرعا). It is sufficient reason for their prohibition that they, and their like, lead the masses of people toward eternal misery and everlasting destruction.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَعَلَّكَ بَاخِعٌ نَفْسَكَ عَلَى آثَارِهِمْ إِنْ لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِهَذَا الْحَدِيثِ أَسَفًا "You might perhaps kill yourself with grief on their account if they do not believe in this discourse" (18:6).

Bakhu‘ (البخوع) and bakh‘ (البخع) mean killing and destroying. Athar (الآثار) are the footprints of passersby on the ground. Asaf (الأسف) is intense grief. "This discourse" (هذا الحديث) refers to the Quran.

This verse and the two following it are in the station of consoling the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), comforting him, and soothing his soul. The "fa" (ف) is to branch the discourse (تفريع) upon their disbelief and denial of Allah's signs, understood from the preceding verses. The meaning is: it is expected of you that you will destroy yourself after their turning away from the Quran and their departure from you out of intense grief. He indicated their turning away and backsliding by His saying: عَلَى آثَارِهِمْ "on their account [literally: upon their tracks]," which is a metaphor (استعارة).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا مَا عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَهَا لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا "Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct" (18:7) until the end of the two verses. Adornment (الزينة) is a beautiful thing added to something to grant it a beauty for which it is desired. Sa‘id (الصعيد) is the surface of the earth. Juruz (الجرز), according to al-Majma‘, is land that does not produce vegetation, as if it "eats" (تأكل) the plants entirely.

In these two verses, He has provided a wondrous explanation of the reality of human earthly life: that human souls—which are in their essential substance (جوهر) celestial and noble—would not have inclined toward the earth and life upon it. Yet Allah decreed that their perfection and eternal happiness be achieved through true belief and righteous deeds. Thus, Divine Providence employed a means to station them in the position of belief and action, to bring them to the touchstone of purification and refinement, and to settle them on the earth for a known term. This was done by casting a bond and attachment between them and the provisions of life on earth—such as wealth, children, and status—and making these beloved to their hearts. Thus, what is on the earth, being beautiful and beloved to them in themselves, became an adornment for the earth and a jewelry with which it is bedecked because it is upon it. Consequently, their souls became attached to the earth because of it and found tranquility therein.

Then, when the term decreed by Allah for their stay on earth expires, having realized the trial and test (البلاء والامتحان) He intended, Allah severs the attachment between them and what is on the earth. He erases its beauty and adornment, and it becomes like a barren land (صعيد جرز) with no vegetation or freshness upon it. The call to depart is issued to them, and they come forth solitary, just as Allah created them the first time.

This is the Sunnah (سنة) — the established way and law — of Allah in the creation of man, his habitation of the earth, and the adornment of what is upon it for him, in order to test him thereby and distinguish the people of happiness from others. Thus, He brings generation after generation and individual after individual, adorning for them the provisions of life on the face of the earth, then leaving them to their choice to test them. Then, when the test is complete, He severs the connection between the person and the adorned vanities of the world and transfers him from the abode of action to the abode of recompense. The Exalted said: وَلَوْ تَرَى إِذِ الظَّالِمُونَ فِي غَمَرَاتِ الْمَوْتِ وَالْمَلَائِكَةُ بَاسِطُوا أَيْدِيهِمْ أَخْرِجُوا أَنْفُسَكُمُ "Were you to see when the wrongdoers are in the throes of death, and the angels stretch out their hands [saying], ‘Surrender your souls!..." until He said: وَلَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونَا فُرَادَى كَمَا خَلَقْنَاكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَتَرَكْتُمْ مَا خَوَّلْنَاكُمْ وَرَاءَ ظُهُورِكُمْ وَمَا نَرَى مَعَكُمْ شُفَعَاءَكُمُ الَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ أَنَّهُمْ فِيكُمْ شُرَكَاءُ لَقَدْ تَقَطَّعَ بَيْنَكُمْ وَضَلَّ عَنْكُمْ مَا كُنْتُمْ تَزْعُمُونَ "‘Certainly you have come to Us alone, just as We created you the first time, and you have left behind your backs whatever We had bestowed on you. We do not see your intercessors with you—those whom you claimed to be [Our] partners in [deciding] your [fate]. Certainly [the ties] between you have been severed, and what you used to claim has forsaken you.’" (6:93-94).

The summary of the verse's meaning is: Do not be distressed or grieve over them because they turned away from your call of warning and good news and occupied themselves with enjoying the provisions of life. They are not outstripping [Allah] nor can they frustrate [Him]. Rather, the reality of this life of theirs is a type of Divine subjugation; We settled them on the earth, then made what is on the earth an adornment that fascinates the onlooker so that their souls become attached to it, that We may test them as to which of them is best in conduct. And We shall surely make this very thing that was adorned for them like a barren land, having no vegetation or anything the soul desires. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, did not will for all of them to believe such that He would be overcome by their disbelief in the Book and their persistence in misguidance, leading you to kill yourself with grief on their account. Rather, He intended for them trial and examination, and He, the Glorified, is the Victor in whatever He wills and intends.

It has become clear from the foregoing that His saying: وَإِنَّا لَجَاعِلُونَ مَا عَلَيْهَا صَعِيدًا جُرُزًا "And We will indeed turn whatever is on it into a barren ground" (18:8) is a metaphor by way of metonymy (استعارة بالكناية). What is intended is the severing of the bond of attachment between man and the provisions of worldly life on the earth.

It has also been said that what is intended is the literal meaning of "barren ground" (صعيد جرز), meaning: We shall return the adornment on the earth to dust level with the ground, making it a smooth surface with no plants and nothing upon it.

His saying: مَا عَلَيْهَا "whatever is on it" is an instance of placing a manifest noun in place of a pronoun; the natural flow of speech would have been "And We shall surely make it (لَجَاعِلُوهُ)." Perhaps the subtle point (النكتة) is the increased focus on the description of it being "upon the earth."

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from al-Barqi, reaching back to Abu Basir, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding: لِيُنْذِرَ بَأْسًا شَدِيدًا مِنْ لَدُنْهُ "to warn of a severe punishment from Him." He said: "The severe punishment (البأس الشديد) is Ali (peace be upon him), and he is 'from Him' [meaning] from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—he fought his enemies alongside him. That is His saying: لِيُنْذِرَ بَأْسًا شَدِيدًا مِنْ لَدُنْهُ."

I say: It was also narrated by Ibn Shahr Ashub from al-Baqir and al-Sadiq (peace be upon them). This is an instance of application (التطبيق) and is not [the literal] exegesis (تفسير).

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu’l-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: فَلَعَلَّكَ بَاخِعٌ نَفْسَكَ "You might perhaps kill yourself." He says: "Killing yourself on their account." As for أَسَفًا "with grief," he says: "out of sadness."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Hakim in al-Tarikh extracted from Ibn Umar, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) recited this verse: لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا 'that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct.' I said: 'What is the meaning of that, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'To test you as to which of you is best in intellect (عقلا), most wary of the prohibitions of Allah, and quickest in obedience to Allah.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu’l-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: صَعِيدًا جُرُزًا "a barren ground." He said: "Having no vegetation in it."

Ayahs

أَمْ حَسِبْتَ أَنَّ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْكَهْفِ وَٱلرَّقِيمِ كَانُوا۟ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا عَجَبًا (٩)
Do you suppose that the Companions of the Cave and the Inscription were among Our wonderful signs? (9)
إِذْ أَوَى ٱلْفِتْيَةُ إِلَى ٱلْكَهْفِ فَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ رَحْمَةً وَهَيِّئْ لَنَا مِنْ أَمْرِنَا رَشَدًا (١٠)
When the youths took refuge in the Cave, they said, ‘Our Lord! Grant us a mercy from Yourself, and help us on to rectitude in our affair.’ (10)
فَضَرَبْنَا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ فِى ٱلْكَهْفِ سِنِينَ عَدَدًا (١١)
So We put them to sleep in the Cave for several years. (11)
ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَـٰهُمْ لِنَعْلَمَ أَىُّ ٱلْحِزْبَيْنِ أَحْصَىٰ لِمَا لَبِثُوٓا۟ أَمَدًا (١٢)
Then We aroused them that We might know which of the two groups better reckoned the period they had stayed. (12)
نَّحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ نَبَأَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ فِتْيَةٌ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ وَزِدْنَـٰهُمْ هُدًى (١٣)
We relate to you their account in truth. They were indeed youths who had faith in their Lord, and We had enhanced them in guidance, (13)
وَرَبَطْنَا عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ إِذْ قَامُوا۟ فَقَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ لَن نَّدْعُوَا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ إِلَـٰهًا ۖ لَّقَدْ قُلْنَآ إِذًا شَطَطًا (١٤)
and fortified their hearts, when they stood up and said, ‘Our Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth. We will never invoke any god besides Him, for then we shall certainly have said an atrocious lie. (14)
هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ قَوْمُنَا ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً ۖ لَّوْلَا يَأْتُونَ عَلَيْهِم بِسُلْطَـٰنٍۭ بَيِّنٍ ۖ فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا (١٥)
These—our people—have taken gods besides Him. Why do they not bring any clear authority touching them? So who is a greater wrongdoer than he who fabricates a lie against Allah? (15)
وَإِذِ ٱعْتَزَلْتُمُوهُمْ وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ فَأْوُۥٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلْكَهْفِ يَنشُرْ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُم مِّن رَّحْمَتِهِۦ وَيُهَيِّئْ لَكُم مِّنْ أَمْرِكُم مِّرْفَقًا (١٦)
When you have dissociated yourselves from them and from what they worship except Allah, then take refuge in the Cave. Your Lord will unfold His mercy for you, and He will help you on to ease in your affair.’ (16)
۞ وَتَرَى ٱلشَّمْسَ إِذَا طَلَعَت تَّزَٰوَرُ عَن كَهْفِهِمْ ذَاتَ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَإِذَا غَرَبَت تَّقْرِضُهُمْ ذَاتَ ٱلشِّمَالِ وَهُمْ فِى فَجْوَةٍ مِّنْهُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ مَن يَهْدِ ٱللَّهُ فَهُوَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِ ۖ وَمَن يُضْلِلْ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ وَلِيًّا مُّرْشِدًا (١٧)
You may see the sun, when it rises, slanting toward the right of their cave, and, when it sets, cut across them towards the left, and they are in a cavern within it. That is one of Allah’s signs. Whomever Allah guides is rightly guided, and whomever He leads astray, you will never find for him any guardian or guide. (17)
وَتَحْسَبُهُمْ أَيْقَاظًا وَهُمْ رُقُودٌ ۚ وَنُقَلِّبُهُمْ ذَاتَ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَذَاتَ ٱلشِّمَالِ ۖ وَكَلْبُهُم بَـٰسِطٌ ذِرَاعَيْهِ بِٱلْوَصِيدِ ۚ لَوِ ٱطَّلَعْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَوَلَّيْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِرَارًا وَلَمُلِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ رُعْبًا (١٨)
You will suppose them to be awake, although they are asleep. We turn them to the right and to the left, and their dog [lies] stretching its forelegs at the threshold. If you come upon them, you will surely turn to flee from them, and you will surely be filled with a terror of them. (18)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ بَعَثْنَـٰهُمْ لِيَتَسَآءَلُوا۟ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ قَالَ قَآئِلٌ مِّنْهُمْ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَبِثْنَا يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ ۚ قَالُوا۟ رَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا لَبِثْتُمْ فَٱبْعَثُوٓا۟ أَحَدَكُم بِوَرِقِكُمْ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ إِلَى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ فَلْيَنظُرْ أَيُّهَآ أَزْكَىٰ طَعَامًا فَلْيَأْتِكُم بِرِزْقٍ مِّنْهُ وَلْيَتَلَطَّفْ وَلَا يُشْعِرَنَّ بِكُمْ أَحَدًا (١٩)
So it was that We aroused them [from sleep] so that they might question one another. One of them said, ‘How long have you stayed [here]?’ They said, ‘We have stayed a day, or part of a day.’ They said, ‘Your Lord knows best how long you have stayed. Send one of you to the city with this money. Let him observe which of them has the purest food, and bring you provisions from there. Let him be attentive, and let him not make anyone aware of you. (19)
إِنَّهُمْ إِن يَظْهَرُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ يَرْجُمُوكُمْ أَوْ يُعِيدُوكُمْ فِى مِلَّتِهِمْ وَلَن تُفْلِحُوٓا۟ إِذًا أَبَدًا (٢٠)
Indeed should they prevail over you, they will [either] stone you [to death], or force you back into their creed, and then you will never be saved. ’ (20)
وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَعْثَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَأَنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهَآ إِذْ يَتَنَـٰزَعُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَمْرَهُمْ ۖ فَقَالُوا۟ ٱبْنُوا۟ عَلَيْهِم بُنْيَـٰنًا ۖ رَّبُّهُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِهِمْ ۚ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ غَلَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرِهِمْ لَنَتَّخِذَنَّ عَلَيْهِم مَّسْجِدًا (٢١)
So it was that We let them come upon them, that they might know that Allah’s promise is true, and that there is no doubt in the Hour. As they disputed among themselves about their matter, they said, ‘Build a building over them. Their Lord knows them best.’ Those who had the say in their matter said, ‘We will set up a place of worship over them.’ (21)
سَيَقُولُونَ ثَلَـٰثَةٌ رَّابِعُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ وَيَقُولُونَ خَمْسَةٌ سَادِسُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ رَجْمًۢا بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۖ وَيَقُولُونَ سَبْعَةٌ وَثَامِنُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ ۚ قُل رَّبِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ بِعِدَّتِهِم مَّا يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ ۗ فَلَا تُمَارِ فِيهِمْ إِلَّا مِرَآءً ظَـٰهِرًا وَلَا تَسْتَفْتِ فِيهِم مِّنْهُمْ أَحَدًا (٢٢)
They will say, ‘[They are] three; their dog is the fourth of them,’ and say, ‘[They are] five, their dog is the sixth of them,’ taking a shot at the invisible. They will say, ‘[They are] seven, their dog is the eighth of them.’ Say, ‘My Lord knows best their number, and none knows them except a few.’ So do not dispute concerning them, except for a seeming dispute, and do not question about them any of them. (22)
وَلَا تَقُولَنَّ لِشَا۟ىْءٍ إِنِّى فَاعِلٌ ذَٰلِكَ غَدًا (٢٣)
Do not say about anything, ‘I will indeed do it tomorrow,’ (23)
إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ إِذَا نَسِيتَ وَقُلْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَهْدِيَنِ رَبِّى لِأَقْرَبَ مِنْ هَـٰذَا رَشَدًا (٢٤)
without [adding], ‘God willing.’ And when you forget, remember your Lord, and say, ‘Maybe my Lord will guide me to [something] more akin to rectitude than this.’ (24)
وَلَبِثُوا۟ فِى كَهْفِهِمْ ثَلَـٰثَ مِا۟ئَةٍ سِنِينَ وَٱزْدَادُوا۟ تِسْعًا (٢٥)
They remained in the Cave for three hundred years, and added nine more [to that number]. (25)
قُلِ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا لَبِثُوا۟ ۖ لَهُۥ غَيْبُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ أَبْصِرْ بِهِۦ وَأَسْمِعْ ۚ مَا لَهُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا يُشْرِكُ فِى حُكْمِهِۦٓ أَحَدًا (٢٦)
Say, ‘Allah knows best how long they remained. To Him belongs the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. How well does He see! How well does He hear! They have no guardian besides Him, and none shares with Him in His judgement.’ (26)

(Commentary)

The verses recount the story of the People of the Cave (أصحاب الكهف), which is one of the three matters that the Jews advised the Quraysh to ask the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) about, in order to test his truthfulness in his claim to prophethood. These were: the story of the People of the Cave, the story of Moses and his servant, and the story of Dhu’l-Qarnayn, according to what has been transmitted in the narrations. However, this story did not begin with an indication that the question pertained to it, as did the story of Dhu’l-Qarnayn: يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنْ ذِي الْقَرْنَيْنِ — "They ask you about Dhul-Qarnayn" (18:83), even if there is something at its conclusion that suggests this, such as His saying: وَلا تَقُولَنَّ لِشَيْءٍ إِنِّي فاعِلٌ ذلِكَ غَداً — "Do not say about anything, ‘I will indeed do it tomorrow’" (18:23), as will be discussed later.

The context of the three verses that open the story suggests that the account of the Cave was known in general terms before the revelation (الوحي) detailed the story, especially the context of His saying: أَمْ حَسِبْتَ أَنَّ أَصْحابَ الْكَهْفِ وَالرَّقِيمِ كانُوا مِنْ آياتِنا عَجَباً — "Do you suppose that the Companions of the Cave and the Tablet were a wonder among Our signs?" (18:9). What the revelation disclosed was the detail of their story, beginning from His saying: نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ نَبَأَهُمْ بِالْحَقِ — "We relate to you their supplement with the truth" (18:13) until the end of the verses.

The connection between the verses of the story and what preceded them is that by mentioning their story and denying that they were a unique wonder among the signs of Allah, He indicates that His act of making whatever is on the earth an adornment for it—to which man becomes attached and finds tranquility, devoting himself to it and becoming heedless of everything else for the purpose of trial (البلاء) and testing (الامتحان), and then making what is upon it after a few days a barren dust (صعيداً جرزاً) appearing to man as nothing but vanity and a mirage—all of this is but a divine sign (آية إلهية). This is analogous to what happened to the People of the Cave when Allah cast sleep upon them in a hollow of the cave for three hundred solar years; then, when He raised them, they did not reckon their stay except as a day or part of a day.

Thus, every human being's stay in this world, their preoccupation with its finery and adornments, and their infatuation with it while oblivious to all else, is a sign that resembles in its meaning the sign of the People of the Cave. Allah will surely raise mankind from this slumber and ask them: كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ عَدَدَ سِنِينَ قالُوا لَبِثْنَا يَوْماً أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ — "He will say, ‘How many years did you tarry on earth?’ They will say, ‘We tarried a day or part of a day’" (23:112-113); and كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَ ما يُوعَدُونَ لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا ساعَةً مِنْ نَهارٍ — "On the day they see what they are promised, it will be as if they had not tarried but an hour of a day" (46:35). Therefore, the sign of the People of the Cave is not an unprecedented wonder among the signs; rather, it is a recurring phenomenon, proceeding as days and nights proceed for man.

It is as if the Exalted, after saying: فَلَعَلَّكَ باخِعٌ نَفْسَكَ عَلى آثارِهِمْ إِنْ لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِهذَا الْحَدِيثِ أَسَفاً — "You might perhaps consume yourself with grief on their track should they not believe in this discourse" (18:6) through the completion of the three verses, addressed His Prophet as if to say: It is as though you did not realize that their preoccupation with the world and their lack of faith in this discourse—due to their attachment to the adornment of the earth—is a divine sign similar to the sign of the People of the Cave staying in their cave and then being raised. For this reason, you grieved and nearly killed yourself with sorrow; rather, you supposed that the People of the Cave were a unique wonder among Our signs, a rarity in this regard.

The reason this meaning was not stated explicitly was to safeguard the station of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) from having heedlessness (الغفلة) or distraction (الذهول) attributed to him, and because allusion (الكناية) is more eloquent than explicit statement.

This is what reflection (التدبر) yields regarding the connection of the story. In this manner, the context proceeds in the connection of what follows this story, such as the parable of the two men, one of whom had two gardens, and the story of Moses and his servant, the explanation of which will follow. Other views regarding the connection of the story have been mentioned, but they are not substantial, and there is no benefit in recounting them.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ حَسِبْتَ أَنَّ أَصْحابَ الْكَهْفِ وَالرَّقِيمِ كانُوا مِنْ آياتِنا عَجَباً — "Do you suppose that the Companions of the Cave and the Tablet were a wonder among Our signs?" (18:9). Reckoning (الحسبان) is to suppose or think. The Cave (الكهف) is a cavern in a mountain, except that it is wider; if it is small, it is called a grotto (غار). The Tablet (الرقيم) is derived from al-raqm (الرقم), which is writing and inscription; it is originally a passive participle (فعيل) meaning "that which is written," like al-jarih (الجريح) and al-qatil (القتيل) meaning the wounded and the slain. Wonder (العجب) is a verbal noun meaning amazement, used here as a descriptive attribute for emphasis.

The apparent context of the story is that the People of the Cave and the Tablet are a specific group, and the story is the story of all of them. Thus, they are called both the People of the Cave and the People of the Tablet. As for their being called the People of the Cave, it is because of their entry into the cave and what occurred to them therein.

As for their being called the People of the Tablet (الرقيم), it has been said that their story was inscribed on a tablet (لوح) placed there or kept in the treasury of kings; thus they were named the People of the Tablet. It has also been said that al-Raqim is the name of the mountain in which the cave was located, or the valley containing the mountain, or the city from which they emerged to the cave, or the dog that was with them—these are five opinions. What will follow in the discussion of their story will support the first opinion.

It has also been said that the People of the Tablet are different from the People of the Cave, and their story is different from theirs; Allah mentioned them with the People of the Cave but did not mention their story. A story has been narrated for them which we will point to in the upcoming discussion of narrations.

This is very far-fetched, for Allah would not, in His eloquent speech, refer to the story of two groups and then detail the account of one while not addressing the other at all, neither briefly nor in detail. Furthermore, the story they have cited for the People of the Tablet does not suit the preceding context, which necessitates the mention of the story of the People of the Cave.

It has become clear from what preceded regarding the connection of the story that the meaning of the verse is: Rather, did you think that the People of the Cave and the Tablet—whom Allah caused to sleep for hundreds of years and then awakened, whereupon they reckoned they had stayed for a day or part of a day—were a most wondrous sign among Our signs? No, they are not a unique wonder; for what happens to the generality of mankind—their infatuation with the adornment of the earth and their heedlessness of the matter of the Return (المعاد), then their being raised while they deem their stay in the world to be short—is a recurring sign that resembles the sign of the Cave.

The apparent context—as previously indicated—is that the story was known to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) in general terms at the time of the revelation of the story, and the primary concern was directed toward informing of its details. This is supported by the following of this verse with the three verses containing the summary of the story, as they mention the summary that leads to them being considered a wondrous and rare sign in their category.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ أَوَى الْفِتْيَةُ إِلَى الْكَهْفِ — "When the youths took refuge in the Cave" (18:10) until the end of the verse. Taking refuge (الأوي) is to return, but not just any return; it is the return of a human or animal to a place where they settle or seek to settle. Al-fityah (الفتية) is a broken plural for fata (فتى), and the youth (الفتى) is a young man; the word is not devoid of a nuance of praise.

Preparation (التهيئة) is to make ready. Al-Baydawi said: "The origin of al-tahyi'ah is to bring about the form (هيأة) of a thing." Right conduct (الرشد), with two fathas or a damma followed by a sukun, is to be guided to the objective. Al-Raghib said: "Al-rushd and al-rashad are the opposite of error (الغي), and it is used in the sense of guidance (الهداية)."

His saying: فَقالُوا رَبَّنا آتِنا مِنْ لَدُنْكَ رَحْمَةً — "they said, ‘Our Lord, grant us a mercy from Yourself’" (18:10) is a derivation of their supplication from their taking refuge, as if they were compelled by the loss of strength and the exhaustion of means to hasten to ask. This is supported by their saying: مِنْ لَدُنْكَ — "from Yourself." Had the paths not failed them, the means not been severed, and despair not surrounded them, they would not have conditioned the requested mercy to be specifically from Him, the Exalted; rather, they would have said: "Grant us mercy," as others said: رَبَّنا آتِنا فِي الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةً — "Our Lord, give us good in this world" (2:201), and رَبَّنا وَآتِنا ما وَعَدْتَنا عَلى رُسُلِكَ — "Our Lord, give us what You have promised us through Your apostles" (3:194). Thus, the intended meaning of the requested mercy is divine support (التأييد الإلهي), for there is no supporter besides Him.

It is possible that the intended meaning of the mercy requested from Him is some of the gifts and blessings exclusive to Him, the Exalted, such as guidance (الهداية), which He explicitly states in various places of His speech is from Him specifically. This is suggested by the restriction in His saying مِنْ لَدُنْكَ, and it is supported by the occurrence of its like in the supplication of those firmly grounded in knowledge (الراسخون في العلم) quoted in His saying: رَبَّنا لا تُزِغْ قُلُوبَنا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَيْتَنا وَهَبْ لَنا مِنْ لَدُنْكَ رَحْمَةً — "Our Lord, do not make our hearts swerve after You have guided us, and bestow on us a mercy from Yourself" (3:8); for they asked for nothing but guidance.

His saying: وَهَيِّئْ لَنا مِنْ أَمْرِنا رَشَداً — "and help us on to rectitude in our affair" (18:10). The intended meaning of "their affair" is the situation specific to them in which they found themselves, having fled from a people who pursue believers, shed their blood, and compel them to worship other than Allah. They took refuge in a cave, not knowing what would happen to them, nor being guided to which path of salvation they should take. From here, it appears that the intended meaning of right conduct (الرشد) is to be guided to that which contains their salvation.

Thus, the sentence—meaning His saying: وَهَيِّئْ لَنا مِنْ أَمْرِنا رَشَداً—is, according to the first of the two previous possibilities regarding the meaning of mercy, an explanatory apposition (عطف تفسير) to His saying: آتِنا مِنْ لَدُنْكَ رَحْمَةً; and according to the second, it is one request after another.

His saying, the Exalted: فَضَرَبْنا عَلَى آذانِهِمْ فِي الْكَهْفِ سِنِينَ عَدَداً — "So We sealed their hearing in the Cave for a number of years" (18:11). It is said in al-Kashshaf: "That is, We struck a veil over them against hearing; meaning We caused them to sleep a heavy sleep in which sounds do not awaken them, just as you see one heavy in sleep being shouted at yet neither hearing nor waking. Thus, the object, which is the veil (الحجاب), was omitted, as it is said: 'He built over his wife,' meaning he built the dome over her."

It is said in al-Majma‘: "The meaning of 'We struck over their ears' is that We cast sleep upon them. This is of the utmost eloquence in speech. It is said: 'Allah struck him with hemiplegia (الفالج)' when Allah afflicts him with it. Qutrub said: 'It is like the saying of the Arabs: The prince struck over the hand of so-and-so when he prevented him from acting.' Al-Aswad ibn Ya‘fur, who was blind, said:

And among the events—may you have no father—is that I Have had the earth struck over me with barriers."

He said: "This is from the eloquent idioms of the Quran that cannot be translated with a meaning that matches the literal wording." What he mentioned of the meaning is more eloquent than what al-Zamakhshari mentioned.

There is a third meaning here, even if they did not mention it: that it is an allusion to what women often do when putting a child to sleep, by striking over the ear with gentle taps of the palms or fingertips so that the child's senses gather upon it and sleep takes hold. Thus, the sentence is a metaphor (كناية) for causing them to sleep for a numbered years with compassion and tenderness, as a nursing mother does with her infant.

His saying: سِنِينَ عَدَداً — "for a number of years" is an adverbial qualification for the striking. Al-adad (العدد) is a verbal noun like al-add (العد) meaning "that which is counted"; so the meaning is "numbered years." It has also been said that a possessive noun is omitted, the estimation being "possessing a number."

It was said in al-Kashshaf that describing the years as "numbered" (العدد) could possibly intend to signify a large amount or a small amount, because the large is small in His sight, as in His saying: "They had not tarried but an hour of a day." Al-Zajjaj said that if a thing is few, the extent of its number is understood and thus does not need to be counted, but if it is many, it needs to be counted.

Perhaps the concern in the description of "number" is that when a thing reaches a great quantity, its counting becomes difficult and it is usually not counted; thus, the description of "number" is a sign of it being few enough to accept counting easily. The Exalted said: وَشَرَوْهُ بِثَمَنٍ بَخْسٍ دَراهِمَ مَعْدُودَةٍ — "And they sold him for a cheap price, a few dirhams" (12:20), meaning few.

The purpose of the description of "number" being to signify fewness is what suits the context as previously mentioned, for the discourse is narrated to deny that their story was a unique wonder, and what suits this is the lessening of the years of their stay, not their increase. The meaning of the verse is clear, and it indicates that they were sleeping in the cave throughout the duration, not dead.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ بَعَثْناهُمْ لِنَعْلَمَ أَيُّ الْحِزْبَيْنِ أَحْصى لِما لَبِثُوا أَمَداً — "Then We raised them up that We may know which of the two groups better reckoned the period they had tarried" (18:12). The intended meaning of raising (البعث) is awakening rather than reviving, by the evidence of the preceding verse. Al-Raghib said: "The group (الحزب) is a company in which there is toughness."

He also said: "Term (الأمد) and eternity (الأبد) are close in meaning, but eternity is an expression for a duration of time that has no defined limit and is not restricted; it is not said: 'the eternity of such-and-such.' The term (الأمد) is a duration that has an unknown limit when used absolutely, and it may be delimited, such as saying: 'the term of such-and-such,' just as one says: 'the time of such-and-such.' The difference between al-amad and al-zaman (time) is that al-amad is said in consideration of the end (الغاية), while al-zaman is general to the beginning and the end. For this reason, some said: al-mada (the extent) and al-amad are close in meaning."

The intended meaning of knowledge (العلم) is active knowledge (العلم الفعلي), which is the manifestation of a thing and its presence with its specific existence before Allah. The occurrence of knowledge in this sense is frequent in the Quran, such as His saying: لِيَعْلَمَ اللهُ مَنْ يَنْصُرُهُ وَرُسُلَهُ بِالْغَيْبِ — "so that Allah may know who helps Him and His apostles in the Unseen" (57:25), and His saying: لِيَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا رِسالاتِ رَبِّهِمْ — "so that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord" (72:28). To this returns the saying of some in its interpretation: that the meaning is "so that what is known to Us may appear according to what We have known."

His saying: لِنَعْلَمَ أَيُّ الْحِزْبَيْنِ أَحْصى — "that We may know which of the two groups better reckoned" etc., is a justification for the raising, and the lam is for the purpose (الغاية). The intended meaning of the "two groups" is the two parties from the People of the Cave when they questioned one another after being raised, one saying: "How long have you tarried?" and they saying: "We tarried a day or part of a day," and others saying: "Your Lord knows best how long you have tarried," as indicated by His saying in the following verses: وَكَذلِكَ بَعَثْناهُمْ لِيَتَساءَلُوا بَيْنَهُمْ — "So it was that We raised them up that they might question one another" (18:19).

As for the saying of the one who says that the "two groups" refers to the two parties from their people, the believers and the disbelievers—as if they differed regarding the term of their stay in the cave between the one correct in his reckoning and the one mistaken, so Allah raised them to clarify and manifest that—the meaning being: "We awakened them so that it might appear which of the two differing parties of believers and disbelievers regarding the term of their stay was correct in its saying"—this is far-fetched.

His saying: أَحْصى لِما لَبِثُوا أَمَداً — "better reckoned the period they had tarried." Ahsa (أحصى) is a past tense verb from al-ihsa (reckoning), and amadan (أمداً) is its object. It is apparent that لِما لَبِثُوا is a qualification for His saying أَمَداً, and the ma is infinitive (مصدرية), meaning: "which of the two groups counted the term of their stay." It has been said that ahsa is a superlative noun (اسم تفضيل) from al-ihsa with the omission of extra letters, like their saying: "He is more counting (أحصى) of wealth" and "more bankrupt (أفلس) than Ibn al-Mudhalliq," and amadan is an accusative governed by a verb indicated by ahsa; this is not devoid of affectation. Other things have also been said.

The meaning of the three verses—meaning His saying: إِذْ أَوَى الْفِتْيَةُ through His saying: أَمَداً—is: When the youths returned to the cave, they then asked their Lord, saying: "Our Lord, bestow upon us from Yourself that by which we may be saved from what threatens us with the choice between worshipping other than You or being killed, and prepare for us from our affair a guidance by which we may be guided to salvation." So We caused them to sleep in the cave for numbered years, then We awakened them so that it might be clarified which of the two groups counted the term of their stay.

The three verses—as you see—mention the summary of their story, indicating thereby the aspect of their being among the signs of Allah and the strangeness of their affair. The first verse indicates their entry into the cave and their request for salvation; the second to their sleep therein for a number of years; and the third to their awakening, their being alerted, and their difference in estimating the time of their stay.

For the summary of the story has three pillars, each of the three verses containing one of them. In this manner proceed the following verses containing the detail of the story, except that they add to that some of what occurred after their matter became known and their condition was clarified to the people, which is what His saying indicates: وَكَذلِكَ أَعْثَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ — "So it was that We let [the people] come upon them" (18:21) until the end of the verses of the story.

His saying, the Exalted: نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ نَبَأَهُمْ بِالْحَقِ — "We relate to you their supplement with the truth" (18:13) until the end of the verse. This is the commencement of mentioning the important particulars of their story in detail. His saying: إِنَّهُمْ فِتْيَةٌ آمَنُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ — "They were indeed youths who had faith in their Lord" (18:13), meaning they believed with a faith pleasing to their Lord; had it not been so, He would certainly not have attributed it to them.

His saying: وَزِدْناهُمْ هُدىً — "and We enhanced them in guidance" (18:13). Guidance (الهدى) after the foundation of faith is inseparable from the elevation of the degree of faith, in which is the guidance of man to everything that leads to the pleasure of Allah. The Exalted said: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَآمِنُوا بِرَسُولِهِ يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ نُوراً تَمْشُونَ بِهِ — "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and have faith in His Apostle. He will grant you a double share of His mercy and give you a light to walk by" (57:28).

His saying, the Exalted: وَرَبَطْنا عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ إِذْ قامُوا فَقالُوا — "We fortified their hearts when they stood up and said" (18:14) until the end of the three verses. Fortifying (الربط) is to bind, and "binding over the hearts" is a metaphor for removing anxiety and agitation from them. Outrage (الشطط) is to exceed the limit and transgress against the truth. Authority (السلطان) is proof and demonstration.

The three verses recount the first part of their dialogue when they rose up to oppose idolatry and contend with them: إِذْ قامُوا فَقالُوا رَبُّنا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ لَنْ نَدْعُوَا مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلهاً لَقَدْ قُلْنا إِذاً شَطَطاً هؤُلاءِ قَوْمُنَا اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً لَوْ لا يَأْتُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِسُلْطانٍ بَيِّنٍ فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً — "when they stood up and said, ‘Our Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth. We will never invoke any god besides Him, for then we would certainly have said an atrocious lie. These people of ours have taken gods besides Him. Why do they not bring any clear authority for them? Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah?’" (18:14-15).

They produced speech full of wisdom and understanding, by which they aimed to nullify the lordship of the lords of idols—among the angels, jinn, and righteous humans—whose divinity and lordship idolatrous philosophy sought to establish, rather than the idols themselves, which are but statues and images of those lords whom their commoners call gods and lords. Evidence for this is His saying: عَلَيْهِمْ — "for them," where He returned to them the pronoun عَلَيْهِمْ which is specific to rational beings.

They began by establishing His monotheism (التوحيد) by their saying: رَبُّنا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Our Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth," attributing the lordship of everything to One who has no partner. Idolatry establishes for every kind of creation a god and a lord, such as the lord of the heaven, the lord of the earth, and the lord of man.

Then they emphasized that by their saying: لَنْ نَدْعُوَا مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلهاً — "We will never invoke any god besides Him," the benefit of which is the negation of the gods whom idolatry establishes above the lords of the species, such as the Universal Intellects worshipped by the Sabians, and Brahma, Shiva, and Vishnu worshipped by the Brahmins and Buddhists. They emphasized it a second time by their saying: لَقَدْ قُلْنا إِذاً شَطَطاً — "for then we would certainly have said an atrocious lie," demonstrating that invoking other than Him is a transgression of the limit by exaggeration regarding the right of the creature, raising it to the degree of the Creator.

Then they turned upon the people in their worship of other than Allah, the Glorified, by their taking of gods, saying: هؤُلاءِ قَوْمُنَا اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً لَوْ لا يَأْتُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِسُلْطانٍ بَيِّنٍ — "These people of ours have taken gods besides Him. Why do they not bring any clear authority for them?" Thus, they rejected their claim by stating that they have no proof for what they claim that indicates it with clear indication.

As for what they argued—their saying that Allah, the Glorified, is too majestic for the perception of His creation to encompass Him, so it is not possible to turn to Him in worship nor to draw near to Him in servitude, thus nothing remains for us but to worship some of the noble beings among His near servants so that they may bring us closer to Him—this is rejected. As for the lack of encompassing perception of Him, the Exalted, it is common between us, the company of humans, and those near servants whom they worship. All of us and all of them know Him by His names, attributes, and signs, each according to their capacity; thus, one may turn to Him in worship according to the extent of one's knowledge.

Furthermore, all the attributes necessitating the entitlement to worship—creation, provision, sovereignty, and governance—belong to Him alone, and no one else possesses any of that; thus, He is to be worshipped, and no one else is.

Then they followed their saying: لَوْ لا يَأْتُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِسُلْطانٍ بَيِّنٍ with their saying: فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً — "Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who fabricates a lie against Allah?" This is part of the complete proof rejecting their claim, and its meaning is that they are obliged to establish a definitive proof for their claim; if they do not establish it, their claim is of the category of speaking without knowledge regarding Allah, which is the fabrication of a lie against Him, the Exalted. Fabrication is wrongdoing (الظلم), and wrongdoing against Allah is the greatest wrongdoing. This shows that they were knowledgeable of Allah, possessing insight in their religion, and they confirmed His saying: وَزِدْناهُمْ هُدىً. In the discourse, despite its brevity, there are restrictions that reveal the detail of their uprising at its start. His saying: وَرَبَطْنا عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ indicates that their saying: رَبُّنا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ etc., was not in secret whispers or in isolation from the idolaters, but was by public declaration and loud proclamation in a situation where hearts melt, souls are terrified, and skins crawl—in the midst of a hostile assembly that sheds blood, tortures, and persecutes.

His saying: لَنْ نَدْعُوَا مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلهاً after His saying رَبُّنا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ—which is a denial and rejection—contains an indication and allusion that there was a compulsory obligation to worship idols and invoke other than Allah.

His saying: إِذْ قامُوا فَقالُوا etc., indicates that at the start of their speech, they were in a council from which the command to worship idols and the compulsion toward it was issued, along with the prohibition of worshipping Allah and the policy characterized by killing and torture—such as the council of the king or his assembly or a similar public assembly. So they stood up and declared their opposition, emerged, and withdrew from the people while in great danger threatening them and attacking them from every side, as indicated by their saying: وَإِذِ اعْتَزَلْتُمُوهُمْ وَما يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا اللهَ فَأْوُوا إِلَى الْكَهْفِ — "When you have withdrawn from them and what they worship except Allah, then take refuge in the Cave" (18:16).

This supports what has been transmitted in the narrations—and the report will follow—that six of them were among the king's inner circle whom he consulted in his affairs; so they stood up from an assembly and declared monotheism (التوحيد) and the negation of any partner for Him, the Exalted.

This does not contradict what will follow in the narrations that they used to hide their faith and act with precautionary dissimulation (التقية), for it is possible that they were proceeding upon it and then surprised the people by declaring faith, then withdrew from them without delay; for they could not have sustained the manifestation of faith, otherwise they would have been killed without doubt.

It might be possible that the intended meaning of their "standing up" was their standing up for Allah in support of the truth, and their saying: رَبُّنا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ etc., was a saying among themselves, and their saying: وَإِذِ اعْتَزَلْتُمُوهُمْ etc., was a saying of theirs after they emerged from the city. Or it could be that the intended meaning was their standing up for Allah, and all that was recounted of their sayings was said among themselves after they emerged from the city and moved away from the people. According to both views, the meaning of "fortifying their hearts" would be that they did not fear the consequence of emerging and fleeing from the city and emigrating from the people. However, the first view is more apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذِ اعْتَزَلْتُمُوهُمْ وَما يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا اللهَ فَأْوُوا إِلَى الْكَهْفِ — "When you have withdrawn from them and what they worship except Allah, then take refuge in the Cave" (18:16) until the end of the verse. Withdrawal (الاعتزال والتعزل) is to move away from a matter. Unfolding (النشر) is to spread out. Gentleness (المرفق), with a kasra on the mim and fatha on the fa or vice versa or with fatha on both, is to treat with kindness.

This is the second part of their dialogue that took place between them after their emergence from among the people, their withdrawal from them and what they worshipped besides Allah, and their moving away from everyone. One of them suggests to them that they enter the cave and hide therein from the enemies of the religion.

They perceived through divine guidance that if they did so, Allah would treat them with His kindness and mercy in a way that would contain their salvation from the people's domination and wrongdoing. The proof for this is their saying with certainty: فَأْوُوا إِلَى الْكَهْفِ يَنْشُرْ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ — "then take refuge in the Cave; your Lord will unfold His mercy for you" etc., and they did not say: "perhaps He will unfold" or "it may be."

These two things which they perceived—the unfolding of mercy and the preparation of gentleness—are the two things they asked for after entering the cave when they said—as Allah recounted—رَبَّنا آتِنا مِنْ لَدُنْكَ رَحْمَةً وَهَيِّئْ لَنا مِنْ أَمْرِنا رَشَداً.

The exception in His saying: وَما يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا اللهَ — "and what they worship except Allah" is a disconnected exception (استثناء منقطع), for the idolaters did not worship Allah along with their other gods such that the exception would serve to exclude some of what first entered into the excepted from; thus it would be connected. So the saying of some that they used to worship Allah and worship idols like other polytheists, as well as the saying of others that it is possible there were among them those who worshipped Allah along with the worship of idols—making the exception connected—is out of place. For the worship of Allah, the Glorified, along with the worship of idols was not known from the idolaters, and their philosophy does not permit that; we have pointed to their argument in that regard just now.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَرَى الشَّمْسَ إِذا طَلَعَتْ تَزاوَرُ عَنْ كَهْفِهِمْ ذاتَ الْيَمِينِ وَإِذا غَرَبَتْ تَقْرِضُهُمْ ذاتَ الشِّمالِ — "You may see the sun, when it rises, slanting toward the right of their cave, and, when it sets, passing them on the left" (18:17) until the end of the two verses. Slanting (التزاور) is to incline, derived from al-zawr meaning inclination. Passing (القرض) is to cut. The hollow (الفجوة) is a spacious part of the ground or the courtyard of a house. What is intended by "the right" and "the left" is the direction adjacent to the right or the left, or the direction bearing the name of right or left, which are the two directions of right and left.

These two verses represent the cave, their resting place within it, their view, and the state that fluctuated over them during the days of their stay therein while they were asleep. The address is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وسلم) in his capacity as a listener, not specifically as himself; this is common in speech, and the address in this manner encompasses every listener without being restricted to a specific addressee.

So His saying: وَتَرَى الشَّمْسَ إِذا طَلَعَتْ تَزاوَرُ عَنْ كَهْفِهِمْ ذاتَ الْيَمِينِ وَإِذا غَرَبَتْ تَقْرِضُهُمْ ذاتَ الشِّمالِ وَهُمْ فِي فَجْوَةٍ مِنْهُ describes the location of the cave and their location within it while they were sleeping. As for their being made to sleep therein after taking refuge and the duration of their stay, it was deemed sufficient to refer to what was indicated in the previous verses regarding their sleep and stay, and what will follow in His saying: وَلَبِثُوا فِي كَهْفِهِمْ etc., preferring brevity.

The meaning is: You see—and every observer whose looking upon them is assumed while they are in the cave sees—the sun when it rises slanting and inclining away from their cave toward the right side, so its light falls upon it; and when it sets, it cuts across the left side, so its rays fall upon it, while they are in a spacious part of the cave that the sun does not reach.

The Glorified has indicated thereby that the cave was neither eastern nor western, such that the sun's rays would fall upon it only at one of the two times; rather, it was polar, its door facing the pole, so the sun's rays would fall on one of its sides from within at sunrise and sunset, but would not fall upon them because they were in a spacious part of it. Thus Allah protected them thereby from the heat of the sun harming them, or changing their colors, or wearing out their clothes; rather, they were comfortable in their sleep, benefiting from the spirit of the air that changed over them at sunrise and sunset while they were in a hollow of it. Perhaps the indefiniteness of "hollow" (فجوة) is to indicate a omitted description, the estimation being: "and they were in a hollow of it where its rays did not reach them."

The commentators have mentioned that the cave's door faced the North Pole, aligned with the Great Bear (بنات النعش). The right side of it is what is adjacent to the west, and the sun's rays fall upon it at its rising; and the left side of it is what is adjacent to the east, and the sun reaches it at its setting. This is based on taking the directions of right and left for the cave in consideration of the one entering it. It is as if they relied on what is famous—that this cave is located in the city of Ephesus in the lands of the Eastern Romans—for the cave there is polar, its door facing the North Pole, slanting slightly toward the east, according to what is said.

What is customary in considering the right and left for things like a cave, a house, a tent, and everything that has a door, is that they are taken in consideration of the one exiting from it, not the one entering it. For when man first felt the need to consider directions, he took them for himself; so he named what was adjacent to his head and feet "high" and "low," "above" and "below"; he named what was adjacent to his face "front" and what opposed it "back"; and he named the strong side of him—which is the one containing his strong hand—"right," and that which opposed it "left" or "north." Then, if the need arose to consider directions in a thing, man would assume himself in its place; so whatever of the sides of that thing corresponded to the face—which is the side with which the thing faces others—would be determined as its front, and what was opposite to it as its back; and whatever corresponded to the right of man among its sides would be its right, and similarly for the left of man, its left.

Since the "face" in things like a house, a dwelling, a tent, and everything that has a door is the side containing the door, the determination of its right and left is in consideration of the one exiting from the door, not the one entering it. Based on this, the cave described by the verse as it did would be southern, its door facing the South Pole, not as they mentioned. The discussion has a completion that will reach you, Allah willing.

In any case, their situation was from the care of Allah and His kindness toward them, to preserve them thereby until the decree reached its term. To this is the indication in His saying immediately following: ذلِكَ مِنْ آياتِ اللهِ مَنْ يَهْدِ اللهُ فَهُوَ الْمُهْتَدِ وَمَنْ يُضْلِلْ فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لَهُ وَلِيًّا مُرْشِداً — "That is one of Allah’s signs. Whomever Allah guides is rightly guided, and whomever He leads astray, you will never find for him any guardian or guide" (18:17).

And His saying: وَتَحْسَبُهُمْ أَيْقاظاً وَهُمْ رُقُودٌ — "You would have supposed them to be awake, though they were asleep" (18:18). Awake (الأيقاظ) is the plural of yaqiz and yaqzan, and asleep (الرقود) is the plural of raqid, which is the sleeper. In the speech is an allusion that they had their eyes open during their sleep like those awake.

And His saying: وَنُقَلِّبُهُمْ ذاتَ الْيَمِينِ وَذاتَ الشِّمالِ — "We turn them to the right and to the left" (18:18), meaning We turn them toward the right side and toward the left side. The intended meaning is: We turn them sometimes from the right to the left and sometimes from the left to the right, so that the earth does not consume them, their clothes do not wear out, and their bodily powers do not fail through stagnation and inactivity during the long stay.

And His saying: وَكَلْبُهُمْ باسِطٌ ذِراعَيْهِ بِالْوَصِيدِ — "and their dog stretching out his forelegs at the threshold" (18:18). The threshold (الوصيد) is the courtyard of the house, and it is said to be the doorstep of the dwelling. The meaning is: they were in the state described, while their dog was lying with his forelegs stretched out at the entrance of the cave. In this is an informing that they had a dog that accompanied them and stayed with them throughout their stay in the cave.

And His saying: لَوِ اطَّلَعْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَوَلَّيْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِراراً وَلَمُلِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ رُعْباً — "Had you come upon them, you would have surely turned to flee from them, and you would have surely been filled with terror of them" (18:18). This is an explanation that they and their condition in this state presented a terrifying and awesome sight; had a human looked upon them, he would have fled from them in fear of their danger, distancing himself from the expected harm from their direction, and his heart would have been filled with dread and panic—terror (رعباً)—which would have spread to all his limbs, filling them all with terror. The discussion regarding the address in His saying لَوَلَّيْتَ and His saying وَلَمُلِئْتَ is like the discussion regarding the address in His saying وَتَرَى الشَّمْسَ.

It has become clear from the preceding clarification: first, the reason for His saying وَلَمُلِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ رُعْباً and He did not say: "and your heart would have been filled with terror."

Second: the reason for the order of the two sentences: لَوَلَّيْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِراراً وَلَمُلِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ رُعْباً. This is because fleeing—which is distancing oneself from the disliked—is an effect of expecting the arrival of the disliked as a precaution against it, and it is not an effect of terror, which is fear and the heart's being affected. The anticipated disliked must be guarded against whether there is terror or not.

Thus, the placing of fleeing before terror is not of the category of placing the effect before its cause, but rather of placing the ruling of fear before terror, and they are two distinct states of the heart. Had it been "fear" instead of "terror," the right of the speech would have been to place the second sentence first and the first last. However, based on what we have mentioned, placing the ruling of fear before the occurrence of terror—both being effects of looking upon their awesome and terrifying sight—is better and more eloquent, because fleeing is a more manifest indication of that than being filled with terror.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ بَعَثْناهُمْ لِيَتَساءَلُوا بَيْنَهُمْ — "So it was that We raised them up that they might question one another" (18:19) until the end of the two verses. Questioning (التساؤل) is the questioning of some of the people by others. Silver coin (الورق), with a fatha then a kasra, refers to dirhams; it is said to be silver, whether minted or otherwise. His saying: "if they come upon you" (إن يظهروا عليكم), meaning if they discover you or if they overcome you.

The indication in His saying: وَكَذلِكَ بَعَثْناهُمْ is to their being made to sleep in the form represented by the previous verses; meaning: just as We made them sleep in the cave for a long age in this wondrous and astonishing state which was one of Our signs, so too We raised them and awakened them that they might question one another.

This comparison and the making of questioning the purpose (غاية) for the raising—along with what preceded of their supplication upon entering the cave and their being made to sleep following that—indicates that they were only raised from their sleep to question one another so that the reality of the matter would appear to them. They were only made to sleep and stayed in their sleep for an age so that they might be raised. Allah made them sleep following their supplication and request for mercy from Allah and a prepared guidance for their affair. They had been disturbed by the dominance of disbelief over their society, the appearance of falsehood, and the surrounding of coercion and compulsion; despair and hopelessness had attacked them regarding the appearance of the word of truth and the freedom of the people of religion in their religion. Thus, they deemed long the stay of falsehood on the earth and its appearance over the truth, like the one who passed by a town while it was fallen on its trellises and said: "How will Allah revive this after its death?" so Allah caused him to die for a hundred years then raised him.

In short, when this notion overcame them and they despaired of the removal of falsehood's dominance, Allah made them sleep for a number of years, then raised them to question one another, so they would answer with "a day or part of a day." Then the transformation of states and the passage of hundreds of years would be disclosed to them, which from another perspective is like a day or part of a day. Thus they would know that the length of time and its shortness is not that which kills a truth or revives a falsehood; rather, it is only Allah, the Glorified, who made what is on the earth an adornment for it, attracted man to it, and ran the ages and days therein to test them as to which of them is best in deed. The world has nothing but to deceive with its adornment those who seek it among those who cling to the earth and follow their desires.

This is a reality that remains apparent to man whenever he reflects on his past days and the events that occurred therein, their sweetness and bitterness; he finds them like a phantom in a sleep or a slumber in the like of a day. However, the intoxication of desire and the preoccupation with the play of the world do not let him wake up to the truth and follow it. But for Allah, the Glorified, there is a day for man when no preoccupation with the world's adornment and finery will distract him from witnessing this reality, and that is the day of death, as narrated from Ali (peace be upon him): "People are asleep; when they die, they wake up." And another day is the day when the carpet of the world and its adornment is folded, and the human world is decreed to perish and become extinct.

It has become clear from what preceded that His saying, the Exalted: لِيَتَساءَلُوا بَيْنَهُمْ is the purpose for their raising, and the lam is for the justification of the purpose. It corresponds to the purpose in His saying: ثُمَّ بَعَثْناهُمْ لِنَعْلَمَ أَيُّ الْحِزْبَيْنِ أَحْصى لِما لَبِثُوا أَمَداً.

Some mentioned that it is part of the purpose, placed in its stead because it leads to the other effects of the raising; as if it were said: "that they might question one another and that would lead to the appearance of their matter, the disclosure of the sign, and the manifestation of power." This, along with the lack of evidence for it from the aspect of the wording, is an obvious affectation.

Others mentioned that the lam in His saying لِيَتَساءَلُوا is for the consequence (العاقبة) rather than the purpose, deeming it unlikely that questioning—which is a minor matter—would be the purpose for the raising, which is a great sign. In this is that making the lam for the consequence does not help in repelling what he deemed unlikely; for just as it is not appropriate to make a minor matter the purpose sought for a grave matter and a great sign, similarly it is not good to mention a small thing as the consequence of a significant matter and a wondrous, astonishing sign. Furthermore, you have just recognized the correctness of questioning being the final cause (علة غائية) for the raising.

His saying: قالَ قائِلٌ مِنْهُمْ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ — "One of them said, ‘How long have you tarried?’" (18:19) is proof that the one asking about their stay was one of them, addressing the rest and asking them about the duration of their stay sleeping in the cave. It is as if the asker sensed a length in their stay from what he found of the heaviness of deep sleep after awakening, so he said: "How long have you tarried?"

His saying: قالُوا لَبِثْنا يَوْماً أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ — "They said, ‘We have tarried a day, or part of a day’" (18:19). They hesitated in their answer between a day and part of a day. It is as if they based the answer on what they observed of the change in the position of the sun; as if they began their sleep in the early part of the day and woke up in its middle or end, then they doubted whether a night had passed over them—in which case their stay would be a day—or whether it had not passed—in which case it would be part of a day. So they answered with the hesitation between a day and part of a day, and it is in any case a single answer.

The saying of some that the hesitation on this basis must be between part of a day and a day and a part, not between a day and part of a day—so the view is that أَوْ (or) is for detail (التفصيل) not for hesitation, and the meaning is: some of them said: "We tarried a day," and others said: "We tarried part of a day"—is not to be considered. Firstly, because this meaning is not received from the context of the like of His saying لَبِثْنا يَوْماً أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ at all, and a single person has answered with its like, as the Exalted said: قالَ كَمْ لَبِثْتَ قالَ : لَبِثْتُ يَوْماً أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ — "He said, ‘How long have you tarried?’ He said, ‘I have tarried a day, or part of a day’" (2:259).

Secondly, because their saying "We tarried a day" was only taken from what they inferred from the matters witnessed by them—due to their high station being above arbitrary judgment, whim, and conjecture. External matters by which man infers—especially one who slept then woke—such as sun, shadow, light, darkness, and the like, do not specify the amount of a complete day without increase or decrease, whether in hesitation or detail. So the intended meaning of "day" in any case is what exceeds a night with its day by some increase, which is a common usage.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا رَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِما لَبِثُمْ — "They said, ‘Your Lord knows best how long you have tarried’" (18:19); meaning another group among them said, in response to those who said "We tarried a day or part of a day": "Your Lord knows best how long you have tarried." Had it not been a response, they would have said: "Our Lord knows best how long we have tarried."

By this, it appears that referring knowledge to Allah in their saying "Your Lord knows best" is not merely for the sake of observing good etiquette (الأدب), as has been said, but rather to clarify one of the realities of the knowledge of monotheism (معارف التوحيد). This is that knowledge of the reality of the meaning of the word belongs only to Allah, the Glorified; for man is veiled from what is beyond himself, possessing nothing by Allah's permission except himself, and encompassing nothing except it. He only obtains knowledge of what is outside himself from what external signs indicate and to the extent that is disclosed by them. As for the encompassing of the essence of things and the events themselves—which is knowledge in reality—it belongs only to Allah, the Glorified, who encompasses everything and is a witness over everything. The verses indicating this reality are innumerable.

Thus, it is not for the monotheist (الموحد) who knows the station of his Lord except to surrender the matter to Him and attribute knowledge to Him, and not to attribute to himself anything of perfection such as knowledge and power except what he is compelled to. So he begins with his Lord, attributing to Him the reality of perfection, then to himself what Allah has made him possess and permitted for him, as He said: عَلَّمَ الْإِنْسانَ ما لَمْ يَعْلَمْ — "taught man what he did not know" (96:5), and He said: قالُوا سُبْحانَكَ لا عِلْمَ لَنا إِلَّا ما عَلَّمْتَنا — "They said, ‘Immaculate are You! We have no knowledge except what You have taught us’" (2:32), among many other verses.

It appears from this that those among them who said "Your Lord knows best how long you have tarried" were of a higher standing in the station of gnosis (المعرفة) than those who said "We tarried a day or part of a day." They did not intend by this saying merely to show etiquette; otherwise, they would have said: "Our Lord knows best how long we have tarried."
