Nor were they one of the two groups whom the Glorified referred to in His previous saying: ثُمَّ بَعَثْناهُمْ لِنَعْلَمَ أَيُّ الْحِزْبَيْنِ أَحْصى لِما لَبِثُوا أَمَداً; for showing etiquette is not called a saying or a reckoning, nor is the one who brings it considered a possessor of a saying or a reckoning.

It is apparent that those among them who said "Your Lord knows best how long you have tarried" were other than those who said "We tarried a day or part of a day," for the context is that of dialogue and response as has been said, and its necessity is that the speakers second were other than the speakers first. Had they been the same individuals, the right of the speech would have been to say: "Then they said: Our Lord knows best how long we tarried" instead of His saying: "Your Lord knows best" etc.

From here, it is inferred that the people were seven or more, since in the recounting of their dialogue "he said" (قالَ) occurred once and "they said" (قالُوا) occurred twice, and the minimum for a plural is three; thus their number was not less than seven.

And His saying, the Exalted: فَابْعَثُوا أَحَدَكُمْ بِوَرِقِكُمْ هذِهِ إِلَى الْمَدِينَةِ فَلْيَنْظُرْ أَيُّها أَزْكى طَعاماً فَلْيَأْتِكُمْ بِرِزْقٍ مِنْهُ — "Now send one of you with this silver coin of yours to the city, and let him see which of them has the purest food, and let him bring you a provision from it" (18:19) is from the completion of the dialogue. In it is a command or a proposal to them to send a messenger from among them to the city to buy them food to nourish themselves. The pronoun in أَيُّها (which of them) refers to the city, and the intended meaning is its people among the tradesmen, by way of synecdoche (استخداما).

The "purity" (زكاء) of food is its being good (طيباً); it is said to be its being lawful (حلالاً), and it is said to be its being clean (طاهراً). Its occurrence in the superlative form أَزْكى طَعاماً (purest food) is not devoid of an indication of the first meaning.

The pronoun in مِنْهُ (from it) refers to the food understood from the speech. It is said to refer to the "purest food," and the "from" (من) is for beginning or partitive; meaning: "let him bring you from that purest food a provision for you to be sustained by." It is said the pronoun refers to the silver coin and "from" is for beginning, which is far-fetched as it necessitates the estimation of another pronoun referring to the previous sentence and its being a masculine pronoun while the silver coin was referred to with a feminine word before.

And His saying, the Exalted: وَلْيَتَلَطَّفْ وَلا يُشْعِرَنَّ بِكُمْ أَحَداً — "and let him be courteous, and not let anyone know about you" (18:19). Courtesy (التلطف) is to employ kindness and gentleness and to manifest it. So His saying: وَلا يُشْعِرَنَّ بِكُمْ أَحَداً is an explanatory apposition to it. The intended meaning, according to what the context yields, is: let him take pains to be gentle with the people of the city in his going, coming, and dealing with them, so that no dispute or contention occurs that would lead to their knowing of your condition and their being informed of you. It is said the meaning is to take pains to be gentle in the transaction, but the absolute nature of the speech rejects this.

And His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُمْ إِنْ يَظْهَرُوا عَلَيْكُمْ يَرْجُمُوكُمْ أَوْ يُعِيدُوكُمْ فِي مِلَّتِهِمْ وَلَنْ تُفْلِحُوا إِذاً أَبَداً — "For if they come upon you, they will stone you, or force you back into their creed, and then you will never be felicitous" (18:20) is a justification for the command to be courteous and an explanation of its benefit.

"Appearing over a thing" (ظهر على الشيء) means to discover it and know of it, and it also means to overcome it. The verse has been interpreted with both meanings. The word, according to what al-Raghib mentioned, is derived from al-zahr (the back) meaning the limb opposite the belly; this was the origin, then it was borrowed for the earth, so it was said: "the back of the earth" opposite its belly. Then "appearance" (الظهور) was taken from it meaning disclosure opposite concealment, due to the correlation between being on the face of the earth and being seen and discovered; similarly between it and overcoming, and similarly between it and dominance usually. So it is said: "he appeared over him," meaning he discovered him and knew of his place, or he overcame him, or he dominated him. Then they expanded in the derivation, saying: azhara, zahara, tazahara, istazhara, and so on.

The apparent context is that يَظْهَرُوا عَلَيْكُمْ means "they discover you" and "they know of your place," for it is the most comprehensive of meanings. For the people were possessors of power and strength, and they had fled and hidden from them; so if they discovered them, they would overcome them and dominate them as they wished.

His saying: يَرْجُمُوكُمْ — "they will stone you," meaning they will kill you with stones, which is the worst of killings and implies the meaning of aversion and expulsion. In the choice of stoning over other types of killing is an indication that the people of the city in general were hostile to them for their religion; so if they discovered them, they would hasten to them and participate in killing them, and the killing that is of this nature is usually by stoning.

His saying: أَوْ يُعِيدُوكُمْ فِي مِلَّتِهِمْ — "or force you back into their creed." It is apparent that "returning" (الإعادة) implies the meaning of "entering" (الإدخال), which is why it was made transitive with fi (in) rather than ila (to).

The necessity of their entering into their creed usually—given that they had publicly rejected it, called it an atrocious lie, and a fabrication against Allah—was that the people would not be content with their mere outward recognition of the creed's truth without being confident of their sincerity in recognition and monitoring them in their deeds. Thus they would participate with the people in the worship of idols and the performance of all the religious duties they had, and be deprived of acting upon anything of the divine laws and uttering the word of truth.

All of this is not problematic for one who is compelled to reside in the lands of disbelief and is confined among its people, like the oppressed captive, by the judgment of reason and transmission. The Exalted has said: إِلَّا مَنْ أُكْرِهَ وَقَلْبُهُ مُطْمَئِنٌّ بِالْإِيمانِ — "excepting him who is forced while his heart is at rest in faith" (16:106), and the Exalted said: إِلَّا أَنْ تَتَّقُوا مِنْهُمْ تُقاةً — "unless you be wary of them out of caution" (3:28). So he may believe in his heart and deny with his tongue. As for the one who was in a place of safety from them and was free in his belief and action, then threw himself into the peril of misguidance and caused himself to be confined in a society of disbelief, such that he could not utter the word of truth and was deprived of being characterized by the human religious duties—he has indeed deprived himself of felicity and will never be felicitous. The Exalted said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ قالُوا فِيمَ كُنْتُمْ قالُوا كُنَّا مُسْتَضْعَفِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ قالُوا أَلَمْ تَكُنْ أَرْضُ اللهِ واسِعَةً فَتُهاجِرُوا فِيها فَأُولئِكَ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَساءَتْ مَصِيراً — "Indeed, those whom the angels take away while they are wronging themselves, they [the angels] say, ‘What state were you in?’ They say, ‘We were abased in the land.’ They say, ‘Was not Allah’s earth vast so that you might have migrated in it?’ Those—their abode shall be hell, and it is an evil destination" (4:97).

Through this, the reason for the sequence of His saying: وَلَنْ تُفْلِحُوا إِذاً أَبَداً (and then you will never be felicitous) following His saying: أَوْ يُعِيدُوكُمْ فِي مِلَّتِهِمْ (or force you back into their creed) becomes clear. This also refutes what has been suggested: that manifesting disbelief (الكفر) under duress (الإكراه) while concealing faith (الإيمان) is pardoned in all times; so how could the eternal lack of felicity (الفلاح) be predicated upon returning to their creed, when it is apparent from their situation that it would be under compulsion? The answer is that if they were to expose themselves to them or lead them in any way to their location, and were then forced back into their creed—even if under duress—this would have been an elective cause (تسببا اختياريا) on their part leading to that outcome, and they would not have been excused at all.

Scholars have answered this problem with other views that are not convincing:

Among them: that being coerced into disbelief may become a cause for Satan to gradually lead one to find it acceptable and persist in it. The flaw in this view is that it would necessitate the phrasing: "and it is feared for you that you will never be felicitous," unless a definitive judgment of non-felicity is decreed.

Among them: that it is possible He meant "they return you to their religion" by invitation rather than coercion. You are well aware that the context (سياق) of the story does not support this.

Among them: that it is possible that at that time, dissimulation (التقية) — the practice of concealing one's faith to avoid harm — by manifesting disbelief was not permissible at all. The flaw here is the lack of evidence for such a claim.

The context of what He narrated of their dialogue—namely His saying: لَبِثْتُمْ (you have stayed) until the end of the two verses—is a wondrous context indicating the perfection of their mutual love in Allah, their brotherhood in religion, their adoption of equality among themselves, and their sincerity (نصح) and compassion (إشفاق) for one another. It has already been mentioned that the saying of those who said: رَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِما لَبِثْتُمْ (Your Lord knows best how long you have stayed) is an awakening and an indication of a station of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — that is higher and more sublime in degree than what is indicated by the saying of the others: لَبِثْنا يَوْماً أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ (We have stayed a day or part of a day).

Furthermore, the saying of the speaker: فَابْعَثُوا (So send), where he proposed sending the messenger to all of them and did not act unilaterally by saying, "Let one of you go"; and his saying: أَحَدَكُمْ (one of you) without saying, "Go, O so-and-so," or "Send so-and-so"; and his saying: بِوَرِقِكُمْ هذِهِ (with this silver of yours), attributing the silver (الورق) to everyone—all of this is evidence of brotherhood and equality.

Then his saying: فَلْيَنْظُرْ أَيُّها أَزْكى طَعاماً (let him see which of them has purer food) etc., and his saying: وَلْيَتَلَطَّفْ (and let him be courteous) etc., is sincere advice (نصح). And his saying: إِنَّهُمْ إِنْ يَظْهَرُوا عَلَيْكُمْ (Indeed, if they come to know of you) etc., is advice to them and compassion for their souls insofar as they are believers committed to their religion.

His saying, the Exalted: بِوَرِقِكُمْ هذِهِ (with this silver of yours), despite the possessive attribution and the demonstrative reference specifying the particular silver, suggests a special concern in mentioning it. For the context of requesting to send someone to buy food for them does not naturally necessitate mentioning the silver with which the food is bought or pointing to it specifically. Perhaps it was mentioned in the verse with the specific demonstrative reference because it was the cause for the discovery of their affair and the disclosure of their state. This is because when he brought it out—its engravings being such that he offered it as payment for food—it was from the coinage of an era over which three centuries had passed. There is nothing in the verses of the story that suggests the cause for the discovery of their affair and the disclosure of their state except this word.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ أَعْثَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌّ وَأَنَّ السَّاعَةَ لا رَيْبَ فِيها إِذْ يَتَنازَعُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَمْرَهُمْ (So it was that We let them be found, so that they might know that Allah’s promise is true, and that there is no doubt about the Hour. As they were disputing among themselves about their affair). It is stated in al-Mufradat: "A man stumbles (عثر) when he falls, and it is used metaphorically for one who comes upon a matter without seeking it." He, the Exalted, said: فَإِنْ عُثِرَ عَلى أَنَّهُمَا اسْتَحَقَّا إِثْماً (Then if it is discovered that the two of them have been guilty of a sin). It is said, "I came upon (عثرت) such and such." He said: وَكَذلِكَ أَعْثَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ (So it was that We let them be found), meaning We made them [the people] come upon them without them seeking it. End quote.

The comparison in His saying: وَكَذلِكَ أَعْثَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ (So it was that We let them be found) is like its counterpart in His saying: وَكَذلِكَ بَعَثْناهُمْ (So it was that We resurrected them), meaning: just as We made them sleep for an age and then resurrected them for such and such, so too We let them be found. The object of "We let be found" (أعثرنا) is the people, as indicated by the context and attested to by the end of the verse. His saying: لِيَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ وَعْدَ اللهِ حَقٌّ (so that they might know that Allah’s promise is true)—the plural pronoun refers to the people, and what is meant by "Allah's promise," as given by the context, is the Resurrection (البعث). Thus, His saying: وَأَنَّ السَّاعَةَ لا رَيْبَ فِيها (and that there is no doubt about the Hour) would be an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسيري) for the preceding phrase.

His saying: إِذْ يَتَنازَعُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَمْرَهُمْ (As they were disputing among themselves about their affair) is an adverbial qualifier (ظرف) for His saying: "We let them be found" (أَعْثَرْنا) or for His saying: "so that they might know" (لِيَعْلَمُوا). Dispute (التنازع) means contention (التخاصم). It is said: the origin of al-tanazu' is mutual pulling (التجاذب), and it is used to express contention. In terms of its original meaning, it is transitive by itself, but in the sense of contention, it is transitive with the preposition fi (in), as in His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ (then if you dispute concerning anything). End quote.

What is meant by the people disputing among themselves "their affair" (أمرهم) is their dispute regarding the matter of the Resurrection. It was attributed to them to indicate their concern and preoccupation with its status. This is the state of the verse in terms of its individual words, with some parts testifying to others.

The meaning, based on what has passed, is: Just as We made them sleep and then resurrected them for such and such, We made the people aware of them at a time when they—meaning the people—were disputing among themselves regarding the matter of the Resurrection, so that they might know that Allah's promise of Resurrection is true and that there is no doubt about the Hour.

Or the meaning is: We let them be found so that the people might know, coinciding with a time when they were disputing among themselves regarding the matter of the Resurrection, that Allah's promise of Resurrection is true.

As for the indication of their being raised from sleep that the Resurrection on the Day of Judgment is true, it is from the perspective that the withdrawal of their souls from their bodies for that long age, the suspension of their consciousness, the stagnation of their senses from their functions, the cessation of the effects of physical forces—such as growth (النشو), development (النماء), the growth of hair and nails, the change of form, the appearance of gray hair, and so on—and the preservation of the outward appearance of their bodies and clothes from decay and wear, and then their return to the exact state they were in the day they entered the cave, is analogous to the withdrawal of souls from bodies at death and then their return to how they were. Both are among the miracles (خوارق العادة) — events that break the natural order of things — which cannot be rejected except by mere dismissal due to perceived improbability (الاستبعاد) without evidence.

This event occurred at a time when a dispute appeared between two groups of people: a monotheist (موحد) who holds that souls depart from bodies at death and then return to them at the Resurrection, and a polytheist (مشرك) (1) who holds the soul to be distinct from the body and departing from it at death, but does not believe in the Resurrection, and perhaps believes in transmigration (التناسخ).

The occurrence of such an event in such a state leaves no doubt for those people that it is a divine sign (آية إلهية) intended to remove doubt from their hearts regarding the matter of the Resurrection, by demonstrating one like thing through another like thing, and removing the sense of improbability through actual occurrence.

This intuition of theirs is strengthened and intensified by their death shortly after being raised; for they did not live after it except for a few hours, which did not suffice for more than the people becoming aware of their state, gathering around them, inquiring about their story, and being informed of it.

From here, another aspect of His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ يَتَنازَعُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَمْرَهُمْ (As they were disputing among themselves about their affair) appears, which is that the first two pronouns refer to the people and the third refers to the People of the Cave, and that "when" (إِذْ) is an adverbial qualifier for His saying: "so that they might know" (لِيَعْلَمُوا). This is supported by His saying afterward: رَبُّهُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِهِمْ (Their Lord knows them best), as will come.

The objection to this view—firstly, that it requires the dispute to be after the discovery, which is not the case; and secondly, that the dispute was before the knowledge and was resolved by it, so how could its time be the same time?—is refuted by the fact that the dispute according to this view in the verse is the people's dispute regarding the affair of the People of the Cave, which occurred after the discovery and coincided temporally with the knowledge. That which was before the discovery and before the knowledge was their dispute regarding the matter of the Resurrection, and it is not what is intended by this view.

His saying, the Exalted: فَقالُوا ابْنُوا عَلَيْهِمْ بُنْياناً رَبُّهُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِهِمْ (So they said, ‘Build a structure over them. Their Lord knows them best.’) The speakers were the polytheists among the people, as evidenced by His saying afterward: قالَ الَّذِينَ غَلَبُوا عَلى أَمْرِهِمْ (Those who had the say in their matter said). What is meant by building a structure over them, as has been said, is to strike a barrier over them by which they are placed behind it and hidden from the people, so that no observer among them may come upon them, just as it is said: "He built a wall over him" when he enclosed him and placed him behind it.

This portion of the speech, when joined with what preceded it of His saying: وَكَذلِكَ بَعَثْناهُمْ (So it was that We resurrected them) and وَكَذلِكَ أَعْثَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ (So it was that We let them be found), alludes to the completion of the story. It is as if it were said: When their messenger came to the city—circumstances having changed and conditions having transformed with the passage of three centuries since their entry into the cave, the authority of polytheism having ended, and the reins of society having been handed over to monotheism—and he did not know, it was not long before his affair became apparent and his news spread. So the people gathered around him, then they rushed and crowded at the door of the cave, and they sought from them their story, and the divine proof was established. Then Allah took them to Himself; they did not remain alive after their being raised except for a few hours by which the people's doubt regarding the matter of the Resurrection was removed. At that point, the polytheists said: "Build a structure over them; their Lord knows them best."

In His saying: رَبُّهُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِهِمْ (Their Lord knows them best) is an indication of the occurrence of a disagreement among the people gathered regarding their affair. For it is the speech of one who has despaired of knowing them and uncovering the reality of their affair. It suggests that the people disputed over something related to them; some gained insight into it while others did not settle upon anything and did not accept the view of their opponents, so they said: "Build a structure for them; their Lord knows them best."

Thus, the meaning of the sentence—namely His saying: رَبُّهُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِهِمْ (Their Lord knows them best)—varies according to the two aforementioned views regarding His saying: إِذْ يَتَنازَعُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَمْرَهُمْ (As they were disputing among themselves about their affair). For the sentence, in any case, is a kind of derivative of a dispute among them, as you have just seen. If the dispute indicated by His saying: إِذْ يَتَنازَعُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَمْرَهُمْ is the dispute regarding the matter of the Resurrection in terms of affirmation and denial—the pronoun in "their affair" (أَمْرَهُمْ) referring to the people—then the meaning is that they disputed regarding the matter of the Resurrection, so We let them be found so that they might know that Allah's promise is true and that there is no doubt about the Hour. However, the polytheists did not cease despite the sign that appeared to them, so they said: "Build a structure over the People of the Cave and leave them as they are, so that people are cut off from them; for nothing of their affair has become clear to us, nor have we attained certainty regarding them; their Lord knows them best." And the monotheists said: "Their affair is apparent and their sign is clear, and we shall surely take a mosque over them where Allah is worshipped and their memory remains by its permanence."

If the dispute was the dispute regarding the People of the Cave—the pronoun in "their affair" (أَمْرَهُمْ) referring to them—then the meaning is that We let the people find them after raising them from their sleep so that the people might know that Allah's promise is true and that there is no doubt about the Hour, at the time when Allah took them back after the people found them and the purpose was achieved, while they—meaning the people—were disputing among themselves regarding their affair—meaning the affair of the People of the Cave. It is as if they differed: Are the people asleep or dead? And is it obligatory that they be buried and entombed, or left in their form in the open space of the cave? So the polytheists said: "Build a structure over them and leave them as they are; their Lord knows them best whether they are asleep or dead." The monotheists said: لَنَتَّخِذَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ مَسْجِداً (We will surely set up a place of worship over them).

However, the context supports the first meaning, because its appearance suggests that the saying of the monotheists: لَنَتَّخِذَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ مَسْجِداً (We will surely set up a place of worship over them) is a rebuttal to the saying of the polytheists: ابْنُوا عَلَيْهِمْ بُنْياناً (Build a structure over them) etc. The two sayings from the two groups only conflict according to the first meaning. Likewise, their saying: رَبُّهُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِهِمْ (Their Lord knows them best), especially where they said "their Lord" (رَبُّهُمْ) and did not say "our Lord," is more appropriate to the first meaning.

His saying: قالَ الَّذِينَ غَلَبُوا عَلى أَمْرِهِمْ لَنَتَّخِذَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ مَسْجِداً (Those who had the say in their matter said, ‘We will surely set up a place of worship over them.’) These speakers are the monotheists. Among the evidences for this is the expression of what they set up as a mosque (المسجد) rather than a temple (المعبد). For a mosque in the usage of the Quran is the place designated for the remembrance of Allah and prostration to Him. He, the Exalted, said: وَمَساجِدُ يُذْكَرُ فِيهَا اسْمُ اللهِ (and mosques wherein the name of Allah is much mentioned) [Al-Hajj: 40].

The speech is presented without a conjunction (فصل) because it stands as an answer to a presumed question, as if someone were asking: "So what did those other than the polytheists say?" It was said: "Those who had the say... said." As for what is meant by their "having the say in their matter" (غلبوا على أمرهم): if what is meant by "their matter" is the matter mentioned in His saying: إِذْ يَتَنازَعُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَمْرَهُمْ (As they were disputing among themselves about their affair) and the pronoun refers to the people, then what is meant by "having the say" is the victory of the monotheists through their success by the sign that was established for the truth of the Resurrection. If the pronoun refers to the youths, then the "having the say" is in terms of taking charge of their affair, and the victors are the monotheists. It has been said: the king and his aides; and it has been said: their guardians from among their relatives—which is the weakest of views.

If what is meant by "their matter" is other than the previous matter and the pronoun refers to the people, then the "having the say" is taking the reins of society's affairs through kingship and governance, and the victors are the monotheists or the king and his aides. If the pronoun refers to the relative pronoun (الموصول), then the victors are the rulers, and the meaning of their "having the say in their matters" is that they are dominant over the matters they intended and are capable. This is the case, and the best of the views is the first.

The verse is a battlefield for the opinions of the exegetes. They have a wondrous disagreement regarding its individual words, the plural pronouns within it, and its sentences. The possibilities they have expressed regarding the meanings of its words, the referents of its pronouns, and the states of its sentences, if multiplied by one another, would reach the thousands. We have pointed out those that align with the context, and the student seeking more than that should refer to the lengthy works.

His saying, the Exalted: سَيَقُولُونَ ثَلاثَةٌ رابِعُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ (They will say, ‘[They were] three, their dog the fourth of them’) until His saying: وَثامِنُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ (and their dog the eighth of them). He, the Exalted, mentions the disagreement of the people regarding the number of the People of the Cave and their sayings about it. These are—according to what He, the Exalted, mentioned, and His word is the Truth—three sequential and ascending sayings. One is that they were three, their dog the fourth of them. The second is that they were five, their dog the sixth of them; and He followed this by saying: رَجْماً بِالْغَيْبِ (guessing at the Unseen) — making claims about that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge without evidence.

This description refers to both sayings together. Had it been specific to the second only, the requirement of the speech would have been to present the second saying first and the first later, and to mention with the third that which was not mentioned with it to indicate its non-acceptance.

The third saying is that they were seven, their dog the eighth of them. Allah, the Glorified, mentioned it and did not follow it with anything indicating its falsification. This is not without an indication that it is the true saying. It has already been mentioned in the discussion of their dialogue narrated by His saying, the Exalted: قالَ قائِلٌ مِنْهُمْ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ قالُوا لَبِثْنا يَوْماً أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ قالُوا رَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِما لَبِثْتُمْ (One of them said, ‘How long have you stayed?’ They said, ‘We have stayed a day or part of a day.’ They said, ‘Your Lord knows best how long you have stayed’) that it suggests, rather indicates, that their number was not less than seven.

Among the subtle artistry of the verse in counting the sayings is its arrangement of the numbers from three to eight in a continuous sequence: for it contains three, the fourth of which is [four], five, the sixth of which is [six], seven, and the eighth of which is [eight].

As for His saying: رَجْماً بِالْغَيْبِ (guessing at the Unseen), it is a specification (تمييز) describing the two sayings as being from speech without knowledge. Al-rajm is the throwing of stones. It is as if what is meant by the Unseen (الغيب) is the absent (الغائب), which is the speech whose meaning is absent from knowledge; its speaker does not know whether it is truth or falsehood. Thus, the one who casts such speech is likened to one who intends to throw stones and throws what he does not know—is it a stone that will hit his target or not? Perhaps this is what is meant by the saying of some: rajman bi-l-ghayb means casting by conjecture (الظن), because the conjectured is absent from the conjecturer, who has no knowledge of it.

It has been said: the meaning of rajman bi-l-ghayb is "conjecturing about the Unseen," but this is far-fetched.

He, the Exalted, said: ثَلاثَةٌ رابِعُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ (three, their dog the fourth of them) and said: خَمْسَةٌ سادِسُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ (five, their dog the sixth of them) without bringing a "and" (واو). Then He said: سَبْعَةٌ وَثامِنُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ (seven, and their dog the eighth of them), bringing a "and." It is said in al-Kashshaf: "Three" is the predicate of a deleted subject, i.e., "They were three." Likewise "five" and "seven." "Their dog the fourth of them" is a sentence consisting of a subject and predicate occurring as an adjective for "three." Likewise "their dog the sixth of them" and "their dog the eighth of them."

If you ask: "So what is this 'and' (واو) entering the third sentence? And why did it enter it and not the first two?"

I say: It is the "and" that enters the sentence occurring as an adjective for an indefinite noun (نكرة), just as it enters that which occurs as a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for a definite noun (معرفة), as in your saying: "A man came to me and with him was another" and "I passed by Zayd and in his hand was a sword." From this is His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَهْلَكْنا مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا وَلَها كِتابٌ مَعْلُومٌ (We did not destroy any town but that it had a known decree). Its benefit is to emphasize the attachment of the adjective to the described and to indicate that its being described by it is a fixed and established matter.

This "and" is what signaled that those who said "seven, and their dog the eighth of them" said it out of established knowledge and tranquility of soul, and did not guess by conjecture as others did. The evidence for this is that Allah, the Glorified, followed the first two sayings with His saying: رَجْماً بِالْغَيْبِ (guessing at the Unseen), and followed the third saying with His saying: ما يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ (none knows them except a few). Ibn Abbas said: "When the 'and' occurred, the counting was cut off," meaning no other count remained after it to be turned to, and it was established that they were seven and their dog the eighth of them with certainty and firmness. End quote.

It is stated in al-Majma', in the conclusion of what he summarized from the speech of Abu Ali al-Farisi: "As for those who said: this 'and' is the 'and of eight' (واو الثمانية) and argued from His saying: حَتَّى إِذا جاؤُها وَفُتِحَتْ أَبْوابُها (until, when they come to it and its gates are opened) because Paradise has eight gates—this is something unknown to the grammarians." End quote.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ رَبِّي أَعْلَمُ بِعِدَّتِهِمْ ما يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ (Say, ‘My Lord knows best their number. None knows them except a few.’) until the end of the verse. It is a command to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to judge regarding their number with the rightful judgment, which is that Allah knows it best. He has alluded in His previous speech to the [correct] saying. This is similar to what He narrated from the youths in their dialogue and [His] approval, when one of them said: "How long have you stayed?" They said: "We have stayed a day or part of a day." They said: "Your Lord knows best how long you have stayed."

Despite this, there is in the speech an indication that some of those addressed by the Prophet's address رَبِّي أَعْلَمُ بِعِدَّتِهِمْ (My Lord knows best their number) etc., were possessed of knowledge regarding that. For His saying: ما يَعْلَمُهُمْ (none knows them)—and He did not say la ya'lamuhum—denotes the negation of the present state (نفي الحال). Thus, the exception from it by His saying: إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ (except a few) denotes affirmation in the present state. What appears from this to the mind is that they are from the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب).

In short, the import of the speech is that the three sayings were established in the era of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Based on this, His saying: سَيَقُولُونَ ثَلاثَةٌ (They will say, ‘[They were] three’) etc., which denotes the future, as well as His saying: وَيَقُولُونَ خَمْسَةٌ (and they say, ‘[They were] five’) etc., and His saying: وَيَقُولُونَ سَبْعَةٌ (and they say, ‘[They were] seven’) etc.—if the latter two are conjoined to the object of the sin in "they will say" (سَيَقُولُونَ)—denote the near future relative to the time of the revelation of the verses or the time of the occurrence of the event. Understand that.

And His saying, the Exalted: فَلا تُمارِ فِيهِمْ إِلَّا مِراءً ظاهِراً (So do not dispute concerning them, except for a superficial dispute). Al-Raghib said: Al-miryah (المرية) is hesitation in a matter, and it is more specific than doubt (الشك). He said: al-imtira' and al-mumarah (المماراة) are arguing in that which contains miryah. He said: its origin is from maraytu al-naqah when you rub the udder of the she-camel for milking. End quote. Thus, naming the debate mumarah is due to the persistence of the disputant in the investigation so that his opponent exhausts all the speech he has and then desists from him.

What is meant by the dispute being "superficial" (ظاهراً) is not to delve deeply into it, by sufficing with what the Quran has narrated without accusing them of ignorance or rebutting them, as has been said. It has also been said: the superficial dispute is that which does away with the opponent's argument; it is said zahara (appeared/prevailed) when it has gone away. The poet said:

And that is a complaint whose shame has departed (ظاهر) from you.

The meaning is: Since your Lord knows best and has informed you of their news, do not argue with them regarding the youths except with a superficial argument that does not delve deep—or an argument that does away with their argument—and do not seek a ruling (الفتيا) regarding the youths from any of them, for your Lord is sufficient for you.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَقُولَنَّ لِشَيْءٍ إِنِّي فاعِلٌ ذلِكَ غَداً إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ (Do not say about anything, ‘I will indeed do it tomorrow,’ without [adding], ‘if Allah wishes.’) This noble verse—whether the address in it is for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) specifically or for him and others—addresses the matter that a human being regards as his own action and informs of its occurrence from him in the future time.

That which the Quran holds in its divine teaching is that whatever exists—be it an essence (ذاتاً), an act (فعلاً), or an effect (أثراً)—is owned by Allah alone. He has the right to do with it what He wishes and to decree regarding it what He intends; there is no reversing His decree. No one else has the right to own anything except what Allah, the Exalted, has made him own and empowered him over; and He is the Owner of what He has made him own and the Powerful over what He has empowered him over. The Quranic verses indicating this reality are very many; there is no need to cite them.

Thus, whatever in the cosmos has an act or an effect—and these are what we call agents (فواعل), causes (أسباباً), and active causes (عللاً فعالة)—is not independent in its causality, nor is it independent of Him, the Exalted, in its act and influence. It does not act or influence except what Allah wishes it to act and influence, meaning He empowered it over it and did not strip it of the power over it by willing the opposite.

In other words, every cause among the cosmic causes is not a cause of its own accord or by a requirement of its essence, but rather by His empowering it, the Exalted, over the act and influence and His not willing the opposite. If you wish, say: by His facilitating, the Exalted, for it the way to reach it. If you wish, say: by His permission (إذنه). Permission is the empowering and the removal of the obstacle. The verses indicating that every work of every worker is dependent upon His permission, the Exalted, have multiplied. He, the Exalted, said: ما قَطَعْتُمْ مِنْ لِينَةٍ أَوْ تَرَكْتُمُوها قائِمَةً عَلى أُصُولِها فَبِإِذْنِ اللهِ (Whatever palm tree you cut down or left standing on its roots, it was by Allah’s leave) [Al-Hashr: 5]. And He said: ما أَصابَ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ (No affliction visits [anyone] except by Allah’s leave) [Al-Taghabun: 11]. And He said: وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَباتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ (As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the leave of its Lord) [Al-A'raf: 58]. And He said: وَما كانَ لِنَفْسٍ أَنْ تَمُوتَ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ (A soul may not die except by Allah’s leave) [Al-Imran: 145]. And He said: وَما كانَ لِنَفْسٍ أَنْ تُؤْمِنَ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ (No soul may believe except by Allah’s leave) [Yunus: 100]. And He said: وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا لِيُطاعَ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ (We did not send any apostle but to be obeyed by Allah’s leave) [Al-Nisa: 64], among many other verses.

Therefore, it is incumbent upon the human being who is cognizant of the station of his Lord and surrendered to Him not to see himself as an independent cause for his action, self-sufficient in it from others, but rather as owning it through Allah's making him own it, and powerful over it through His empowering him; and that power belongs to Allah entirely. When he resolves upon an act, he should resolve while relying upon Allah; He, the Exalted, said: فَإِذا عَزَمْتَ فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ (And when you have decided, then put your trust in Allah). And when he promises something or informs of what he will do, he should condition it upon Allah's permission or His not willing the opposite.

This meaning is what first comes to the mind that has been preceded by this Quranic reality when His saying, the Exalted, strikes its door: وَلا تَقُولَنَّ لِشَيْءٍ إِنِّي فاعِلٌ ذلِكَ غَداً إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ (Do not say about anything, ‘I will indeed do it tomorrow,’ without [adding], ‘if Allah wishes.’) Especially after what has preceded in the verses of the story of the explanation of His uniqueness, the Exalted, in His divinity and lordship, and what preceded before the verses of the story regarding that which is on the earth being an adornment for it which Allah will make into a barren dust. Among what is on the earth are the actions of the human being, which are an adornment attracting the human being by which he is tested, while he sees them as owned by himself.

That is because His saying: وَلا تَقُولَنَّ لِشَيْءٍ إِنِّي فاعِلٌ ذلِكَ غَداً (Do not say about anything, ‘I will indeed do it tomorrow’) is a prohibition against attributing his action to himself. There is certainly no harm in this attribution [in itself], for He, the Glorified, frequently attributes actions in His speech to His Prophet and to others among the people, and sometimes commands him to attribute actions to himself. He, the Exalted, said: فَقُلْ لِي عَمَلِي وَلَكُمْ عَمَلُكُمْ (say, ‘My deeds belong to me and your deeds belong to you’) [Yunus: 41]. And He said: لَنا أَعْمالُنا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمالُكُمْ (Our deeds belong to us and your deeds belong to you) [Al-Shura: 15].

Thus, the basis of attributing the act to its agent is not something the Noble Quran denies. Rather, it denies the claim of independence in the act and self-sufficiency from His will and permission, the Exalted. This is what the exception—namely His saying: إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ (unless Allah wishes)—rectifies.

From here it becomes clear that the speech assumes the ba' of accompaniment (باء الملابسة), and it is a voided exception (استثناء مفرغ) from all states or all times. Its estimation is: "And do not say for anything—meaning for the sake of something you resolve upon—'I will indeed do that tomorrow' in any state among states or any time among times, except in a state or time that accompanies your saying with the Will, by saying: 'I will indeed do that tomorrow if Allah wishes that I do it' or 'unless Allah wishes that I do not do it'." The meaning in any case is: "if Allah permits its being done."

This is what reflection upon the meaning of the verse yields, and its conclusion supports it. The exegetes have other interpretations for it.

Among them: that the meaning is the previous meaning, except that the speech assumes the word "saying" (القول) in the exception. The estimation of the speech is: "unless you say: if Allah wishes." When "you say" was deleted, "if Allah wishes" was transferred to the wording of the future. Thus, this would be a refinement from Allah for the servants and a teaching for them to link what they inform of with this phrase, so that it exits the limit of definitive assertion; thus, no lie or breach of oath (حنث) would be incumbent upon them if they do not do it due to an obstacle. This view is attributed to al-Akhfash.

The flaw in this is that it is an unnecessary affectation. Moreover, the mentioned substitution changes the meaning, which is apparent.

Among them: that the speech is according to its literal sense, except that the interpreted infinitive (المصدر المؤول) "that Allah wishes" (أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ) is in the sense of the object (المفعول). The meaning is: "Do not say about anything, 'I will indeed do that tomorrow,' except that which Allah wishes and intends." Since Allah only wishes acts of obedience (الطاعات), it is as if it were said: "And do not say regarding anything, 'I will indeed do it,' except acts of obedience." The prohibition is for discouragement (التنزيه), not for forbiddenness (التحريم), so that it is not objected to by the permissibility of resolving upon permissible acts (المباحات) and informing of them.

The flaw in this is that it is based on taking the Will (المشية) to mean the legislative will (الإرادة التشريعية), for which there is no evidence. The Will has never been used in His speech, the Exalted, with this meaning. The exception of the ontological will (المشية التكوينية) has been used in several places in His speech, as narrated from the saying of Moses to Khidr: سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ صابِراً (You will find me, if Allah wishes, to be patient) [Al-Kahf: 69]; and the saying of Shu'ayb to Moses: سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ (You will find me, if Allah wishes, to be one of the righteous) [Al-Qasas: 27]; and the saying of Ishmael to his father: سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ مِنَ الصَّابِرِينَ (You will find me, if Allah wishes, to be one of the patient) [Al-Saffat: 102]; and His saying, the Exalted: لَتَدْخُلُنَّ الْمَسْجِدَ الْحَرامَ إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ آمِنِينَ (You will surely enter the Holy Mosque, if Allah wishes, in security) [Al-Fath: 27], among other verses.

This view is based on Mu'tazilite principles. According to the Mu'tazilites, Allah, the Glorified, has no will in the actions of the servants except the legislative will related to acts of obedience. This is refuted by both reason and tradition.

Among them: that the exception is from the act rather than the saying, without need for estimation. The meaning is: "Do not say about anything in this manner—which is to say: 'I will indeed do that tomorrow by my independence'—unless Allah wishes otherwise by manifesting an obstacle," according to what the Mu'tazilites say: that the servant is an independent agent of the act unless Allah manifests an obstacle against it that is stronger than him. The outcome of the meaning is: "Do not speak regarding the act according to the saying of the Mu'tazilites."

The flaw in this is that the attachment of the exception to the act rather than the saying, according to the previous explanation, is more complete. Thus, there is no reason for the prohibition against attaching the exception to the act. Its attachment to the act has occurred in several places in His speech without Him rejecting it, such as His saying narrating from Abraham: وَلا أَخافُ ما تُشْرِكُونَ بِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ رَبِّي شَيْئاً (I do not fear what you ascribe to Him as partners, unless my Lord wishes something) [Al-An'am: 80]; and His saying narrating from Shu'ayb: وَما يَكُونُ لَنا أَنْ نَعُودَ فِيها إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ (It is not for us to return to it, unless Allah, our Lord, wishes) [Al-A'raf: 89]; and His saying: ما كانُوا لِيُؤْمِنُوا إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ (they would not believe unless Allah wished) [Al-An'am: 111], among other verses. So let the verse we are in be interpreted according to what accords with them.

Among them: that the exception is from the most general of times, except that the object of "wishes" (يَشاءَ) is the saying. The meaning is: "And do not say that unless Allah wishes that you say it." What is meant by the Will is permission (الإذن), meaning: "Do not say that unless you are permitted in it by being informed."

The flaw in this is that it is based on the estimation of something for which there is no evidence from the perspective of the wording, which is "informing." Had it not been estimated, it would be a duty regarding the unknown.

Among them: that the exception is for eternity (التأبيد), similar to His saying: خالِدِينَ فِيها ما دامَتِ السَّماواتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا ما شاءَ رَبُّكَ عَطاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ (to remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord may wish—an endless bounty) [Hud: 108]. The meaning is: "Do not say that ever."

The flaw in this is that it contradicts the many verses cited just now which attribute to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and to other people their actions, past and future; rather, they command the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to attribute his actions to himself, such as His saying: فَقُلْ لِي عَمَلِي وَلَكُمْ عَمَلُكُمْ (say, ‘My deeds belong to me and your deeds belong to you’) [Yunus: 41], and His saying: قُلْ سَأَتْلُوا عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْهُ ذِكْراً (Say, ‘I will soon recite to you a narrative of him’) [Al-Kahf: 83].

His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرْ رَبَّكَ إِذا نَسِيتَ وَقُلْ عَسى أَنْ يَهْدِيَنِ رَبِّي لِأَقْرَبَ مِنْ هذا رَشَداً (And remember your Lord when you forget, and say, ‘Maybe my Lord will guide me to [something] closer to rectitude than this.’) The connection of the verse and its sharing with what preceded it in the context of duty (التكليف) requires that what is meant by forgetfulness (النسيان) is the forgetting of the exception. Accordingly, what is meant by remembering his Lord is remembering Him in His station, the attention to which was the cause for the exception—which is that He is the One standing over every soul with what it has earned, Who made him own the act and empowered him over it, and He is the Owner of what He made him own and the Powerful over what He empowered him over.

The meaning is: When you forget the exception and then remember that you forgot it, then remember your Lord whenever that may be, with that which you would have remembered Him with had you been mindful—which is the surrender of ownership and power to Him and the conditioning of actions upon His permission and will.

Since the command for remembrance is absolute and not specified by a particular wording, that which is encouraged is to remember Him, the Exalted, in His specific status, whether it be with the wording of the exception by attaching it to the speech—if he remembers it while the speech is not yet complete—or repeating the speech and making the exception, or intending the speech and then making the exception if there is a short or long interval, as has been mentioned in some (1) narrations that when the verses were revealed, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "If Allah wishes" (إن شاء الله); or the remembrance be by seeking forgiveness (استغفار) and the like.

It appears from what has passed that what some have mentioned—that the verse is independent of what preceded it and that what is meant by forgetfulness is forgetting Him, the Exalted, or absolute forgetfulness, the meaning being: "And remember your Lord when you have forgotten Him and then remembered Him" or "And remember your Lord when you forget anything among things"—as well as what some have mentioned based on the previous view that what is meant by His remembrance, the Exalted, is specifically the exception even if the interval is long, or specifically seeking forgiveness or regret over negligence—all of these are unsound views.

And His saying: وَقُلْ عَسى أَنْ يَهْدِيَنِ رَبِّي لِأَقْرَبَ مِنْ هذا رَشَداً (and say, ‘Maybe my Lord will guide me to [something] closer to rectitude than this.’) The connection and sharing in the context of duty between the clauses of the verse also require here that the reference in His saying "this" (هذا) be to the remembrance after forgetfulness. The meaning is: And hope that your Lord will guide you to a matter that is closer in rectitude (رشداً) than forgetfulness followed by remembrance, which is permanent remembrance without forgetfulness. Thus, it would be among the verses calling him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to permanent remembrance, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرْ رَبَّكَ فِي نَفْسِكَ تَضَرُّعاً وَخِيفَةً وَدُونَ الْجَهْرِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ بِالْغُدُوِّ وَالْآصالِ وَلا تَكُنْ مِنَ الْغافِلِينَ (And remember your Lord within your heart beseechingly and fearfully, and without raising your voice, at morning and evening, and do not be among the heedless) [Al-A'raf: 205]. Remembering a thing whenever it is forgotten and then remembered, and guarding it time after time, is among the causes of the permanence of His remembrance.

It is strange that the exegetes have taken His saying "this" (هذا) in the verse as a reference to the news of the People of the Cave and mentioned that the meaning of the verse is: "Say: maybe my Lord will give me from the signs indicating my prophethood that which is closer in guiding the people than the news of the People of the Cave." This is as you see.

Even stranger is what is narrated from some that this is a reference to the forgotten thing, and that the meaning of the verse is: "Supplicate to Allah when you forget something that He may remind you of it, and say—if He does not remind you of what you forgot—'Maybe my Lord will guide me to something that is closer in good and benefit than the forgotten thing'."

Even stranger is what is narrated from another that His saying: وَقُلْ عَسى أَنْ يَهْدِيَنِ (and say, ‘Maybe...’) etc., is an explanatory conjunction for His saying: وَاذْكُرْ رَبَّكَ إِذا نَسِيتَ (And remember your Lord when you forget). The meaning is: "When forgetfulness occurs from you, then repent to your Lord, and your repentance is to say: 'Maybe my Lord will guide me to [something] closer to rectitude than this'." It is possible to make the second and third views a single view; their basis in any case is that what is meant by His saying "when you forget" (إِذا نَسِيتَ) is absolute forgetfulness, and you have already recognized what is in that.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَبِثُوا فِي كَهْفِهِمْ ثَلاثَ مِائَةٍ سِنِينَ وَازْدَادُوا تِسْعاً (They stayed in their cave for three hundred years, and added nine more.) An explanation of the duration of their stay in the cave in the state of sleep. For this stay is the object of concern in the verses of the story. The duration of the stay was alluded to summarily by His saying at the beginning of the verses: فَضَرَبْنا عَلَى آذانِهِمْ فِي الْكَهْفِ سِنِينَ عَدَداً (So We sealed their ears in the cave for a number of years).

This is supported by its being followed by His saying in the next verse: قُلِ اللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما لَبِثُوا (Say, ‘Allah knows best how long they stayed’), then His saying: وَاتْلُ ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ (Recite what has been revealed to you) etc., then His saying: وَقُلِ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ (And say, ‘[This is] the truth from your Lord’). He did not mention any number other than this. Thus, His saying: قُلِ اللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما لَبِثُوا (Say, ‘Allah knows best how long they stayed’) after mentioning the duration of the stay is like His saying: قُلْ رَبِّي أَعْلَمُ بِعِدَّتِهِمْ (Say, ‘My Lord knows best their number’), alluding to the correctness of the mentioned number.

Therefore, no heed should be paid to the saying of the one who says that His saying: وَلَبِثُوا فِي كَهْفِهِمْ (They stayed in their cave) etc., is a narration of the saying of the People of the Book and His saying: قُلِ اللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما لَبِثُوا (Say, ‘Allah knows best how long they stayed’) is a rebuttal to it. Likewise, the saying of the one who says that His saying: وَلَبِثُوا (They stayed) etc., is the word of Allah, the Exalted, and His saying: وَازْدَادُوا تِسْعاً (and added nine more) is an allusion to the saying of the People of the Book—the pronoun referring to them—the meaning being that the People of the Book added nine years to the actual number, and then His saying: قُلِ اللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما لَبِثُوا (Say, ‘Allah knows best how long they stayed’) is a rebuttal to it. Moreover, what is narrated from them is that they spoke of their stay being two hundred years or less, not three hundred and nine nor three hundred.

His saying: سِنِينَ (years) is not a specification (مميز) for the number, otherwise it would have been said thalatha mi'ati sanatin (three hundred year [singular]); rather, it is a substitute (بدل) for "three hundred," as they have said. In the speech is a resemblance to His saying in what was summarized at the beginning of the verses: سِنِينَ عَدَداً (a number of years).

Perhaps the subtle point in substituting "years" (سِنِينَ) for "year" (سنة) is to emphasize the length of the duration of the stay. Based on this, His saying: وَازْدَادُوا تِسْعاً (and added nine more) is not without the meaning of digression (الإضراب), as if it were said: "And they stayed in their cave for three hundred years—these long years and extended age—rather, they added nine." This does not contradict what preceded in His saying: سِنِينَ عَدَداً (a number of years) that this was to minimize the number of years and belittle it. For the two stations are different according to the purpose. The purpose there was related to denying wonder at the sign of the cave by comparing it to the sign of making what is on the earth an adornment for it; thus, what was more appropriate to it was the belittling of the duration. The purpose here is the explanation of the stay being one of His signs and a proof against the deniers of the Resurrection; what is more appropriate to it is the emphasizing of the length of the duration. The duration, in both respects, admits both descriptions: it is, relative to Him, the Exalted, a trivial thing, and relative to us, a long age.

The addition of nine years to the three hundred years of the duration of the stay suggests that they stayed in their cave for three hundred solar years. For the difference in three hundred years, if taken once as solar and another as lunar, reaches approximately this amount. There should be no doubt that what is meant by "years" in the verse are lunar years, because the year in the usage of the Quran is the lunar one composed of the crescent months, and it is what is considered in the Islamic Law (الشريعة).

In al-Tafsir al-Kabir, he strongly denounced this due to the lack of exact correspondence between the two numbers upon investigation, and he questioned what was narrated from Ali (peace be upon him) in this regard. However, the difference between the two numbers—three hundred solar and three hundred and nine lunar years—is less than three months, and approximation (التقريب) in such ratios is widespread in speech without dispute.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ اللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما لَبِثُوا لَهُ غَيْبُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (Say, ‘Allah knows best how long they stayed. To Him belongs the Unseen of the heavens and the earth.’) until the end of the verse. A continuation in the discourse of the People of the Cave by alluding to the disagreement of the people in that, and that what Allah, the Exalted, narrated of their story is the truth in which there is no doubt.

Thus, His saying: قُلِ اللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما لَبِثُوا (Say, ‘Allah knows best how long they stayed’) suggests that the duration of their stay mentioned in the previous verse was not accepted among the people; so the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was commanded to argue in that by the knowledge of Allah, and that He knows them better than anyone else.

And His saying: لَهُ غَيْبُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ (To Him belongs the Unseen of the heavens and the earth) is a justification for His being, the Exalted, most knowing of how long they stayed. The lam is for possessive exclusivity (الاختصاص الملكي). What is meant is that He, the Exalted, alone owns what is in the heavens and the earth of the Unseen (غيب) that is not witnessed; thus, nothing escapes Him even if it escapes the heavens and the earth. Since He is the Owner of the Unseen by the reality of the meaning of ownership, and He possesses perfection of sight and hearing, He is most knowing of their stay, which is from the Unseen.

Based on this, His saying: أَبْصِرْ بِهِ وَأَسْمِعْ (How well He sees and hears!)—which are forms of wonder (صيغ التعجب) meaning the perfection of His sight and hearing—is to complete the justification. It is as if it were said: "And how could He not be most knowing of their stay when He owns them despite their being from the Unseen, and He has seen their state and heard their speech?"

From here it becomes clear that the saying of some—that the lam in "To Him belongs the Unseen" (لَهُ غَيْبُ) etc., is for epistemic exclusivity (الاختصاص العلمي), meaning that to Him, the Exalted, belongs that in terms of knowledge, and it necessitates the establishment of His knowledge for all other creatures because he who knows the hidden knows other things by greater reason—is unsound. This is because the appearance of His saying: أَبْصِرْ بِهِ وَأَسْمِعْ is for foundation (التأسيس) rather than emphasis (التأكيد), and likewise the appearance of the lam is absolute ownership rather than epistemic ownership.

And His saying: ما لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍ (They have no guardian besides Him) etc. What is meant by the first clause is the negation of the guardianship (ولاية) of other than Allah for them independently of Allah. By the second, the negation of the guardianship of other than Him by sharing it with Him; meaning, they have no guardian other than Allah, neither independently nor non-independently.

It is not improbable to benefit from the arrangement—considering the expression in the second clause وَلا يُشْرِكُ فِي حُكْمِهِ أَحَداً (and He does not make anyone a partner in His judgment) with the verb rather than the description, and the attachment of the negation of partnership to judgment (الحكم) rather than guardianship (الولاية)—that the first clause negates the guardianship of other than Him, the Exalted, for them, whether it be by independence—thus being independent in managing their affair without Allah—or by partnership—thus overseeing some of their affairs without Allah. The second clause negates the partnership of other than Him, the Exalted, in judgment and decree; in that their guardianship belongs to Allah, the Exalted, but He has appointed another over them and delegated their affair and the judgment over them to him, as rulers do in appointing judges and officials in different branches of their affairs, whereby the judges and officials carry out judgments of which the rulers have no knowledge.

The meaning results in: how could He, the Exalted, not be most knowing of their stay when He, the Exalted, alone is their Guardian who directly carries out the judgment running in them and over them?

The pronoun in His saying: لَهُمْ (for them) refers to the People of the Cave, or to all that is in the heavens and the earth understood from the previous clause by giving preponderance to the side of the possessors of intellect, or to whoever is in the heavens and the earth. The three views are arranged in quality, and the best of them is the first.

Accordingly, the verse contains two proofs that Allah is most knowing of how long they stayed. One is a general proof for them and others, which is His saying: لَهُ غَيْبُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَبْصِرْ بِهِ وَأَسْمِعْ (To Him belongs the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. How well He sees and hears!). Thus, He is most knowing of all things, and among them is the stay of the People of the Cave. The second is a specific proof regarding them, which is His saying: ما لَهُمْ (They have no...) until the end of the verse. Thus, He, the Exalted, is their Guardian who directly carries out the decree running over them; so how could He not be most knowing of them than anyone else? Due to the causal nature (العلية) in the two clauses, they were presented without a conjunction.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ حَسِبْتَ أَنَّ أَصْحابَ الْكَهْفِ (Do you suppose that the Companions of the Cave...) the verse. He said: He says: "We have given you of the signs that which is more wondrous than it." And they were youths who were in the interval (الفترة) between Jesus son of Mary and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). As for the Inscription (الرقيم), they were two tablets of inscribed copper—meaning written upon them was the affair of the youths, the matter of their Islam, what the king Decius (دقيانوس) wanted from them, and how their affair and state were.

In it: my father narrated to us from Ibn Abi Umayr from Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: The cause for the revelation of Surah al-Kahf was that Quraysh sent three individuals to Najran: al-Nadr ibn al-Harith ibn Kaldah, Uqbah ibn Abi Mu'ayt, and al-As ibn Wa'il al-Sahmi, to learn from the Jews questions to ask the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

So they went out to Najran to the scholars of the Jews and asked them. They said: "Ask him about three questions—if he answers you regarding them according to what we have, then he is truthful—then ask him about one question; if he claims knowledge of it, then he is a liar."

They said: "And what are these questions?" They said: "Ask him about youths who were in the first age—they went out, disappeared, and slept; how long did they remain in their sleep until they woke up? And what was their number? And what was with them from other than themselves? And what was their story? And ask him about Moses when Allah commanded him to follow the Scholar and learn from him—who was he? And how did he follow him? And what was his story with him? And ask him about a traveler who traveled the setting of the sun and its rising until he reached the barrier of Gog and Magog—who was he? And how was his story?" Then they dictated to them the reports of these three questions and said to them: "If he answers you with what we have dictated to you, then he is truthful; and if he informs you contrary to that, then do not believe him."

They said: "So what is the fourth question?" They said: "Ask him when the Hour will occur! For if he claims knowledge of it, then he is a liar—for the occurrence of the Hour is known to none but Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He."

So they returned to Mecca and gathered with Abu Talib and said: "O Abu Talib, your nephew claims that the news of heaven comes to him—and we are asking him about questions—if he answers us regarding them, we will know that he is truthful—and if he does not inform us, we will know that he is a liar." Abu Talib said: "Ask him about whatever occurs to you." So they asked him about the three questions. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Tomorrow I will inform you," and he did not make the exception (يستثن). So the revelation was withheld from him for forty days until the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) became distressed—and his companions who had believed in him doubted, and Quraysh rejoiced and mocked and caused harm, and Abu Talib was saddened.

When forty days had passed, Surah al-Kahf was revealed to him. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "O Gabriel, you have delayed." He said: "We are not able to descend except by the permission of Allah." So Allah, the Exalted, revealed: أَمْ حَسِبْتَ (Do you suppose), O Muhammad, أَنَّ أَصْحابَ الْكَهْفِ وَالرَّقِيمِ كانُوا مِنْ آياتِنا عَجَباً (that the Companions of the Cave and the Inscription were among Our wondrous signs?) Then He narrated their story and said: إِذْ أَوَى الْفِتْيَةُ إِلَى الْكَهْفِ فَقالُوا رَبَّنا آتِنا مِنْ لَدُنْكَ رَحْمَةً وَهَيِّئْ لَنا مِنْ أَمْرِنا رَشَداً (When the youths took refuge in the cave, they said, ‘Our Lord, grant us a mercy from Yourself, and help us [to find] rectitude in our affair.’)

He said: So al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: The Companions of the Cave and the Inscription were in the time of a tyrannical, rebellious king, and he used to call the people of his kingdom to the worship of idols; whoever did not answer him, he killed him. These were a believing people who worshipped Allah, Mighty and Majestic. The king appointed guards at the gate of the city and did not let anyone exit until he prostrated to the idols. These [youths] went out under the pretext of hunting—and that was because they passed by a shepherd on their way and called him to their affair—he did not answer them, but with the shepherd was a dog—so the dog answered them and went out with them.

He (peace be upon him) said: So the Companions of the Cave went out from the city under the pretext of hunting—fleeing from the religion of that king. When evening came, they entered that cave and the dog was with them. So Allah cast slumber upon them as Allah said: فَضَرَبْنا عَلَى آذانِهِمْ فِي الْكَهْفِ سِنِينَ عَدَداً (So We sealed their ears in the cave for a number of years). So they slept until Allah destroyed that king and the people of the city—and that time went away and another time and other people came.

Then they woke up and some of them said to others: "How long have we slept here?" They looked at the sun and it had risen, so they said: "We have slept a day or part of a day." Then they said to one of them: "Take this silver and enter the city in disguise so they do not recognize you, and buy food for us—for if they come to know of us and recognize us, they will kill us or return us to their religion."

So that man came and saw a city contrary to the one he knew—and he saw a people contrary to those; he did not recognize them—and they did not recognize his language and he did not recognize their language. They said to him: "Who are you, and from where have you come?" So he informed them. Then the king of that city went out with his companions and the man was with them—until they stood at the door of the cave and began looking into it. Some of them said: "These are three, their dog the fourth of them." And some of them said: "Five, their dog the sixth of them." And some of them said: "Seven, and their dog the eighth of them."

Allah (الله) veiled them with a shroud of awe—so that none dared enter upon them except their companion. When he entered, he found them fearful, sensing that they might be the followers of Decius (دقيانوس). Their companion informed them that they had been asleep for this long duration and that they were a sign (آية) for the people. They wept and asked Allah (الله) to return them to their resting places in sleep as they had been.

Then the king said: "We should build a mosque (مسجداً) here that we may visit, for these are a believing people." Every year, they have two turnings: they sleep for six months on their right sides and six months on their left sides. The dog is with them, stretching out its forelegs at the threshold of the cave. This is [the meaning of] His saying, the Exalted: نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ نَبَأَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ "We relate to you their supplement with the truth" [18:13] until the end of the verses.

I say: This narration is among the clearest in terms of its text and the most free from confusion. Nevertheless, it implies that those who differed concerning their number—saying they were three, five, or seven—were the people of the city who gathered at the door of the cave after the youths awoke, which contradicts the apparent meaning of the verse. It also implies that the Companions of the Cave (أصحاب الكهف) did not die a second time but returned to their sleep, as did their dog, stretching its forelegs at the threshold (بالوصيد), and that every year they have two turnings from right to left and vice versa, remaining in their form. Yet, there is no known cave on the face of the earth containing people sleeping in this manner.

Furthermore, in the conclusion of this narration—which we have omitted here due to the possibility of it being the speech of al-Qummi or from another report—it is claimed that His saying, the Exalted: وَلَبِثُوا فِي كَهْفِهِمْ ثَلاثَ مِائَةٍ سِنِينَ وَازْدَادُوا تِسْعاً "And they remained in their cave for three hundred years, and added nine more" [18:25] is the speech of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), and that His subsequent saying: قُلِ اللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما لَبِثُوا "Say, 'Allah knows best how long they remained'" [18:26] is a rebuttal of it. You have already seen in the preceding commentary (البيان) that the context (السياق) refutes this, and the eloquent composition (النظم البليغ) does not accept it.

(1) Their right side (variant). (2) Their left side (variant).

Narrations explaining the story have proliferated through the chains of both sects (الفريقين), yet they are contradictory and divergent; one can hardly find two reports whose content agrees in all respects.

Among the discrepancies is what appears in some narrations, such as the aforementioned one, that the question posed to the Prophet was about four matters: the story of the Companions of the Cave, the story of Moses (عليه السلام) and the Scholar (العالم), the story of Dhul-Qarnayn (ذي القرنين), and the Hour (الساعة)—when will it occur?

In others, the question was about the story of the Companions of the Cave, Dhul-Qarnayn, and the Spirit (الروح). They mentioned that the sign of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) truthfulness was that he would not answer the last of the questions. Thus, he answered regarding the story of the Companions of the Cave and Dhul-Qarnayn, and the verse قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي "Say, 'The Spirit is by my Lord's command'" [17:85] was revealed, so he did not [technically] answer it. However, you have already seen in the commentary on the verse of the Spirit that the speech is structured as an answer and is not an evasion.

Another discrepancy is that most narrations state they were a single group who were called the Companions of the Cave and the Inscription (الرقيم). In some, however, the Companions of the Inscription are distinct from the Companions of the Cave, and that Allah (سبحانه) alluded to both in His speech but narrated the story of the Companions of the Cave and turned away from the story of the Companions of the Inscription. They mentioned a story for the latter: that a group of three men went out seeking provisions for their families when they were caught in a storm. They took shelter in a cave, and a boulder fell from the top of the mountain, blocking its entrance.

They said to one another: "Let each of us mention a righteous deed (عمله الصالح) he performed and supplicate to Allah (الله) by it; perhaps He will grant us relief." One of them mentioned a deed he did for the sake of Allah (الله) and supplicated, so the rock moved enough to let in some light. Then the second did the same until they could recognize each other, then the third, and Allah (الله) relieved them, and they went out. This was narrated by al-Nu'man ibn Bashir as a marfu' (مرفوعاً) — a tradition traced back directly to the Prophet — from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

However, one who is familiar with the style of the Wise Remembrance (الذكر الحكيم) — the Quran — finds it difficult to believe that it would allude to two stories in its call and then detail one while completely forgetting the other.

Among the discrepancies is what the narrations mention regarding the king from whom the youths fled. Some say it was Decius (دقيانوس) (Diocletian 285–305 CE), the Roman Emperor; some say he claimed divinity; others say it was Decius (دسيوس) (Decius 249–251 CE), the Roman Emperor—and between them are decades of years. The king used to call for the worship of idols and kill the people of monotheism (أهل التوحيد). In some narrations, he was a Magian (مجوسياً) calling to the religion of the Magians, though history does not record Magianism being widespread in Roman lands to this extent. In other narrations, they were before Jesus (عليه السلام).

Furthermore, some narrations state that the Inscription (الرقيم) is the name of the town they left; some say it is the name of the valley; some say the name of the mountain containing the cave; some say the name of their dog. Others say it is a tablet of stone, or lead, or copper, or gold, upon which their names, their fathers' names, and their story were inscribed (رقم) and placed at the door of the cave, or inside it, or hung on the city gate, or kept in the royal treasuries. Some say they were two tablets.

Also, some narrations state the youths were sons of kings; some say sons of nobles; some say sons of scholars. Some say they were seven, the seventh being a shepherd who joined them with his dog on the way. In a hadith from Wahb ibn Munabbih, they were bathhouse attendants working in one of the city's baths, and he recounted a story of the king's call to idol worship. In others, they were ministers of the king whom he consulted in his affairs.

Additionally, some narrations state they openly showed their opposition and the king knew of it before they left; others say he only knew after their departure. Some say they conspired to leave together; others say they left without knowing of each other's state or having an appointment, then recognized each other and agreed in the wilderness. Some say a shepherd joined them as the seventh; others say he did not follow them, but his dog followed and went with them.

Moreover, some narrations state that when they fled and the king discovered their matter, he searched for them but found no trace. Others say he searched and found them sleeping in their cave, so he ordered a structure to be built over the cave's entrance to trap them so they would die of hunger and thirst as punishment for their disobedience. They remained in this state until Allah (الله) willed to awaken them, sending a shepherd who demolished the structure to make a pen for his sheep, at which point Allah (الله) raised them awake, and their affair proceeded as Allah (الله) narrated.

Some narrations state that when their matter became known, the king came to them with the people and entered the cave. He spoke to them, and while he was speaking to them and they to him, they bid him farewell, greeted him, and passed away (قضوا نحبهم). Others say they died or slept before the king entered, and the door of the cave was hidden from their sight, so they could not find the way in and thus built a mosque (مسجداً) there to pray in.

Some narrations state their souls were taken; others say Allah (الله) put them back to sleep, and they remain sleeping until the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة), being turned twice every year from right to left and vice versa.

There is also disagreement in the narrations regarding the duration of their stay. Most state that the three hundred and nine years mentioned in the verse is the word of Allah (تعالى). Some say it is a report of the speech of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), and His saying: قُلِ اللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما لَبِثُوا "Say, 'Allah knows best how long they remained'" [18:26] is a rebuttal. Others say the three hundred is the word of Allah (سبحانه) and the addition of nine is the word of the People of the Book.

There are other points of disagreement among the narrations. Most have been collected from Sunni sources in al-Durr al-Manthur and from Shi'i sources in al-Bihar, Tafsir al-Burhan, and Nur al-Thaqalayn. Whoever wishes to examine them should refer there. What can be considered agreed upon, or nearly so, is that they were a monotheistic (موحدين) people who fled from a tyrannical king who forced people into polytheism (الشرك), so they took refuge in the cave and slept, until the end of what Allah (تعالى) narrated.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Sulayman ibn Ja'far al-Hamdani, who said: "Ja'far ibn Muhammad (عليه السلام) said to me: 'O Sulayman, who is the youth (الفتى)?' I said to him: 'May I be your ransom, among us the youth (الفتى) is the young man (الشاب).' He said to me: 'Do you not know that the Companions of the Cave were all middle-aged (كهولاً)? Yet Allah named them youths (فتية) because of their faith (إيمانهم). O Sulayman, whoever believes in Allah and is God-conscious (اتقى) is the youth (الفتى).'"

I say: Something similar is narrated in al-Kafi from al-Qummi as a marfu' (مرفوعاً) report from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام). It has also been narrated from Ibn Abbas that they were young men (شبان).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim recorded from Abu Ja'far that the Companions of the Cave were money-changers (صيارفة).

I say: Al-Qummi also narrated with his chain from Sudayr al-Sayrafi from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) who said: "The Companions of the Cave were money-changers (صيارفة)." However, in Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Durust from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام): he mentioned the Companions of the Cave and said: "They were money-changers (صيارفة) of speech (كلام) and were not money-changers of dirhams."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) who said: "The Companions of the Cave concealed their faith (الإيمان) and outwardly displayed disbelief (الكفر), so Allah rewarded them twice."

I say: Something similar is narrated in al-Kafi from Hisham ibn Salim from him (عليه السلام). Al-Ayyashi also narrated something similar from al-Kahili from him (عليه السلام) and from Durust in two reports from him (عليه السلام). In one of the two reports: "They used to fasten the waist-belts (الزنانير) and attend the festivals (الأعياد)."

This is not contradicted by the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted, recounting their words: إِذْ قامُوا فَقالُوا رَبُّنا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ لَنْ نَدْعُوَا مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلهاً "When they stood up and said, 'Our Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth. We will never invoke any god besides Him'" [18:14], which suggests they did not practice precautionary concealment (التقية), as some exegetes assumed in interpreting His saying recounting their words: أَوْ يُعِيدُوكُمْ فِي مِلَّتِهِمْ وَلَنْ تُفْلِحُوا إِذاً أَبَداً "or force you back into their creed, and then you will never be felicitous" [18:20], as previously discussed.

This is because you have realized that their departure from the city was a migration (هجرة) from the abode of polytheism (دار الشرك) which prevented them from manifesting the word of truth and practicing the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد).
