However, their conspiracy to leave—being six prominent and noble men—and their abandonment of family, wealth, and homeland could only be characterized as opposition to the idolatrous religion. They were in grave danger had the people discovered them, and their situation would have ended in one of two ways: stoning or forced entry into the people's creed.

Thus, it becomes clear that their initial standing and saying: رَبُّنا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ لَنْ نَدْعُوَا مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلهاً was not a public demonstration of opposition or an open condemnation of the people's creed and a denunciation of their way, for the general circumstances would not have permitted them that. Rather, it was a standing for Allah (قياماً لله) and a determination to remain firm upon the word of monotheism. Even if the phrase إِذْ قامُوا فَقالُوا were to indicate public manifestation and the rejection of precautionary concealment (التقية), it would have been in the final days of their stay among the people, whereas before that, they were undoubtedly practicing concealment. Thus, it is clear that the context of neither verse contradicts the youths practicing precautionary concealment as long as they were among the people in the city.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, also from Abu Bakr al-Hadrami from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) who said: "The Companions of the Cave went out without prior knowledge of each other or an appointment. When they reached the wilderness, they took covenants and pacts from one another. This one took from that one, and that one from this one. Then they said: 'Reveal your matter,' so they revealed it, and behold, they were all upon the same matter."

I say: Something similar is narrated from Ibn Abbas in the following report.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim recorded from Ibn Abbas who said: "We went on a campaign with Mu'awiyah, the Campaign of the Strait toward Rome. We passed by the cave in which were the Companions of the Cave mentioned by Allah in the Quran. Mu'awiyah said: 'If only they were uncovered for us so we could look at them.' Ibn Abbas said to him: 'That is not for you; Allah prevented that from one who was better than you,' for He said: لَوِ اطَّلَعْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَوَلَّيْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِراراً وَلَمُلِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ رُعْباً 'Had you come upon them, you would have surely turned your back on them in flight, and you would have surely been filled with terror of them' [18:18]. Mu'awiyah said: 'I will not stop until I know their state,' so he sent some men and said: 'Go and enter the cave and look.' They went, but when they entered the cave, Allah sent a wind upon them that drove them out. When this reached Ibn Abbas, he began to narrate about them."

He said: "They were in the kingdom of one of the tyrants. People began to worship until they worshipped idols, while these youths were in the city. When they saw that, they left that city. Allah gathered them without an appointment. Some began to say to others: 'Where are you heading? Where are you going?' Each began to hide his intent from the other because he did not know why the other had left. They took covenants and pacts to inform one another; if they agreed on something, [well and good], otherwise they would keep each other's secret. They agreed on a single word and said: رَبُّنا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ 'Our Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth' until His saying مِرْفَقاً 'a cushion' [18:16]."

He said: "They sat down, and their families came looking for them, not knowing where they had gone. Their matter was raised to the king, who said: 'These people shall surely have a significant story hereafter—people who left and it is not known where they went, without any betrayal or known cause.' He called for a tablet of lead and wrote their names on it, then placed it in his treasury. That is the saying of Allah: أَمْ حَسِبْتَ أَنَّ أَصْحابَ الْكَهْفِ وَالرَّقِيمِ 'Do you suppose that the Companions of the Cave and the Inscription...' [18:9]. The Inscription (الرقيم) is the tablet they wrote. They proceeded until they entered the cave. Allah struck their ears and they slept. Had the sun risen upon them, it would have burned them, and had they not been turned, the earth would have consumed them. That is the saying of Allah: وَتَرَى الشَّمْسَ 'And you see the sun...' [18:17]."

He said: "Then that king passed away and another king came. He worshipped Allah, abandoned those idols, and was just toward the people. Allah raised them for what He willed. One of them said: 'How long have you remained?' Some said: 'A day,' some said: 'Two days,' and some said: 'More than that.' Their leader said: 'Do not disagree, for never did a people disagree but they perished. Send one of you with this silver of yours to the city.'"

"He saw a sign (شارة) — an appearance or dress — that he did not recognize, and buildings he did not recognize. Then he approached a baker and threw a dirham to him. Their dirhams were like the hooves of a young camel (ولد الناقة). The baker did not recognize the dirham and said: 'Where did you get this dirham? You must have found a treasure; either you show it to me or I will take you to the governor.' He said: 'Do you threaten me with the governor?' The village head (الدهقان) brought him to the governor, who asked: 'Who is your father?' He named someone, but the governor did not know him. He asked: 'Who is the king?' He named someone, but the governor did not know him. The people gathered around them, and he was taken to their scholar, who questioned him and was informed. The scholar said: 'Bring me the tablet.' It was brought, and he named his companions, so-and-so and so-and-so, and they were written on the tablet. He said to the people: 'Allah has guided you to your brothers.'"

"They set out and rode until they reached the cave. When they drew near, the youth said: 'Stay in your places while I enter upon my companions. Do not rush in, lest they be terrified of you, for they do not know that Allah has brought you and turned toward you.' They said: 'Will you come back out to us?' He said: 'Yes, if Allah wills.' He entered, and they did not know where he went; it was hidden from them. They searched and tried but were unable to enter upon them. They said: 'We will surely build a mosque (مسجداً) over them.' So they built a mosque over them, praying for them and seeking forgiveness for them."

I say: This narration is famous; exegetes have included it in their commentaries and accepted it. Yet it is not free from issues. Among them is that its apparent meaning suggests they are still in the form of sleepers, and it is impossible to see them due to a divine diversion. The cave in the Strait, which is the well-known Cave of Ephesus today, does not fit this description.

The verse Ibn Abbas used as evidence only describes their state while they were asleep before being raised, not after. Another narration has been reported from Ibn Abbas that contradicts this one, which is in al-Durr al-Manthur from Abd al-Razzaq and Ibn Abi Hatim from Ikrimah. The story is mentioned therein, and at its end: "The king rode and the people rode with him until they reached the cave. The youth said: 'Let me enter to my companions.' When they saw him and he saw them, [Allah] struck their ears. When they [the people] found him slow to return, the king entered and the people entered with him, and behold, there were bodies of which nothing had decayed, except that there were no souls in them. The king said: 'This is a sign Allah has raised for you.'"

"Ibn Abbas went on a campaign with Habib ibn Maslamah and they passed by the cave, and behold, there were bones. A man said: 'These are the bones of the Companions of the Cave.' Ibn Abbas said: 'Their bones have been gone for more than three hundred years...'" (the hadith).

This narration increases the difficulty, as his saying "their bones have been gone..." implies the story occurred at the beginning of the common era or before it, thus contradicting the general narrations except those stating they were before Christ.

Another issue is in the saying: "Some said: 'A day,' and some said: 'Two days'..." whereas what occurs in the Quran is: قالُوا لَبِثْنا يَوْماً أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ قالُوا رَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِما لَبِثْتُمْ "They said, 'We have remained a day, or part of a day.' They said, 'Your Lord knows best how long you have remained'" [18:19]. This is what is rational and accords with the norm for people who slept, then awoke and spoke about the duration of their stay based on circumstantial evidence. As for the possibility of two days or more, there is no way to it, nor does any custom support it. Furthermore, their disagreement in determining the duration was not the blameworthy disagreement—which is disagreement in action that leads to destruction and is thus forbidden—but rather a disagreement in opinion, which is unavoidable.

Another issue is at its end: "He entered and they did not know where he went; it was hidden from them..." If the meaning is what appears in some narrations—that the door of the cave vanished from their sight because Allah wiped it out and effaced it—this does not harmonize with the beginning of the narration stating it was visible and known in those lands. Did Allah efface it for that king and his companions and then manifest it to the people?

Regarding the statement at the beginning of the narration from Ibn Abbas that "the Inscription (الرقيم) is a tablet of lead on which their names were written," al-Ayyashi narrated something similar in his Tafsir from Ahmad ibn Ali from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام). However, other narrations from Ibn Abbas report his denial of this, as in al-Durr al-Manthur from Sa'id ibn Mansur, Abd al-Razzaq, al-Faryabi, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and al-Zajjaji in his Amali, and Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn Abbas who said: "I do not know what the Inscription (الرقيم) is. I asked Ka'b and he said: 'The name of the village they left.'"

Also therein, from Abd al-Razzaq from Ibn Abbas who said: "I know all of the Quran except four [words]: ghislin (غسلين), hananan (حناناً), awwah (أوّاه), and raqim (رقيم)."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَنْ نَدْعُوَا مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلهاً لَقَدْ قُلْنا إِذاً شَطَطاً "We will never invoke any god besides Him, for then we would have said something egregious" [18:14], he said: "Meaning an injustice against Allah if we were to say He has a partner."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Muhammad ibn Sinan from al-Battikhi from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) regarding the saying of Allah: لَوِ اطَّلَعْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَوَلَّيْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِراراً وَلَمُلِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ رُعْباً "Had you come upon them, you would have surely turned your back on them in flight, and you would have surely been filled with terror of them" [18:18], he said: "This did not refer to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); it only referred to the believers [in relation] to one another—but it is the state they are in."

In the commentary Ruh al-Ma'ani, their names according to what is authentic from Ibn Abbas are: Maksalmina, Yamlikha, Martulas, Thabyunus, Dardunus, Kafashititutus, and Mantanwasis—who is the shepherd—and the dog's name is Qitmir.

He said: "It is narrated from Ali, may Allah ennoble his face: Their names are Yamlikha, Maksalminia, and Maslinia—these were the companions of the king's right side; and Marnush, Dabarnush, and Shadhnush—these were the companions of his left side. He used to consult the six, and the seventh was the shepherd." This narration did not mention his name but mentioned the dog's name as Qitmir.

He said: "There is some discussion regarding the authenticity of attributing this narration to Ali, may Allah ennoble his face." Al-Allama al-Suyuti mentioned in the marginalia of al-Baydawi that al-Tabarani narrated this from Ibn Abbas in his al-Mu'jam al-Awsat with a sahīh (صحيح) — authentic — chain. What is in al-Durr al-Manthur is al-Tabarani's narration in al-Awsat with an authentic chain as we previously cited from Ibn Abbas.

He said: "They have been named in some narrations with names other than these." Al-Hafiz ibn Hajar mentioned in the commentary on al-Bukhari that there is much disagreement in the pronunciation of their names and no reliance can be placed on their precision. In al-Bahr, it is stated that the names of the Companions of the Cave are non-Arabic (أعجمية) and cannot be precisely determined by vowels or dots, and the chain of transmission for knowing them is weak. End of his words.

The narration attributed to Ali (عليه السلام) is the one narrated by al-Tha'labi in al-Ara'is and al-Daylami in his book as a marfu' (مرفوعة) report, and it contains wonders.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh recorded from Ibn Abbas who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'The Companions of the Cave are the helpers of the Mahdi (المهدي).'"

In al-Burhan, from Ibn al-Farisi, Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) said: "There shall emerge for the Qa'im (القائم) (عليه السلام) from the back of the Kaaba twenty-seven men from the people of Moses—those who used to guide by the truth and by it were just—and seven from the People of the Cave, and Joshua son of Nun (يوشع بن نون), Abu Dujanah al-Ansari, Miqdad ibn al-Aswad, and Malik al-Ashtar; they shall be before him as helpers and governors."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Abdullah ibn Maymun from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) from his father Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) who said: "If a man swears by Allah, he has the right of exception (ثنياها) up to forty days. That is because a group of Jews asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about something and he said: 'Come to me tomorrow'—and he did not make an exception (ولم يستثن) — saying 'if Allah wills' — 'so that I may inform you.' Gabriel was withheld from him for forty days, then he came to him and said: وَلا تَقُولَنَّ لِشَيْءٍ إِنِّي فاعِلٌ ذلِكَ غَداً ـ إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ وَاذْكُرْ رَبَّكَ إِذا نَسِيتَ 'Do not say about anything, "I will do that tomorrow," without [adding], "if Allah wishes." And remember your Lord when you forget' [18:23-24]."

I say: Al-thunya (الثنيا) is the noun for exception (استثناء). There are other narrations with this meaning from the two Sadiqs (عليهما السلام). It appears from some of them that what is meant by "swearing" is the decisiveness of speech and its emphasis, as hinted at by his (عليه السلام) citation in this narration of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying. As for the discussion on qualifying an oath with it after it has been contracted and whether perjury occurs with it or not, that is deferred to jurisprudence (الفقه).

(Commentary)

A Discussion on the Story of the Companions of the Cave in Several Sections

1 - The story of the Cave has been reported in detail and completeness in several narrations from the Companions (الصحابة), the Successors (التابعين), and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), such as the narration of al-Qummi, Ibn Abbas, Ikrimah, and Mujahid—which were recorded in al-Durr al-Manthur—and the narration of Ibn Ishaq in al-Ara'is—recorded in al-Burhan—and the narration of Wahb ibn Munabbih—recorded in al-Durr al-Manthur and in al-Kamil without attribution—and the narration of al-Nu'man ibn Bashir regarding the Companions of the Inscription (أصحاب الرقيم)—recorded in al-Durr al-Manthur.

These narrations—some of which we cited in the preceding traditional discussion and alluded to others—differ in their texts to such an extent that they hardly agree on any prominent aspect of the story. As for the narrations concerning specific details, such as the time of their standing, the king in whose reign they stood, their lineage, their appearance, their names, and the reason for naming them the Companions of the Inscription, the disagreement is even more intense, and obtaining that which brings tranquility to the soul is more difficult.

The primary reason for the discrepancy in these hadiths, in addition to what has affected such narrations of fabrication and interpolation, is two things:

First: The story is among those cared for by the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), as inferred from the narrations stating that Quraysh received it from them and asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about it. Indeed, it is inferred from the statues—mentioned by historians from the Christians—and from the images found in various caves in different parts of the earth in Asia, Europe, and Africa, that the story acquired a global fame. It is the nature of such stories to manifest to every people in a form that suits their views and beliefs, leading to divergent reports.

Furthermore, the Muslims went to great lengths in receiving and recording the narration, expanding upon it and taking what was with others just as they took what was with themselves. This was especially so as they were joined by a group of scholars from the People of the Book who entered Islam, such as Wahb ibn Munabbih and Ka'b al-Ahbar. The Companions and Successors took much from them regarding the reports of the ancients, then the later generations took from the earlier ones, treating their narrations as mawquf (موقوفة) — traditions ending at a Companion — reports from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), which became a trial.

Second: The custom of His speech, the Exalted, in the stories He relates is to restrict itself to selected important points that are effective in fulfilling the purpose, without expanding the discourse by mentioning the full text or addressing all its aspects, circumstances, and accompanying conditions. For the Book of Allah is not a book of history; it is a book of guidance (هدى).

This is among the clearest things encountered by one who contemplates the stories mentioned in His speech, the Exalted, such as the story of the Companions of the Cave and the Inscription. He first mentioned a portion of their dialogue indicating the meaning of their standing for Allah, their steadfastness upon the word of truth, their subsequent withdrawal from the people, their entry into the cave, their sleep therein with their dog for a long duration, and then He mentioned their being raised from sleep and a second dialogue which led to the discovery of their state and the manifestation of their affair to the people. Then He mentioned the people's discovery of them, indicating their second passing after the divine purpose was achieved, and what was done afterward in taking a mosque over them. This is the course His speech, the Exalted, followed.

He refrained from mentioning their names, lineages, births, how they were raised, the occupations they took for themselves, their position in their society, the time of their standing and withdrawal, the name of the king from whom they fled, the city they left, the people they were among, the name of the dog that accompanied them—whether it was a hunting dog of theirs or a sheepdog belonging to the shepherd—and its color—in which the narrations have delved deeply—and other matters upon which the purpose of guidance does not depend, even if the purpose of historical research does.

Then, when the exegetes from the early generations began to research the verses of the stories, they sought to explain the connection between the verses by joining the omitted parts of the stories to the selected parts taken from them, so as to forge a story complete in its parts and comprehensive in its aspects. Their differing views led to a disagreement similar to the disagreement in transmission, and the matter resulted in what we observe.

2 - The Story of the Companions of the Cave in the Quran: He, the Exalted, said addressing His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): فَلا تُمارِ فِيهِمْ إِلَّا مِراءً ظاهِراً وَلا تَسْتَفْتِ فِيهِمْ مِنْهُمْ أَحَداً "So do not dispute concerning them except with an outer dispute, and do not ask any of them for a pronouncement concerning them" [18:22]. The Companions of the Cave and the Inscription were youths who grew up in a polytheistic society that recognized only the worship of idols. The religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد) seeped into the society, and a group of them believed in Allah. The society denounced them for that and met them with severity, restriction, trial, and torment, forcing them to worship idols and reject the religion of monotheism. Whoever returned to their creed was left alone, and whoever persisted in opposition was killed in the most evil manner.

The youths were among those who believed in Allah with an insight-based faith (إيماناً على بصيرة), so Allah increased them in guidance upon their guidance and poured upon them knowledge and wisdom. He revealed to them, through the light He gave them, what concerned them of their affair and strengthened their hearts (ربط على قلوبهم), so they feared none but Allah, nor were they lonely because of the events and hardships that awaited them. They knew that if they continued to stay in their ignorant and domineering society, they would have no choice but to follow their path, thus not uttering the word of truth nor practicing the law of truth. They knew their path was to stand upon monotheism and the rejection of polytheism, then to withdraw from the people. They knew that if they withdrew and entered the cave, Allah would save them from the affliction they were in.

So they stood and said, in response to the people's proposal and dominance: رَبُّنا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ لَنْ نَدْعُوَا مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلهاً لَقَدْ قُلْنا إِذاً شَطَطاً هؤُلاءِ قَوْمُنَا اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً لَوْ لا يَأْتُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِسُلْطانٍ بَيِّنٍ فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً "Our Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth. We will never invoke any god besides Him, for then we would have said something egregious. These our people have taken gods besides Him. Why do they not bring a clear authority for them? So who is more unjust than he who fabricates a lie against Allah?" [18:14-15].

Then they said: وَإِذِ اعْتَزَلْتُمُوهُمْ وَما يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا اللهَ فَأْوُوا إِلَى الْكَهْفِ يَنْشُرْ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَيُهَيِّئْ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَمْرِكُمْ مِرْفَقاً "And when you have withdrawn from them and what they worship except Allah, then take refuge in the cave: your Lord will unfold His mercy for you, and He will help you on to ease in your affair" [18:16].

Then they entered the cave and settled in an open space (فجوة) within it, with their dog stretching its forelegs at the threshold (بالوصيد). They supplicated to their Lord for what they had previously intuited He would do for them, saying: "Our Lord, grant us mercy from Yourself and provide for us rectitude in our affair." So Allah struck their ears in the cave for years, and they remained in their cave—with their dog—for three hundred years and added nine more. You see the sun when it rises declining from their cave to the right, and when it sets passing them by on the left, while they are in an open space within it. You would suppose them awake, though they were asleep. Allah turns them to the right and to the left, while their dog stretches its forelegs at the threshold. Had you come upon them, you would have surely turned your back on them in flight, and you would have surely been filled with terror of them.

Then Allah raised them after this long age, which was three hundred and nine years from the day they entered the cave, to show them how He saved them from their people. They all awoke and found that the sun had changed its position, and there was still some lingering effect of their heavy sleep. One of them said: "How long have you remained?" A group of them said: "We have remained a day or part of a day," when they saw the change in the position of the rays, and they hesitated whether a night had passed over them or not.

Others among them said: "Rather, your Lord knows best how long you have remained." Then he said: "Send one of you with this silver of yours to the city, and let him see which of them has the purest food, and let him bring you a provision from it," for you are hungry. "And let the one of you who goes to the city be subtle in his journey to it and in buying the food, and let him not make anyone aware of you. Indeed, if they come to know of your place, they will stone you or force you back into their creed, and then you will never be felicitous."

This was the time for Allah (سبحانه) to make the people discover them, for the people from whom they had withdrawn and separated on the day they entered the cave had perished, and Allah had taken them away along with their kingdom and creed, and brought another people among whom the dominance was for the people of monotheism (أهل التوحيد) and authority. They—meaning the people of monotheism and others—had differed regarding the matter of the Resurrection (المعاد), so Allah (سبحانه) willed to show them a sign in that regard, so He caused them to discover the Companions of the Cave.

The one sent from the youths went out and came to the city, thinking it was the one he had left yesterday. But he found the city had changed in a way not known in a day or a lifetime; the people were not the people, and the conditions and circumstances were not what he had observed yesterday. He remained in a state of bewilderment until he wanted to buy food with the silver he had, which was the currency in circulation three centuries prior. A dispute arose over it, and it was not long before a wondrous matter was revealed: that the youth was among those who lived there three centuries before. He was one of the youths who were in a polytheistic and oppressive society, so they migrated from their homeland and withdrew from the people to preserve their faith, entered the cave, and Allah put them to sleep for this long age, then raised them. And here they are now in the cave, waiting for this one they sent to the city to buy them food to nourish themselves.

The news spread in the city instantly, and a large crowd of its people gathered and went to the cave with the youth sent from the Companions of the Cave. They witnessed what confirmed the youth's report regarding his companions, and the divine sign regarding the matter of the Resurrection became manifest to them.

The Companions of the Cave did not remain long after being raised before Allah (سبحانه) took their souls. At that point, those gathered at the door of the cave from the people of the city differed again. The polytheists among them said: "Build a structure over them; their Lord knows them best." Those who prevailed in their affair—and they were the monotheists—said: "We will surely take a mosque (مسجداً) over them."

3 - The Story Among Non-Muslims: Most people of report and history agree that the story occurred in the period between the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and Jesus (عليه السلام). Therefore, it is not mentioned in the books of the Two Testaments (العهدين) — the Bible — nor did the Jews concern themselves with it, even though several narrations state that Quraysh received the story from the Jews. Rather, the Christians were the ones who cared for it and concerned themselves with it in ancient and modern times. What is narrated from them regarding the story is close to what Ibn Ishaq reported in al-Ara'is from Ibn Abbas, except that their narrations differ from the Muslim narrations in several matters:

First: Syriac sources mention the number of the Companions of the Cave as eight, while Muslims, as well as Greek and Western sources, mention seven.

Second: Their story is devoid of any mention of the dog of the Companions of the Cave.

Third: They mentioned that the duration of the stay of the Companions of the Cave was two hundred years or less, while most Muslims state it was three hundred and nine years, as is the apparent meaning of the Noble Quran. The reason for their determination of that is their mention that the tyrant who forced people to worship idols and from whom the youths fled was the Emperor Decius (449–451) [sic], and the Companions of the Cave awoke, according to what they mentioned, in the year 435 CE, 437 CE, or 439 CE. Thus, there remains for their stay in the cave only two hundred years or less. The first of their historians to mention this, as recorded, was James of Sarug, the Syriac, who was born in 451 CE and died in 521 CE, and then others took it from him. There is a completion to this discussion that will follow.

4 - Where is the Cave of the Companions of the Cave? Several caves and caverns have been found in various parts of the earth, and on their walls are statues of three, five, or seven men with a dog, and in some, there is an offering before them. Upon seeing them, the stories of the Companions of the Cave come to mind. It is probable that these engravings and statues are an allusion to the story of the youths and that it spread and became famous after its occurrence in various regions, thus becoming a memorial for monks and those who secluded themselves for worship in these caves.

As for the cave to which the Companions of the Cave took refuge and hid, and where what happened to them occurred, people differ regarding it, and it has been claimed in several places.

First: The Cave of Ephesus. Ephesus (أفسوس) is an ancient ruined city located in Turkey, 73 kilometers from the town of Izmir. The cave is one kilometer or less from Ephesus, near the village of Ayasuluk at the foot of Mount Pion (ينايرداغ).

It is a spacious cave containing—as is said—hundreds of graves built of brick. It is at the foot of the mountain, and its door faces the northeast. There is no trace of a mosque, cell, or church near it. This cave is the most well-known among Christians and has been mentioned in several Muslim narrations.

This cave—despite its extreme fame—does not fit the characteristics mentioned in the Mighty Book.

Firstly: He, the Exalted, said: وَتَرَى الشَّمْسَ إِذا طَلَعَتْ تَزاوَرُ عن كَهْفِهِمْ ذاتَ الْيَمِينِ وَإِذا غَرَبَتْ تَقْرِضُهُمْ ذاتَ الشِّمالِ "And you see the sun when it rises declining from their cave to the right, and when it sets passing them by on the left" [18:17]. This is explicit that the sun's rays fall at sunrise on the right side of the cave and at sunset on the northern side of it. This necessitates that the cave's door faces south. The door of the cave in Ephesus faces northeast.

This matter—namely, the door of the Ephesus cave facing north and the reported characteristic of the sun hitting it at sunrise and sunset—is what led exegetes to consider the right and left of the cave in relation to one entering it, not one leaving it, even though the latter is what is commonly understood—as previously mentioned in the interpretation of the verse. Al-Baydawi said in his Tafsir: "The door of the cave is opposite the Great Bear (بنات النعش), and the closest sunrises and sunsets to its alignment are the sunrise and sunset of the head of Cancer. When the sun's orbit is its orbit, it rises slanting away from it, opposite its right side which is toward the west, and sets aligned with its left side. Thus, its rays fall on its side, dissipating its dampness and tempering its air, but do not fall upon them so as to harm their bodies or decay their clothes." End quote. Something similar was mentioned by others.

Furthermore, the door facing northeast, not the North Pole and the Great Bear as they mentioned, necessitates the non-applicability of the description even according to the consideration they adopted. For the sun's rays would then fall on the western side adjacent to the door at its rising, but at sunset, the door and its surroundings would be submerged in shadow, the rays having vanished shortly after the sun's meridian and the shadow having spread.

Unless it is claimed that what is meant by His saying: وَإِذا غَرَبَتْ تَقْرِضُهُمْ ذاتَ الشِّمالِ is the absence of the rays falling, or their falling behind them, not on their left.

Secondly: Because His saying, the Exalted: وَهُمْ فِي فَجْوَةٍ مِنْهُ "while they were in an open space within it" [18:17] means in a high part of it, and there is no high space (فجوة) in the Ephesus cave—as is said. This is based on fajwah (فجوة) meaning a high place, which is not certain; it was previously stated that it means a courtyard.

Thirdly: Because His saying, the Exalted: قالَ الَّذِينَ غَلَبُوا عَلى أَمْرِهِمْ لَنَتَّخِذَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ مَسْجِداً "Those who prevailed in their affair said, 'We will surely take a mosque over them'" [18:21] is apparent in that they built a mosque over the cave. There is no trace at the Ephesus cave of a mosque, cell, or the like. The closest thing there is a church at a distance of approximately three kilometers, with no connection to the cave at all.

Moreover, there is nothing there of an inscription (رقيم), writing, or any other matter that testifies, even partially, that some of those hundreds of graves are the graves of the Companions of the Cave, or that they remained there in a state of sleep for an age, then Allah raised them, then took their souls.

The Second Cave: The Cave of Rajib. This cave is located eight kilometers from the city of Amman, the capital of Jordan, near a village called Rajib. The cave is in a mountain, carved into the rock on its southern slope. Its sides from the eastern and western directions are open, and the sun's rays fall on it from them. The door of the cave faces south. Inside the cave is a small platform approximately three meters by two and a half meters on one side of the cave's floor, which measures approximately three by three. In the cavern are several graves in the form of Byzantine sarcophagi, numbering eight or seven.

On the walls are engravings and lines in ancient Greek and Thamudic, effaced and unreadable, as well as an image of a dog painted in red and other decorations and embellishments.

Above the cavern are the remains of a Byzantine cell, which coins and other discovered artifacts indicate was built during the time of Emperor Justin I (418–427). Other remains show that the cell was later converted, after the Muslims took control of the land, into an Islamic mosque containing a mihrab (محراب), a minaret (مأذنة), and a place for ablution (ميضاة). In the courtyard opposite the cave's door are the remains of another mosque built by Muslims in the early period of Islam, then renovated and constructed time after time. It is built upon the ruins of a Byzantine church, as is the mosque above the cave.

This cave—despite the people's interest in it and their care for its affair as revealed by the remains—was abandoned and forgotten, and with the passage of time became a ruin and collapsed debris, until the Jordanian Department of Antiquities recently (1) undertook excavation and digging in it and discovered it. Thus, it appeared again after being hidden for centuries.

Several archaeological signs and evidences have established its being the Cave of the Companions of the Cave mentioned in the Quran.

(1) This excavation and discovery occurred in the year 1963 CE, corresponding to 1342 SH. The person in charge, the virtuous archaeologist Rafiq Wafa al-Dajani, wrote a book titled Discovery of the Cave of the People of the Cave, published in 1964 CE. He details therein the efforts of the department and what he endured in research and excavation, describing the characteristics he found in this cave and the artifacts discovered that support this cave being the Cave of the Companions of the Cave mentioned in the Mighty Book. He mentions the applicability of the signs mentioned therein and other marks found there to this cave, rather than the cave in Ephesus, Damascus, Petra, or Scandinavia.

He considered it probable that the tyrant from whom the Companions of the Cave fled and entered the cave was Emperor Trajan (98–117 CE), not Emperor Decius (249–251 CE) mentioned by Christians and some Muslims, nor Emperor Diocletian (285–305 CE) mentioned by others in their narrations.

He argued that the righteous king in whose time Allah raised the Companions of the Cave was Emperor Theodosius (408–450 CE) by the consensus of Christian and Muslim historians. If we subtract the duration of the period mentioned by the Quran for the sleep of the People of the Cave, which is 309 years, from the middle of this righteous king's reign, which is 421, there remains 112 years, which coincides with the reign of Emperor Trajan. In this year, Trajan issued a decree stating that every Christian who refuses to worship the gods shall be tried as a traitor to the state and subjected to death.

In this way, the objection of some Christian historians like Gibbon in the book The Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire regarding the duration of the youths' stay—309 years mentioned in the Quran—is refuted, as he claimed it does not agree with what history has recorded and established: that they were raised in the time of the righteous king Theodosius (408–451 CE) and entered the cave in the time of Decius (249–251 CE), and the interval between the two reigns is two hundred years or less. This [archaeologist's] effort—may Allah reward his endeavor—is a sound argument, yet several matters are directed against it:

Among them: his subtraction of the 309 years mentioned in the Quran, which are lunar years apparently, whereas he should have considered them 300 years to be solar, then subtracted them from 430, the middle of the years of the righteous king's reign.

Among these points is that he mentioned a consensus (إجماع) among Muslim and Christian historians regarding the appearance of the youths' affair during the reign of Theodosius. However, no such consensus exists, as most of their narrations remain silent on naming this righteous king; only a few mention him by name. Perhaps they took this from Christian historians, or perhaps it was a conjecture (حدس) based on what is attributed to Jacob of Serugh (452–521 CE)—that he mentioned the story in a book he authored in 474 CE—leading them to identify the king as Theodosius. Furthermore, such a consensus is like their composite consensus (إجماعهم المركب) that their tyrant was either Decius or Decianus, which in any case negates him being Trajan.

Another point is his mention that archaeological evidence (البينات الأثرية) indicates the chapel atop the cave was built during the time of Justin I (518–527 CE). This would imply its construction occurred approximately one hundred years after the youths' affair became known. Yet, the apparent meaning of the Mighty Book is that its construction was contemporaneous with the people's discovery of them. Based on this, one would have to believe that its construction was a renovation (بناء مجدد) rather than the original structure built at the time of their appearance.

After all this, the identifying features (المشخصات) mentioned in the Noble Quran for the cave apply more clearly to the Cave of Rajib than to any other.

It has been mentioned in some Muslim narrations that the cave of the Companions of the Cave (أصحاب الكهف) is in Amman, as we previously indicated. Yaqut mentioned it in Mu'jam al-Buldan, stating that al-Raqim (الرقيم) is the name of a village near Amman where Yazid ibn Abd al-Malik had a palace, with another palace in a nearby village called al-Muwaqqar. The poet refers to both of them saying:

يزرن على تنانيه يزيدا بأكناف الموقر والرقيم

"They visit Yazid, despite his distance, In the regions of al-Muwaqqar and al-Raqim."

The city of Amman is also built on the site of the city of Philadelphia, which was among the most famous and beautiful cities of its era before the rise of the Islamic mission (الدعوة الإسلامية). It and its surrounding areas were under Roman control from the beginning of the second century CE until the Muslims conquered the Holy Land.

The truth is that the identifying features of the cave of the People of the Cave (أهل الكهف) apply more clearly to this cave than to any other.

The Third Cave: A cave at Mount Qasiyun near Salihiyah in Damascus, Syria, attributed to the Companions of the Cave.

The Fourth Cave: A cave in Petra, in the land of Palestine, which they attribute to the Companions of the Cave.

The Fifth Cave: A cave discovered—as it is said—in the Scandinavian Peninsula in Northern Europe, where they found seven undecayed corpses in Roman attire, thought to be the youths who were the Companions of the Cave.

Other caves are sometimes mentioned and attributed to the Companions of the Cave, such as a cave near the town of Nakhchivan in the Caucasus region, which the people of those parts believe to be the cave of the Companions of the Cave, and people used to journey to it and visit it.

However, there is no evidence (شاهد) to testify that any of these caves is the cave mentioned in the Noble Quran. Furthermore, historical sources refute the latter two, as the story is, in any case, a Roman one, and their authority—even in the days of their glory and sovereignty (سؤددهم)—never reached these regions of Northern Europe and the Caucasus.

Ayahs

وَٱتْلُ مَآ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْكَ مِن كِتَابِ رَبِّكَ ۖ لَا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ وَلَن تَجِدَ مِن دُونِهِۦ مُلْتَحَدًا (٢٧)
Recite what has been revealed to you from the Book of your Lord. Nothing can change His words, and you will never find any refuge besides Him. (27)
وَٱصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ مَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَدَوٰةِ وَٱلْعَشِىِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُۥ ۖ وَلَا تَعْدُ عَيْنَاكَ عَنْهُمْ تُرِيدُ زِينَةَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَا تُطِعْ مَنْ أَغْفَلْنَا قَلْبَهُۥ عَن ذِكْرِنَا وَٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ وَكَانَ أَمْرُهُۥ فُرُطًا (٢٨)
Content yourself with the company of those who supplicate their Lord morning and evening, desiring His Face, and do not lose sight of them, desiring the glitter of the life of this world. And do not obey him whose heart We have made oblivious to Our remembrance, and who follows his own desires, and whose conduct is [mere] profligacy. (28)
وَقُلِ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ فَمَن شَآءَ فَلْيُؤْمِن وَمَن شَآءَ فَلْيَكْفُرْ ۚ إِنَّآ أَعْتَدْنَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ نَارًا أَحَاطَ بِهِمْ سُرَادِقُهَا ۚ وَإِن يَسْتَغِيثُوا۟ يُغَاثُوا۟ بِمَآءٍ كَٱلْمُهْلِ يَشْوِى ٱلْوُجُوهَ ۚ بِئْسَ ٱلشَّرَابُ وَسَآءَتْ مُرْتَفَقًا (٢٩)
And say, ‘[This is] the truth from your Lord: let anyone who wishes believe it, and let anyone who wishes disbelieve it.’ Indeed, We have prepared for the wrongdoers a Fire whose curtains will surround them [on all sides]. If they cry out for help, they will be helped with a water like molten copper, which will scald their faces. What an evil drink, and how ill a resting place! (29)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ إِنَّا لَا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ مَنْ أَحْسَنَ عَمَلًا (٣٠)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds—indeed We do not waste the reward of those who are good in deeds. (30)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ يُحَلَّوْنَ فِيهَا مِنْ أَسَاوِرَ مِن ذَهَبٍ وَيَلْبَسُونَ ثِيَابًا خُضْرًا مِّن سُندُسٍ وَإِسْتَبْرَقٍ مُّتَّكِـِٔينَ فِيهَا عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ ۚ نِعْمَ ٱلثَّوَابُ وَحَسُنَتْ مُرْتَفَقًا (٣١)
For such there will be the gardens of Eden with streams running in them. They will be adorned therein with bracelets of gold and wear green garments of silk and brocade, reclining therein on couches. How excellent a reward, and how good a resting place! (31)

(Commentary)

This is a return and a transition back to the point where the discourse ended before the story—concerning the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه وآله وسلم reaching a state of grief and sorrow over their lack of faith in the Book revealed to him and their rejection of his true call. It then offers him consolation by explaining that this world is an abode of trial (البلاء) and examination (الامتحان), and that whatever is upon it is a mere adornment for it, which Allah will eventually turn into a barren mound (صعيداً جرزاً). Therefore, it is not fitting for him صلى‌الله‌عليه وآله وسلم to be distressed on their account if they do not respond to his call or believe in his Book.

Rather, his duty is to keep himself patient with those poor believers who constantly call upon their Lord, and not to turn his attention toward those affluent disbelievers who boast of what they possess of the adornment of the worldly life—which will soon become a barren mound. Instead, he should call them to their Lord and do no more than that; whoever among them wishes may believe, and whoever wishes may disbelieve, and he bears no responsibility for them. As for what they must face, whether they disbelieve or believe, it is not something for him to grieve or rejoice over; rather, it is the punishment or reward that Allah has prepared for both groups.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتْلُ ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ "And recite what has been revealed to you" until the end of the verse. In al-Majma‘, it is stated: "He leaned (لحد) toward him," and "leaning (التحد)" means "inclining." Thus, the place of refuge (الملتحد) is a noun of place derived from "inclining (الالتحاد)," meaning a place to which one inclines. The "Book of your Lord" refers to the Quran or the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), though the latter is more suited to His saying: لا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِماتِهِ "There is no changer of His words."

In the discourse—as you have just recognized—there is a return to what preceded the story. Accordingly, it is more appropriate that His saying: وَاتْلُ "And recite..." be a conjunction to His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْنا ما عَلَى الْأَرْضِ "Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth..." etc. The meaning is: Do not consume yourself with grief over their footsteps, but recite what has been revealed to you of the Book of your Lord, for there is no changer of His words—as they are established truths—and because you will find no place of refuge (ملتحداً) besides Him to which you may incline.

From this, it becomes clear that both His saying: لا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِماتِهِ "There is no changer of His words" and His saying: لَنْ تَجِدَ مِنْ دُونِهِ مُلْتَحَداً "you will never find any refuge besides Him" serve as justifications (التعليل). They are two proofs for the command in His saying: وَاتْلُ "And recite." Perhaps for this reason, the address in His saying: وَلَنْ تَجِدَ "and you will never find..." was specified to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه وآله وسلم, even though the ruling is general, as no one will ever find a refuge besides Him.

It is also possible that the meaning is: You shall not find a refuge besides Him because you are a Messenger, and there is no sanctuary for a Messenger, insofar as he is a Messenger, except with the One who sent him. Based on this, it is more appropriate that His saying: لا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِماتِهِ "There is no changer of His words" be a single proof whose import is: Recite to them these verses containing the divine command of delivery (التبليغ), for it is a divine word and His words do not change; and you are a Messenger who has no choice but to incline toward your Sender and fulfill His message. This meaning is supported by His saying in another place: قُلْ إِنِّي لَنْ يُجِيرَنِي مِنَ اللهِ أَحَدٌ وَلَنْ أَجِدَ مِنْ دُونِهِ مُلْتَحَداً إِلَّا بَلاغاً مِنَ اللهِ وَرِسالاتِهِ "Say, ‘Nothing can ever shelter me from Allah, nor can I ever find any refuge besides Him, except a delivery from Allah and His messages’" (72:22-23).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَالْعَشِيِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ "And content yourself with those who supplicate their Lord morning and evening, desiring His face" until the end of the verse. Al-Raghib said: Patience (الصبر) is to restrain oneself in a state of constriction. It is said: "I stabled (صبرت) the beast," meaning I confined it without fodder. And "I detained (صبرت) so-and-so," meaning I held him in a confinement from which there is no exit. Patience is the restraining of the soul (النفس) upon what reason (العقل) and the Law (الشرع) necessitate, or from what they necessitate its restraint. (End of the relevant portion).

The "face (الوجه)" of a thing is that by which it confronts and meets you. The linguistic origin of its meaning is the "face" as a physical limb. The "face" of the Exalted refers to His Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) and His Sublime Attributes (الصفات العليا) through which those who turn to Him do so, those who supplicate Him call upon Him, and those who worship Him serve Him. The Exalted said: وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى فَادْعُوهُ بِها "To Allah belong the Best Names, so supplicate Him by them" (7:180). As for the Transcendent Essence (الذات المتعالية), there is no path to It; rather, those who seek Him and desire Him do so because He is the God (إله), the Lord (رب), the High (علي), the Great (عظيم), the Possessor of Mercy (ذو رحمة) and Pleasure (رضوان), among other Names and Attributes.

The one who supplicates Allah, desiring His face, if he desires His active attributes (صفاته الفعلية)—such as His mercy, pleasure, bestowal, and grace—then he only desires that these should encompass and overwhelm him, so that he may be clothed in them in some manner, becoming one who is shown mercy, one with whom He is pleased, and one blessed by His favor. If he desires His non-active attributes—such as His knowledge, power, pride, and greatness—then he only desires to draw near to the Exalted through these sublime attributes. Or if you wish, say: he desires to place himself in a position necessitated by the divine attribute, such as standing in a position of lowliness and insignificance before His might, pride, and greatness, or standing in the position of the ignorant, helpless, and weak before His knowledge, power, and strength, and so on. Understand this well.

From this, the weakness in the statement of some becomes apparent: that "face" refers to "pleasure (الرضى)" and "pleasing obedience" metaphorically, because one who is pleased with a person turns toward him, while one who is angry turns away. Similarly, the statement of others: that "face" refers to the "Essence (الذات)" and the discourse involves a suppressed possessive noun. Likewise, the statement of others: that "face" refers to "turning (التوجه)," meaning they desire to turn toward Him and seek proximity to Him.

The meaning of their supplicating their Lord "morning and evening" is the continuity of supplication and its constant performance, because permanence is realized by the repetition of morning after evening and evening after morning in sensory perception; thus, the speech is used as a metonymy (الكناية). It has also been said: the supplication of morning and evening refers to the prayers at the two ends of the day, and it has been said: the daily obligatory prayers—but this is as you see (i.e., weak).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَعْدُ عَيْناكَ عَنْهُمْ تُرِيدُ زِينَةَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا "and do not let your eyes pass them by, desiring the glitter of the life of this world." The linguistic origin of passing by (العدو), as stated by al-Raghib, is "transgressing" or "exceeding," which is the meaning present in all its derivatives and usages. It is said in al-Qamus: "He passed (عدا) the matter and passed from (عنه) it," meaning he exceeded it and left it. Thus, the meaning of "do not let your eyes pass them by" is: do not exceed them nor let your eyes leave them while you desire the adornment of the worldly life.

However, some have mentioned that "exceeding (المجاوزة)" is only made transitive with the particle "from (عن)" when it carries the meaning of "pardoning." Therefore, al-Zamakhshari said in al-Kashshaf: His saying لا تَعْدُ عَيْناكَ عَنْهُمْ involves the word "pass (عدا)" implying the meaning of "turning away (نبا)" or "rising above (علا)," as in your saying: "his eye turned away from him" or "his eye rose above him" when he looks down upon him and does not fix his gaze. Were it not for this, it would have been necessary to say: "and let not your eyes pass them (ولا تعدهم عيناك)."

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تُطِعْ مَنْ أَغْفَلْنا قَلْبَهُ عَنْ ذِكْرِنا "and do not obey him whose heart We have made oblivious to Our remembrance." The meaning of making his heart oblivious is to allow heedlessness (الغفلة) to overcome it and to make him forget the remembrance of Allah, the Glorified, by way of requital (المجازاة). Since they were antagonistic toward the truth, Allah led them astray by making them oblivious to His remembrance. His speech regarding people in this state is similar to what will come at the end of the verses: إِنَّا جَعَلْنا عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَنْ يَفْقَهُوهُ وَفِي آذانِهِمْ وَقْراً وَإِنْ تَدْعُهُمْ إِلَى الْهُدى فَلَنْ يَهْتَدُوا إِذاً أَبَداً "Indeed We have cast veils over their hearts lest they should understand it, and a deafness into their ears. And if you invite them to guidance, they will never be guided" (18:57).

Thus, there is no room for the claim of those who say that the verse is among the proofs for His compulsion (الجبر) toward disbelief and disobedience. This is because compulsion as a requital (إلجاء مجازاة) does not negate free will (الاختيار); what negates it is initial compulsion (الإلجاء ابتداء), and the context of the verse is of the first type.

There is no need for forced interpretations, such as the claim of those who say that "We made his heart oblivious (أَغْفَلْنا قَلْبَهُ)" means "We exposed him to heedlessness," or that it means "We found him heedless," or "We attributed heedlessness to him," or that "making oblivious (الإغفال)" means making it "unmarked (غفلاً)"—having no mark (سمة) or sign (علامة)—meaning: We made his heart unmarked, not marking it with the mark of the hearts of the believers, so that the angels would not recognize it by that mark. All of these are as you see (i.e., far-fetched).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ وَكانَ أَمْرُهُ فُرُطاً "and who follows his desires, and whose affair is [gone] beyond bounds." It is stated in al-Majma‘: Excess (الفرط) is the transgression of the truth and the departure from it, from their saying: "he exceeded (أفرط)" when he is extravagant. Following desire and excess are among the effects of the heart's heedlessness; therefore, the conjunction of these two sentences to His saying: "We made oblivious (أَغْفَلْنا)" is like an explanatory conjunction (عطف التفسير).

His saying, the Exalted: وَقُلِ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ فَمَنْ شاءَ فَلْيُؤْمِنْ وَمَنْ شاءَ فَلْيَكْفُرْ "And say, ‘[This is] the truth from your Lord: let anyone who wishes believe, and let anyone who wishes disbelieve.’" This is conjoined to what His saying: وَاتْلُ ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ "And recite what has been revealed to you" and His saying: وَاصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ "And content yourself" were conjoined to. Thus, the context is an enumeration of the duties of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه وآله وسلم in the face of their disbelief in what was revealed to him and their persistence in it. The meaning is: Do not grieve over them, but recite what has been revealed to you, and keep yourself patient with these poor believers, and say to the disbelievers: "The truth is from your Lord," and do no more than that. Whoever among them wishes to believe, let him believe, and whoever among them wishes to disbelieve, let him disbelieve. Their faith does not benefit Us, nor does their disbelief harm Us; rather, whatever benefit, harm, reward, or consequence of punishment exists therein returns to themselves. So let them choose what they wish, for We have prepared for the wrongdoers such-and-such, and for the righteous among the believers such-and-such.

From here, it becomes clear that His saying: فَمَنْ شاءَ فَلْيُؤْمِنْ وَمَنْ شاءَ فَلْيَكْفُرْ "let anyone who wishes believe, and let anyone who wishes disbelieve" is from the speech of the Exalted addressing His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه وآله وسلم, and is not part of the statement he was commanded to say. Therefore, no weight should be given to what some have mentioned—that the sentence is part of the commanded speech.

It also becomes clear that the saying: إِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا لِلظَّالِمِينَ ناراً "Indeed We have prepared for the wrongdoers a Fire" etc., is in the position of justification (التعليل) for giving them the choice between faith and disbelief—which is a choice in form but a threat in meaning. The meaning is: We have only forbidden you from grieving and commanded you to suffice with delivery alone, and to be content with saying: "The truth is from your Lord" only, and We did not resort to persistence or urging because We have prepared for them the consequences of this call, whether rejected or accepted. What We have prepared is sufficient as an incentive and a deterrent, and there is no need for more than that; it is for them to choose for themselves whichever of the two stations they wish.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا لِلظَّالِمِينَ ناراً "Indeed We have prepared for the wrongdoers a Fire" until the end of the verse. It is stated in al-Majma‘: The pavilion (السرادق) is the tent (الفسطاط) that encompasses what is within it; it is also said that the suradiq is a cloth draped around the tent. He said: Molten metal (المهل) is the dregs of oil; it is also said to be molten copper. He said: The resting place (المرتفق) is the place of reclining, from the elbow (المرفق); it is said "he reclined (ارتفق)" when he leaned on his elbow. (End of quote). To scald (الشي) is to cook thoroughly; it is said "he scalded (شوى)" when it is cooked.

In the substitution of "disbelief" with "wrongdoing (الظلم)" in His saying: إِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا لِلظَّالِمِينَ "Indeed We have prepared for the wrongdoers"—rather than saying "for the disbelievers"—there is an indication that the mentioned consequence is for the wrongdoers insofar as they are wrongdoers. He has defined them in His saying: الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَيَبْغُونَها عِوَجاً وَهُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ كافِرُونَ "those who bar [others] from the way of Allah, and seek to make it crooked, and disbelieve in the Hereafter" (7:45). The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ إِنَّا لا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ مَنْ أَحْسَنَ عَمَلاً "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds—indeed We do not let the reward of those who are upright in conduct go to waste." This is an explanation of the reward for the believers for their faith and righteous deeds. He said: "Indeed We do not let... go to waste" rather than saying "We have prepared for these such-and-such" to indicate His special care for them and His gratitude toward them.

His saying: إِنَّا لا نُضِيعُ "Indeed We do not let... go to waste" is in the position of the predicate (خبر) for "Indeed (إن)." It is, in reality, the placement of the cause (السبب) in the place of the effect (المسبب). The intended meaning is: Indeed, those who believe and do righteous deeds, We shall fulfill their reward, for they are doers of good (محسنون), and We do not let the reward of those who are upright in conduct go to waste.

Since the verse counts punishment as an effect of wrongdoing (الظلم) and then counts reward in contrast as a recompense for faith and righteous deeds, we derive from it that there is no reward for faith stripped of righteous deeds; rather, the verse perhaps intimates that such a state is a form of wrongdoing.

His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكا لَهُمْ جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهِمُ الْأَنْهارُ "For such there will be gardens of Eden with streams running at their feet" until the end of the verse. Eden (العدن) is "residence," and the "Gardens of Eden" are gardens of permanent residence. Bracelets (الأساور) is said to be the plural of aswirah, which is the plural of siwar (with a kasrah on the sin), which is an ornament for the wrist. Al-Raghib mentioned that it is an Arabized Persian word, originally dastwarah. Silk (السندس) is fine brocade, and brocade (الإستبرق) is thick brocade. Couches (الأرائك) is the plural of arikah, which is a bed. The meaning of the verse is clear.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated via Juwaybir from al-Dahhak from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: وَلا تُطِعْ مَنْ أَغْفَلْنا قَلْبَهُ عَنْ ذِكْرِنا "and do not obey him whose heart We have made oblivious to Our remembrance." He said: "It was revealed concerning Umayyah ibn Khalaf—this was because he called the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه وآله وسلم to a matter that Allah disliked: the expulsion of the poor from his presence and the drawing near of the chieftains of the people of Mecca. So Allah revealed: وَلا تُطِعْ مَنْ أَغْفَلْنا قَلْبَهُ 'and do not obey him whose heart We have made oblivious'—meaning: upon whose heart We have set a seal—عَنْ ذِكْرِنا 'to Our remembrance'—meaning: monotheism (التوحيد)—وَاتَّبَعَ هَواهُ 'and who follows his desires'—meaning: polytheism (الشرك)—وَكانَ أَمْرُهُ فُرُطاً 'and whose affair is [gone] beyond bounds'—meaning: excess in the matter of Allah and ignorance of Allah."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu‘aym in al-Hilyah, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu‘ab al-Iman narrated from Salman who said: "Those whose hearts were to be reconciled (المؤلفة قلوبهم)—‘Uyaynah ibn Badr and al-Aqra‘ ibn Habis—came to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه وآله وسلم and said: ‘O Messenger of Allah, if only you would sit at the head of the assembly and hide yourself from these people and the smell of their cloaks’—meaning Salman, Abu Dharr, and the poor Muslims, who were wearing wool cloaks—‘we would sit with you, converse with you, and learn from you.’ So Allah revealed: وَاتْلُ ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ كِتابِ رَبِّكَ 'And recite what has been revealed to you of the Book of your Lord' until His saying: أَعْتَدْنا لِلظَّالِمِينَ ناراً 'We have prepared for the wrongdoers a Fire,' threatening them with the Fire."

I say: Al-Qummi narrated similarly in his Tafsir, but he mentioned only ‘Uyaynah ibn al-Husayn ibn al-Hudhayfah ibn Badr al-Fazari. The implication of this narration is that the two verses are Medinan, and there are other narrations containing a similar story; however, the context of the verses does not support this.

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah عليهما السلام regarding His saying: وَاصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَالْعَشِيِ "And content yourself with those who supplicate their Lord morning and evening." They both said: "He only meant by it the prayer (الصلاة)."

In the same source, from ‘Asim al-Kuzi from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه السلام, who said: "I heard him saying regarding the word of Allah: فَمَنْ شاءَ فَلْيُؤْمِنْ وَمَنْ شاءَ فَلْيَكْفُرْ 'let anyone who wishes believe, and let anyone who wishes disbelieve'—he said: ‘It is a threat (وعيد).’"

In al-Kafi, Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, and others, from Abu Hamzah from Abu Ja‘far عليه السلام regarding His saying: وَقُلِ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ فَمَنْ شاءَ فَلْيُؤْمِنْ وَمَنْ شاءَ فَلْيَكْفُرْ "And say, ‘[This is] the truth from your Lord: let anyone who wishes believe, and let anyone who wishes disbelieve’"—[he said it was revealed] concerning the guardianship (ولاية) of ‘Ali عليه السلام.

I say: This is of the nature of "application (الجري)"—the interpretive principle of applying a verse to its most prominent instances.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Tirmidhi, Abu Ya‘la, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Hibban, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Shu‘ab narrated from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه وآله وسلم regarding His saying: بِماءٍ كَالْمُهْلِ "with water like molten metal." He said: "Like the dregs of oil; when it is brought near to him, the skin of his face falls into it."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying: بِماءٍ كَالْمُهْلِ "with water like molten metal," he said: He عليه السلام said: "The muhl is that which remains at the bottom of the oil."

In Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi, from ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه السلام, who said: "The son of Adam was created hollow; he must have food and drink. The Exalted said: وَإِنْ يَسْتَغِيثُوا يُغاثُوا بِماءٍ كَالْمُهْلِ يَشْوِي الْوُجُوهَ 'and if they plead for help, they will be helped with water like molten metal which scalds the faces.'"

Ayahs

۞ وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلًا رَّجُلَيْنِ جَعَلْنَا لِأَحَدِهِمَا جَنَّتَيْنِ مِنْ أَعْنَـٰبٍ وَحَفَفْنَـٰهُمَا بِنَخْلٍ وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُمَا زَرْعًا (٣٢)
Draw for them the parable of two men for each of whom We had made two gardens of vines, and We had surrounded them with date palms, and placed crops between them. (32)
كِلْتَا ٱلْجَنَّتَيْنِ ءَاتَتْ أُكُلَهَا وَلَمْ تَظْلِم مِّنْهُ شَيْـًٔا ۚ وَفَجَّرْنَا خِلَـٰلَهُمَا نَهَرًا (٣٣)
Both gardens yielded their produce without stinting anything of it. And We had set a stream gushing through them. (33)
وَكَانَ لَهُۥ ثَمَرٌ فَقَالَ لِصَـٰحِبِهِۦ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُۥٓ أَنَا۠ أَكْثَرُ مِنكَ مَالًا وَأَعَزُّ نَفَرًا (٣٤)
He had abundant fruits, so he said to his companion, as he conversed with him: ‘I have more wealth than you, and am stronger with respect to numbers.’ (34)
وَدَخَلَ جَنَّتَهُۥ وَهُوَ ظَالِمٌ لِّنَفْسِهِۦ قَالَ مَآ أَظُنُّ أَن تَبِيدَ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أَبَدًا (٣٥)
He entered his garden while he wronged himself. He said, ‘I do not think that this will ever perish, (35)
وَمَآ أَظُنُّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ قَآئِمَةً وَلَئِن رُّدِدتُّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّى لَأَجِدَنَّ خَيْرًا مِّنْهَا مُنقَلَبًا (٣٦)
and I do not think that the Hour will ever set in. And even if I am returned to my Lord I will surely find a resort better than this.’ (36)
قَالَ لَهُۥ صَاحِبُهُۥ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُۥٓ أَكَفَرْتَ بِٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكَ مِن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ سَوَّىٰكَ رَجُلًا (٣٧)
His companion said to him, as he conversed with him: ‘Do you disbelieve in Him who created you from dust, then from a drop of [seminal] fluid, then fashioned you as a man? (37)
لَّـٰكِنَّا۠ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ رَبِّى وَلَآ أُشْرِكُ بِرَبِّىٓ أَحَدًا (٣٨)
But I [say], ‘‘He is Allah, my Lord,’’ and I do not ascribe any partner to my Lord. (38)
وَلَوْلَآ إِذْ دَخَلْتَ جَنَّتَكَ قُلْتَ مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِٱللَّهِ ۚ إِن تَرَنِ أَنَا۠ أَقَلَّ مِنكَ مَالًا وَوَلَدًا (٣٩)
Why did you not say, when you entered your garden, ‘‘[This is] as Allah has willed! There is no power except by Allah!’’ If you see that I have lesser wealth than you and children, (39)
فَعَسَىٰ رَبِّىٓ أَن يُؤْتِيَنِ خَيْرًا مِّن جَنَّتِكَ وَيُرْسِلَ عَلَيْهَا حُسْبَانًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَتُصْبِحَ صَعِيدًا زَلَقًا (٤٠)
maybe my Lord will give me [something] better than your garden, and He will unleash upon it bolts from the sky, so that it becomes a bare plain. (40)
أَوْ يُصْبِحَ مَآؤُهَا غَوْرًا فَلَن تَسْتَطِيعَ لَهُۥ طَلَبًا (٤١)
Or its water will sink down, so that you will never be able to obtain it.’ (41)
وَأُحِيطَ بِثَمَرِهِۦ فَأَصْبَحَ يُقَلِّبُ كَفَّيْهِ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَنفَقَ فِيهَا وَهِىَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا وَيَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى لَمْ أُشْرِكْ بِرَبِّىٓ أَحَدًا (٤٢)
And ruin closed in on his produce, and he began to wring his hands for what he had spent on it, as it lay fallen on its trellises. He was saying, ‘I wish I had not ascribed any partner to my Lord.’ (42)
وَلَمْ تَكُن لَّهُۥ فِئَةٌ يَنصُرُونَهُۥ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا كَانَ مُنتَصِرًا (٤٣)
He had no party to help him, besides Allah, nor could he help himself. (43)
هُنَالِكَ ٱلْوَلَـٰيَةُ لِلَّهِ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ هُوَ خَيْرٌ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ عُقْبًا (٤٤)
There, all authority belongs to Allah, the Real. He is best in rewarding, and best in requiting. (44)
وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا كَمَآءٍ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَٱخْتَلَطَ بِهِۦ نَبَاتُ ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَصْبَحَ هَشِيمًا تَذْرُوهُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحُ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّقْتَدِرًا (٤٥)
Draw for them the parable of the life of this world: [It is] like the water We send down from the sky. Then the earth’s vegetation mingles with it. Then it becomes chaff, scattered by the wind. And Allah has power over all things. (45)
ٱلْمَالُ وَٱلْبَنُونَ زِينَةُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَٱلْبَـٰقِيَـٰتُ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتُ خَيْرٌ عِندَ رَبِّكَ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ أَمَلًا (٤٦)
Wealth and children are an adornment of the life of the world, but lasting righteous deeds are better with your Lord in reward and better in hope. (46)

(Commentary)

The verses contain two parables illustrating the reality of what man possesses in his worldly life of wealth and children. These are the ornaments and glitters of life—deceptive, swiftly vanishing, and fleeting—with which life beautifies itself for man, distracting him from the remembrance of his Lord and drawing his imagination toward eternalizing himself through them and relying upon them. Thus, he imagines that he owns them and has power over them, until a visitation from Allah, the Glorified, encompasses them, and they perish and vanish. Nothing remains for man from them except what resembles the dream of a sleeper or a false wish.

The verses return the discourse to clarifying the reality that He, the Glorified, alluded to in His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا مَا عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَهَا (Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it...) until His saying: صَعِيدًا جُرُزًا (...a barren plain) [Qur'an 18:7-8].

His saying, the Exalted: وَاضْرِبْ لَهُمْ مَثَلًا رَجُلَيْنِ جَعَلْنَا لِأَحَدِهِمَا جَنَّتَيْنِ مِنْ أَعْنَابٍ — And draw for them the parable of two men: for one of them We made two gardens of vines... [Qur'an 18:32]. That is, strike for these people who are infatuated with the glitter of the worldly life and turning away from the remembrance of Allah a parable, so it may become clear to them that they have attached themselves to nothing but an illusory mirage with no reality.

Some commentators have mentioned that what the parable contains is a hypothetical, assumed story, and it is not necessary for the content of the parable to have occurred externally. Others have stated that it is a factual story, and they have narrated many differing accounts regarding it which cannot be relied upon. However, contemplating the context of the story—including the mention of them being specifically two gardens, the restriction of their trees to vines and palms, the occurrence of crops between them, and other details—supports its being a factual story.

His saying: جَنَّتَيْنِ مِنْ أَعْنَابٍ (two gardens of vines) means of grapevines, for the fruit is often used to refer to its tree. His saying: وَحَفَفْنَاهُمَا بِنَخْلٍ (and We surrounded them with date palms) means We made the palms encompass them, bordering them from all sides. His saying: وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُمَا زَرْعًا (and We placed crops between them) means between the two gardens and in their midst. By this, the cultivation was continuous and complete, and both staples and fruits were gathered for him.

His saying, the Exalted: كِلْتَا الْجَنَّتَيْنِ آتَتْ أُكُلَهَا — Both gardens yielded their produce... [Qur'an 18:33]. The term ukul (أُكُل) refers to that which is eaten, and the meaning of the gardens yielding their produce is the fruiting of their trees, both vines and palms.

His saying: وَلَمْ تَظْلِمْ مِنْهُ شَيْئًا (and did not stint aught of it). Injustice (الظلم) here means deficiency (النقص). The pronoun refers to the produce (ukul); that is, the gardens did not diminish anything of their yield, but rather produced to the full extent of their capacity. His saying: وَفَجَّرْنَا خِلَالَهُمَا نَهَرًا (and We made a river gush through them) means We cleaved a river of water through their midst to irrigate them and fulfill their need for drink by the easiest means without any burden.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَانَ لَهُ ثَمَرٌ — He had [other] fruit... [Qur'an 18:34]. The pronoun refers to the man, and thamar (ثَمَر) refers to types of wealth, as stated in al-Sihah and narrated from al-Qamus. It has also been said that the pronoun refers to the palms and the thamar is their fruit. It was also said that it means the man had wealth he owned outside of his garden. The first of these views is the most appropriate, followed by the second. It is also possible that the meaning of the two gardens yielding their produce without stinting refers to their trees reaching the stage and season of fruiting, while His saying: وَكَانَ لَهُ ثَمَرٌ refers to the actual presence of fruit on their trees, as in summer. This is a view free from affectation.

His saying, the Exalted: فَقَالَ لِصَاحِبِهِ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُ أَنَا أَكْثَرُ مِنْكَ مَالًا وَأَعَزُّ نَفَرًا — ...so he said to his companion while he was conversing with him, ‘I am wealthier than you and have a stronger retinue.’ [Qur'an 18:34]. Conversation (المحاورة) is addressing and responding in speech. The retinue (النفر) refers to individuals who accompany a person with a certain degree of closeness; they are called nafar because they go forth (yanfirun) with him. For this reason, some have interpreted it as servants and children, and others as a group or kinsfolk. The first is more in line with what Allah the Exalted will narrate of his companion's response: إِنْ تَرَنِ أَنَا أَقَلَّ مِنْكَ مَالًا وَوَلَدًا (If you see me as less than you in wealth and children), where he substituted "children" for "retinue." The meaning is that the one for whom We made the two gardens said to his companion while he was conversing with him: "I am wealthier than you and have a stronger retinue," meaning children and servants.

This which he said to his companion reflects a specific delusion he held, deviated from the truth. He looked at himself while having absolute disposal over what Allah had granted him of wealth and children, facing no interference in what he desired therein. Thus, he believed he was their owner. This was true in a sense, but he forgot that Allah, the Glorified, is the One who made him owner and is the true Owner of what He granted him. He forgot that these were things Allah had subjected to him and given him power over from the ornaments of the worldly life, which is a trial (فتنة) and a test (بلاء) by which man is examined so that Allah may distinguish the corrupt from the good. Rather, the ornament attracted his soul to itself, so he reckoned himself independent of his Lord, self-sufficient in what he possessed, and believed that all causal influence lay with the apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) that were subjected to him.

Thus, he forgot Allah, the Glorified, and relied upon the causes; this is polytheism (الشرك). Then he turned to himself and saw that he was disposing of the causes, dominating them, so he thought this was due to his own nobility and was seized by pride, becoming arrogant toward his companion. To this refers the difference between the two descriptions: the description of the Exalted regarding his ownership when He said: جَعَلْنَا لِأَحَدِهِمَا جَنَّتَيْنِ (We made for one of them two gardens...) and He did not say "one of them had two gardens," and the man's description of himself when he said to his companion: "I am wealthier than you and have a stronger retinue." He saw only himself and forgot that his Lord was the One who gave him power over the wealth he had and honored him with the retinue he possessed. Thus, his saying to his companion, "I am wealthier than you and have a stronger retinue," follows the same course as Qarun's saying to those who advised him not to exult and to do good with what Allah had given him of wealth: إِنَّمَا أُوتِيتُهُ عَلَى عِلْمٍ عِنْدِي (I have indeed been given this because of the knowledge I have) [Qur'an 28:78].

This which is revealed by his saying, "I am wealthier than you," namely the claim of personal nobility and inherent entitlement, followed by polytheism through heedlessness of Allah and reliance upon apparent causes, is what he manifested when he entered his garden and said, as narrated by Allah: مَا أَظُنُّ أَنْ تَبِيدَ هَذِهِ أَبَدًا وَمَا أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قَائِمَةً (I do not think that this will ever perish, and I do not think that the Hour will ever set in...) [Qur'an 18:35-36].

His saying, the Exalted: وَدَخَلَ جَنَّتَهُ وَهُوَ ظَالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ قَالَ — He entered his garden while he was wrongdoing himself. He said... [Qur'an 18:35] until the end of the two verses. The four pronouns refer to the man. The word "garden" (الجنة) is used in the singular to refer to the genus, which is why it was not dualized. It has also been said that this is because entry cannot be realized in both gardens simultaneously; rather, it occurs in one after the other.

It is said in al-Kashshaf: "If you ask: Why did He singularize 'garden' after dualizing it? I say: Its meaning is that he entered that which was his garden—meaning he has no other garden besides it. That is, he has no portion in the Garden promised to the believers, so what he possessed in the world was his garden and nothing else; he did not intend the two gardens specifically nor just one of them." This is a subtle point.

His saying: وَهُوَ ظَالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ (while he was wrongdoing himself)—he was a wrongdoer because he was arrogant toward his companion when he said, "I am wealthier than you," which reveals his self-conceit and his polytheism through forgetting Allah and relying on apparent causes. All of these are destructive vices.

His saying: قَالَ مَا أَظُنُّ أَنْ تَبِيدَ هَذِهِ أَبَدًا (He said, ‘I do not think that this will ever perish’). Perishing (البيد والبيدودة) refers to destruction and annihilation. The demonstrative "this" refers to the garden. The sentence is detached because it is in the sense of a response to a hypothetical question; as if when it was said, "He entered his garden," it was asked, "What did he do?" and it was replied, "He said: I do not think that this will ever perish..."

He expressed the permanence of his garden by saying, "I do not think it will perish," and the negation of "thinking" (الظن) regarding a matter is an allusion to it being a supposition or possibility that is not even considered or inclined toward. Thus, the meaning of "I do not think this will perish" is that its permanence and duration are matters the soul is so tranquil about and certain of that it does not even hesitate or think about its perishing or suppose it will vanish.

This is the state of man: his soul does not attach itself to a vanishing thing insofar as it is changing and quickly passing; rather, the heart attaches itself to what it perceives in it of the mark of permanence (سمة البقاء), however it may be. Thus, he is drawn to it and does not turn away toward any possibility of its annihilation. You see him, when the world approaches him, becoming tranquil toward it, beginning to enjoy its ornaments, devoting himself to it, while his whims encompass him and his hopes lengthen, as if he does not see any end for himself, nor any vanishing for the blessing in his hand, nor any severance for the causes that have aided him. And you see him, when the world turns away from him, seized by despair and hopelessness, making him forget every hope for relief, and concluding that his misery and ill state will endure forever.

The reason for all this is what Allah has placed in his primordial nature (الفطرة) of attachment to this vanishing ornament as a trial and test. When he turns away from the remembrance of his Lord, he becomes cut off, sufficing with himself, the worldly ornaments before him, and the apparent causes surrounding him. He attaches himself to the present state he witnesses, and the attraction of ornaments and glitters calls him to freeze upon them and not pay attention to their vanishing—which is the claim of permanence. Whenever the strike of the primordial intellect (العقل الفطري) knocks at him—warning that time will betray him, causes will fail him, the goods of life will bid him farewell, and his deferred life will reach its term—the following of whims and long hopes prevent him from listening or paying heed.
