This is the condition of the people of the world: they remain in a contradiction of opinion, doing what they believe with their whims while denying it with their intellects. However, they find tranquility in the opinion of whim, which prevents them from paying heed to the judgment of the intellect.

This is the meaning of their belief in the duration of apparent causes and the permanence of the ornaments of worldly life. For this reason, he said in what Allah narrated: مَا أَظُنُّ أَنْ تَبِيدَ هَذِهِ أَبَدًا (I do not think that this will ever perish) and he did not say, "This will never perish."

His saying: وَمَا أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قَائِمَةً (and I do not think that the Hour will ever set in) is based on what preceded regarding eternalization in his saying, "I do not think that this will ever perish." For this gives rise to the deemed impossibility (استبعاد) of the current state changing by the setting in of the Hour. All that Allah, the Glorified, has narrated of the arguments of the polytheists in denying the Resurrection is based on this deemed impossibility, such as their saying: مَنْ يُحْيِي الْعِظَامَ وَهِيَ رَمِيمٌ (Who will revive the bones when they have decayed?) [Qur'an 36:78] and their saying: أَإِذَا ضَلَلْنَا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ (When we have been lost in the earth, shall we indeed be created anew?) [Qur'an 32:10].

His saying: وَلَئِنْ رُدِدْتُ إِلَى رَبِّي لَأَجِدَنَّ خَيْرًا مِنْهَا مُنْقَلَبًا (and even if I am returned to my Lord, I will surely find a resort better than this) [Qur'an 18:36] is based on what preceded of the claim of personal nobility and entitlement to good. This produces in man a false hope for every good and happiness without any deed that necessitates it. He says: It is unlikely that the Hour will set in, but if it does and I am returned to my Lord, I will surely find—by my own nobility (and he does not say "my Lord will give me")—a resort better than this garden to which I shall return.

This speaker's soul deceived him in the nobility it claimed, until he swore by what he said, as indicated by the lam of the oath in His saying: وَلَئِنْ رُدِدْتُ (and even if I am returned) and the lam of emphasis and the nun in His saying: لَأَجِدَنَّ (I will surely find). He said: رُدِدْتُ (I am returned) and did not say "my Lord returns me to Him," and he said: لَأَجِدَنَّ (I will surely find) and did not say "Allah gave me."

The two verses are like His saying, the Exalted: وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَاهُ رَحْمَةً مِنَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ هَذَا لِي وَمَا أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قَائِمَةً وَلَئِنْ رُجِعْتُ إِلَى رَبِّي إِنَّ لِي عِنْدَهُ لَلْحُسْنَى (And if We let him taste a mercy from Us after some adversity that has visited him, he will surely say, ‘This is my due! I do not think that the Hour will ever set in. And even if I am returned to my Lord, I will indeed have the best [reward] with Him.’) [Qur'an 41:50].

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ لَهُ صَاحِبُهُ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُ أَكَفَرْتَ بِالَّذِي خَلَقَكَ مِنْ تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ سَوَّاكَ رَجُلًا — His companion said to him, as he was conversing with him, ‘Do you disbelieve in Him who created you from dust, then from a drop of fluid, then fashioned you as a man?’ [Qur'an 18:37]. This verse and the three following it are a rebuttal from the man's companion, refuting his saying: "I am wealthier than you and have a stronger retinue," and then his saying when he entered his garden: "I do not think that this will ever perish." The speech, in terms of the speaker's intent, has been analyzed into two aspects: First, his exaltation over Allah, the Glorified, by claiming independence in himself and in what he possesses of wealth and retinue, and his self-aggrandizement through the power and strength he has. Second, his exaltation over his companion and his belittling of him for his lack of wealth and lowliness. He then refuted each of the two claims by severing their substance and uprooting them. Thus, his saying: أَكَفَرْتَ بِالَّذِي خَلَقَكَ (Do you disbelieve in Him who created you...) until His saying: إِلَّا بِاللَّهِ (...except by Allah) is a rebuttal of the first claim, and his saying: إِنْ تَرَنِ أَنَا أَقَلَّ (If you see me as less...) until His saying: طَلَبًا (...to seek it) is a rebuttal of the second.

His saying: قَالَ لَهُ صَاحِبُهُ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُ (His companion said to him, as he was conversing with him)—the repetition of the phrase "as he was conversing with him" indicates that he did not deviate from his state of tranquil faith and dignity upon hearing what he heard from the man. Rather, he continued his conversation while maintaining its etiquette. Part of this etiquette was his gentleness with him in speech and not being harsh by mentioning what would be considered a prayer against him that would normally upset him. Thus, he did not mention his children with any ill, as he mentioned his garden; rather, he sufficed with what was alluded to in his mention of the garden—the possibility of it becoming a slippery plain and its water sinking away.

His saying: أَكَفَرْتَ بِالَّذِي خَلَقَكَ (Do you disbelieve in Him who created you...)—the interrogation is for denunciation (الاستفهام للإنكار). He denounces what his speech contained of polytheism toward Allah, the Glorified, by claiming independence for himself and for causes and effects, as previously indicated. Among the branches of his polytheism was his deeming the setting in of the Hour as impossible and his hesitation regarding it.

As for what was mentioned in al-Kashshaf—that he deemed him a disbeliever in Allah and a denier of His blessings because of his doubt in the Resurrection, just as one who belies the Messenger is a disbeliever—this is not sound. How could it be so when he mentions in his rectification the negation of polytheism from himself? If it were as he said, he would have mentioned faith in the Return (المعاد).

If you say: The verses are explicit regarding the man's polytheism, and the polytheists deny the Return. I say: The man was not among the polytheists in the sense of idol-worshippers. He acknowledged during his speech what the principles of paganism do not allow; for he referred to Him, the Glorified, by saying: رَبِّي (my Lord), whereas the pagans do not see Him as a Lord for man or a worshipped deity; rather, He is to them the Lord of lords and God of gods. Nor did he deny the Return from its root, as previously indicated; rather, he hesitated and deemed it unlikely by turning away from reflecting upon it. Had he denied it, he would have said "If I were returned" and not "And even if (وَلَئِنْ) I am returned to my Lord."

Thus, the evil consequence mentioned for his affair in the verse is only due to his polytheism in the sense of forgetting his Lord and claiming independence for himself and for apparent causes. In this is the removal of Him, the Exalted, from Lordship (الربوبية) and the handing over of the reins of sovereignty and governance to other than Him. This is the root of corruption upon which every corrupt branch grows, whether he acknowledges monotheism (التوحيد) with his tongue or denies it and affirms other deities. Al-Zamakhshari said regarding His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ مَا أَظُنُّ أَنْ تَبِيدَ هَذَا أَبَدًا (He said, ‘I do not think that this will ever perish’), and how excellent is what he said: "You see most of the wealthy among the Muslims, even if they do not utter such things with their tongues, the tongues of their states speak it and cry out with it."

This believer invalidated his companion's claim by saying: أَكَفَرْتَ بِالَّذِي خَلَقَكَ مِنْ تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ سَوَّاكَ رَجُلًا (Do you disbelieve in Him who created you from dust, then from a drop of fluid, then fashioned you as a man?) by drawing his attention to his origin, which is dust, then the drop of fluid. For that is the origin of man; whatever is added to that until man becomes a complete human being with attributes and effects is a pure gift from Allah. His origin possesses nothing of that, nor do any of the other apparent cosmic causes; for they are like man—they possess nothing of themselves or their own effects except by a gift from Allah, the Glorified.

So, what man possesses as a fashioned man—of humanity and its effects such as knowledge, life, power, and governance by which he subjects cosmic causes to reach his goals and needs—all of that is purely owned by Allah. He gave it to man and made him its owner, yet man did not thereby exit the ownership of Allah, nor was he cut off from Him. Rather, man was clothed with what he was clothed with from them, so they were attributed to him by His will. Had He not willed, man would not have owned any of that. Thus, man has no right to be independent of Him, the Exalted, in anything of himself or his effects, nor does any of the cosmic causes have that right.

He says: You are that dust, then that semen, which possessed nothing of humanity, manhood, or their effects. Allah, the Glorified, is the One who gave them to you by His will and made you their owner, and He is the true Owner of what He gave you. So why do you disbelieve in Him and conceal His Lordship? And where do you stand in relation to independence?

His saying, the Exalted: لَكِنَّا هُوَ اللَّهُ رَبِّي وَلَا أُشْرِكُ بِرَبِّي أَحَدًا — But as for me, He is Allah, my Lord, and I do not associate anyone with my Lord. [Qur'an 18:38]. The famous recitation is lakinna (لَكِنَّا) with a short a on the doubled nun in continuous speech and its preservation in pause. Its origin, according to what they mentioned, is lakin ana (لكن أنا). The hamza was deleted after its vowel was transferred to the nun, and the nun was elided into the nun. Thus, the connection is with a doubled nun with a fatha without an alif, and the pause is with the alif, as in the first-person pronoun ana (أنا).

The word رَبِّي (my Lord) is repeated in the verse, the second being the placement of a manifest noun in place of a pronoun. The context would normally require "and I do not associate anyone with Him." This is to indicate the reason for the judgment by linking it to the attribute, as if he said: "and I do not associate anyone with Him because He is my Lord, and it is not permissible to associate anyone with Him due to His Lordship." This is a clarification of the believer's state in contrast to what the disbeliever claimed for himself, and the meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْلَا إِذْ دَخَلْتَ جَنَّتَكَ قُلْتَ مَا شَاءَ اللَّهُ لَا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِاللَّهِ — Why did you not say, when you entered your garden, ‘[This is] as Allah has wished! There is no power except by Allah!’? [Qur'an 18:39]. This is a continuation of the believer's speech to his disbelieving companion. It is an exhortation and a rebuke to his companion for saying when he entered his garden: "I do not think that this will ever perish." He should have replaced it with his saying: "As Allah has wished! There is no power except by Allah!" thus attributing the entire matter to the will of Allah and restricting power to Him, based on what he had clarified to him—that every blessing is by the will of Allah and there is no power except by Him.

His saying: مَا شَاءَ اللَّهُ (As Allah has wished) is either on the assumption of: "The matter is what Allah has wished," or: "What Allah has wished is [existent]." In both cases, ma (مَا) is a relative pronoun. It is also possible for it to be conditional, the assumption being: "Whatever Allah wished, came to be." The most appropriate to the context is the first assumption, because the purpose is to clarify the return of all matters to the will of Allah, the Exalted, in contrast to one who claims independence and self-sufficiency.

His saying: لَا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِاللَّهِ (There is no power except by Allah) signifies that power subsists through Allah and restricts all power to Him, in the sense that whatever power appears in His creatures is itself subsisting through Him, without what He has given of it being cut off so that the creature becomes independent with it. The Exalted said: أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا (...that power belongs entirely to Allah) [Qur'an 2:165].

With this, the response to what the disbeliever said to his companion and what he said upon entering his garden is complete.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ تَرَنِ أَنَا أَقَلَّ مِنْكَ مَالًا وَوَلَدًا فَعَسَى — If you see me as less than you in wealth and children, maybe... [Qur'an 18:39] until the end of the two verses. It is said in al-Majma‘: The origin of husban (حُسْبَان) is the arrows that are shot to run in a single course, which was from the shooting of the knights. The root of the word is hisab (calculation), and that which is shot is called husban because it is as numerous as a calculation. He said: Zalaq (زَلَق) is smooth, level ground with no vegetation or anything else on it. The root of zalaq is that which feet slip from and do not remain firm upon.

It has preceded that the sa‘id (صَعِيد) is the surface of the earth when it is level with no vegetation on it. The meaning of the water becoming ghawr (غَوْر) is its becoming sunken, disappearing into the depths of the earth.

The two verses, as previously indicated, are a rebuttal from the believer to his disbelieving companion regarding his exaltation over him by being wealthier and having a stronger retinue. The rebuttal he presented is derived from his previous clarification, and its summary is: Since matters are by the will and power of Allah, and He has made you wealthier than me and stronger in retinue, the matter in that belongs to Him, not to you, such that you should boast and exalt yourself over me. Thus, it is possible and hoped that He will give me better than your garden and destroy your garden, turning me to a state better than your state today and turning you to a state worse than my state today, making me richer than you relative to myself and making you poorer than me relative to yourself.

It appears that taran (تَرَنِ) in His saying: إِنْ تَرَنِ أَنَا أَقَلَّ (If you see me as less...) is from ra’y (opinion) in the sense of belief, thus being one of the "verbs of the heart." The word أَنَا (I) is a separating pronoun (ضمير فصل) placed between its two objects, which were originally a subject and predicate. It is also possible for it to be from ru’yah in the sense of sight, in which case ana is a nominative pronoun emphasizing the elided object of taran.

The meaning of the verse is: If you see me as less than you in wealth and children, there is no harm, for the matter in that belongs to my Lord. Perhaps my Lord will give me better than your garden and send upon it—that is, upon your garden—missiles (مرامي) of His heavenly punishment, such as hail, a scorching wind, a thunderbolt, or the like, so it becomes empty, smooth ground with no trees or crops. Or its water becomes sunken, so you will never be able to seek it due to its deep penetration into the ground.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأُحِيطَ بِثَمَرِهِ فَأَصْبَحَ يُقَلِّبُ كَفَّيْهِ — And his produce was encompassed [by destruction], and he began to wring his hands... [Qur'an 18:42]. Encompassing (الإحاطة) a thing is an allusion to its destruction; it is taken from the enemy's surrounding and encircling it from all sides such that it is cut off from every helper and supporter, which is destruction. The Exalted said: وَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ أُحِيطَ بِهِمْ (...and they think they are encompassed...) [Qur'an 10:22].

His saying: فَأَصْبَحَ يُقَلِّبُ كَفَّيْهِ (and he began to wring his hands) is an allusion to regret, for the regretful person often turns his hands over, back to front. His saying: وَهِيَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَى عُرُوشِهَا (while it lay fallen on its trellises) is an allusion to complete ruin, as has been said; for ruined, demolished houses first have their trellises—which are their roofs—fall to the ground, then their walls fall upon the fallen trellises. Khaway (الخوي) means falling, and it was said the root meaning is emptiness.

His saying: وَيَقُولُ يَا لَيْتَنِي لَمْ أُشْرِكْ بِرَبِّي أَحَدًا (and saying, ‘I wish I had not ascribed any partner to my Lord!’) means: I wish I had not attached myself to what I attached myself to, nor relied upon nor found tranquility in these causes which I reckoned had independence in influence, and that I had returned the entire matter to my Lord; for my effort has gone astray and my soul has perished.

The meaning is: All types of his wealth were destroyed or the fruit of his garden was spoiled, so he became regretful over the wealth he had spent while the garden lay in ruins, saying: I wish I had not associated anyone with my Lord, nor found repose in what I found repose in and was deceived by—of myself and other causes—which have benefited me nothing.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمْ تَكُنْ لَهُ فِئَةٌ يَنْصُرُونَهُ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَمَا كَانَ مُنْتَصِرًا — He had no party to help him besides Allah, nor could he help himself. [Qur'an 18:43]. A party (الفئة) is a group, and one who helps himself (المنتصر) is one who is immune or defended.

Just as the first five verses—namely His saying: قَالَ لَهُ صَاحِبُهُ (His companion said to him...) until His saying: طَلَبًا (...to seek it)—were a verbal clarification of the man's error in his disbelief and polytheism, so too are these two verses—namely His saying: وَأُحِيطَ بِثَمَرِهِ (And his produce was encompassed...) until His saying: وَمَا كَانَ مُنْتَصِرًا (...nor could he help himself)—an actual, practical clarification for him. As for his attachment to the duration of the world and the continuity of its ornament in his saying: "I do not think that this will ever perish," the error in it was made manifest to him when his produce was encompassed and his garden became fallen on its trellises. As for his repose in causes and reliance upon them when he said to his companion, "I am wealthier than you and have a stronger retinue," his error in it was clarified by His saying: "He had no party to help him besides Allah." As for the claim of his independence in himself and his boasting of it, the aspect of its invalidity was alluded to by His saying: "nor could he help himself."

His saying, the Exalted: هُنَالِكَ الْوَلَايَةُ لِلَّهِ الْحَقِّ هُوَ خَيْرٌ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ عُقْبًا — There, all authority belongs to Allah, the Real. He is best in rewarding and best in [providing] a destination. [Qur'an 18:44]. The famous recitation is al-walayah (الْوَلَايَةُ) with a fatha on the waw, and it has been recited with a kasra; the meaning is the same. Some mentioned that with a fatha it means support (النصرة) and with a kasra it means authority (السلطان), but this is not established. Likewise, الْحَقِّ (the Real) is with a jar. Reward (الثواب) is the general consequence and wage, and it has become predominant for a good and beautiful wage. ‘Uqub (عُقْبًا) with a damma then sukun, or with two dammas, means the end or destination (العاقبة).

The commentators mentioned that the allusion in His saying: هُنَالِكَ (There) is to the meaning of His saying: أُحِيطَ بِثَمَرِهِ (his produce was encompassed); that is, in that place or at that time—which is the place and time of destruction—authority belongs to Allah. They say al-walayah means support (النصرة); that is, Allah, the Glorified, is the Supporter of man when affliction encompasses him and he is cut off from all causes; there is no supporter besides Him.

This is a true meaning in itself, but it does not suit the purpose for which the verses were sent down, which is to clarify that the entire matter belongs to Allah, the Glorified, and He is the Creator of everything and the Governor of every affair, and for other than Him there is nothing but the mirage of illusion and the beautification of life for the purpose of trial and examination. Had it been as they mentioned, it would have been more appropriate to describe Him, the Exalted, in His saying: لِلَّهِ الْحَقِّ (to Allah, the Real) with power, might, ability, dominance, and the like, not with something like "the Real" (الحق) which stands in contrast to "the False" (الباطل). Also, there would be no apparent reason or beautiful placement for His saying: هُوَ خَيْرٌ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ عُقْبًا (He is best in rewarding and best in [providing] a destination).

The truth, and Allah knows best, is that authority (الولاية) means the ownership of governance (مالكية التدبير), which is the meaning permeating all its derivations, as passed in the discussion on His saying: إِنَّمَا وَلِيُّكُمُ اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ (Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle...) [Qur'an 5:55]. That is, when destruction encompasses and causes fail to have influence, and the helplessness of man—who used to see himself as independent and self-sufficient—becomes clear, the authority over the affair of man and everything, and the ownership of its governance, belongs to Allah. This is because He is a Real God (إله حق) to whom belongs governance and influence according to the reality of the matter. Other than Him, from the apparent causes called partners with Him in governance and influence, are false (باطل) in themselves, possessing nothing of effect except what Allah has permitted them and made them own. They have no independence except in name according to what man imagined; thus, they are false in themselves, real through Allah, the Glorified, and Allah is the Real by His essence, the Independent, the Self-Sufficient in Himself.

When a comparison is made between Him—exalted is He above comparison—and other causes called partners in influence, Allah, the Glorified, is better than them in reward; for He rewards the one who submits to Him with a true reward, while they reward the one who submits to them and attaches himself to them with a false, vanishing reward that does not endure—and even that is from Allah and by His permission. And Allah, the Glorified, is better than them in destination (عاقبة) because He, the Glorified, is the Fixed Truth who does not perish, vanish, or change from what He is of Majesty and Honor. These other things are vanishing, changing matters that Allah made as an ornament for the worldly life, with which man becomes infatuated and to which his heart attaches, until the decree reaches its term, and Allah will surely make them a barren plain.

Since man cannot do without attaching himself to something to which he attributes governance and from which he expects the rectification of his state, his Lord is better for him than any other, because He is best in rewarding and best in destination.

Some mentioned that the allusion in His saying: هُنَالِكَ (There) is to the Day of Resurrection, so the meaning of reward and destination would be what is on that day. The context, as you know, does not support any of that.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاضْرِبْ لَهُمْ مَثَلَ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا كَمَاءٍ أَنْزَلْنَاهُ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ — Draw for them the parable of the life of this world: [It is] like the water We send down from the sky... [Qur'an 18:45]. This is the second parable, struck to represent the worldly life with its accompanying ornament that is swift to vanish.

Hashim (هَشِيم) is a passive participle from hashm, which, as al-Raghib said, is the breaking of a soft thing like a plant. Dhara yadhru dharwan (ذَرَا يَذْرُو ذَرْوًا) means to scatter; it was also said it means to bring and take away. His saying: فَاخْتَلَطَ بِهِ نَبَاتُ الْأَرْضِ (and the vegetation of the earth mingles with it) and He did not say "it mingled with the vegetation of the earth" to indicate its dominance in the formation of the plant over other parts. He did not mention with the water of the sky other waters from springs and rivers because the origin of all is the rain water. His saying: فَأَصْبَحَ هَشِيمًا (then it becomes chaff)—"becomes" (asbaha) here, as has been said, is in the sense of sara (to become), so it does not restrict the report to the morning.

The meaning is: Strike for these people who are infatuated with the ornament of the world and turning away from the remembrance of their Lord the parable of the worldly life: like water We sent down from the sky—which is rain—and the vegetation of the earth mingled with it, so it flourished with freshness and joy and appeared in the most beautiful attire. Then after that, it became broken, severed chaff that the winds play with, scattering it and bringing it and taking it away. And Allah has power over all things.

His saying, the Exalted: الْمَالُ وَالْبَنُونَ زِينَةُ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا — Wealth and children are an adornment of the life of the world... [Qur'an 18:46]. The verse is like a conclusion to the previous parable, which is that wealth and children—even if hearts attach to them and souls long for them, expecting benefit from them and surrounding them with hopes—are a swiftly vanishing, deceptive ornament. They cannot reward him or benefit him in all that he desired from them, nor can they fulfill all that he hopes and wishes for, nor even most of it. In the verse—as you see—is a return to the beginning of the discourse, namely His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا مَا عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَهَا (Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it...) [Qur'an 18:7-8].

His saying: وَالْبَاقِيَاتُ الصَّالِحَاتُ خَيْرٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ أَمَلًا (but lasting righteous deeds are better with your Lord in reward and better in hope). What is meant by lasting righteous deeds (الباقيات الصالحات) are righteous acts; for the deeds of man are preserved for him with Allah by the text of the Qur'an, so they are lasting. If they are righteous, they are lasting righteous deeds. They are better with Allah in reward because Allah rewards the person who brings them with the best reward. And they are better in hope because what is hoped for through them of Allah's mercy and honor is attainable for man; thus, they are truer in hope than the ornaments and glitters of the world, which do not fulfill for man most of what they promise. The hopes attached to the world are mostly false, and what is true among them is deceptive and misleading.

Several narrations have been reported through the paths of the Shi‘a and the Sunnis from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and through the paths of the Shi‘a from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), stating that the lasting righteous deeds are the four glorifications (التسبيحات الأربع): Subhan Allah, wal-Hamdu lillah, wa la ilaha illallah, wallahu Akbar. In another, it is the prayer (الصلاة), and in another, the love for the Ahl al-Bayt. All of these are of the nature of applying the concept to its prime examples (الجري والانطباق).

Ayahs

وَيَوْمَ نُسَيِّرُ ٱلْجِبَالَ وَتَرَى ٱلْأَرْضَ بَارِزَةً وَحَشَرْنَـٰهُمْ فَلَمْ نُغَادِرْ مِنْهُمْ أَحَدًا (٤٧)
The day We shall set the mountains moving and you will see the earth in full view, We shall muster them, and We will not leave out anyone of them. (47)
وَعُرِضُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ صَفًّا لَّقَدْ جِئْتُمُونَا كَمَا خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍۭ ۚ بَلْ زَعَمْتُمْ أَلَّن نَّجْعَلَ لَكُم مَّوْعِدًا (٤٨)
They will be presented before your Lord in ranks: ‘Certainly you have come to Us just as We created you the first time. But you maintained that We shall not appoint a tryst for you.’ (48)
وَوُضِعَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ فَتَرَى ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا فِيهِ وَيَقُولُونَ يَـٰوَيْلَتَنَا مَالِ هَـٰذَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا يُغَادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَلَا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّآ أَحْصَىٰهَا ۚ وَوَجَدُوا۟ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ حَاضِرًا ۗ وَلَا يَظْلِمُ رَبُّكَ أَحَدًا (٤٩)
The Book will be set up. Then you will see the guilty apprehensive of what is in it. They will say, ‘Woe to us! What a book is this! It omits nothing, big or small, without enumerating it.’ They will find present whatever they had done, and your Lord does not wrong anyone. (49)
وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِۦٓ ۗ أَفَتَتَّخِذُونَهُۥ وَذُرِّيَّتَهُۥٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِى وَهُمْ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّۢ ۚ بِئْسَ لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بَدَلًا (٥٠)
When We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis. He was one of the jinn, so he transgressed against his Lord’s command. Will you then take him and his offspring for guardians in My stead, though they are your enemies? How evil a substitute for the wrongdoers! (50)
۞ مَّآ أَشْهَدتُّهُمْ خَلْقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا خَلْقَ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَمَا كُنتُ مُتَّخِذَ ٱلْمُضِلِّينَ عَضُدًا (٥١)
I did not make them witness to the creation of the heavens and the earth, nor to their own creation, nor do I take those who mislead others as assistants. (51)
وَيَوْمَ يَقُولُ نَادُوا۟ شُرَكَآءِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ فَدَعَوْهُمْ فَلَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَهُمْ وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُم مَّوْبِقًا (٥٢)
The day He will say [to the polytheists], ‘Call those whom you maintained to be My partners,’ they will call them, but they will not respond to them, for We shall set an abyss between them. (52)
وَرَءَا ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ ٱلنَّارَ فَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُم مُّوَاقِعُوهَا وَلَمْ يَجِدُوا۟ عَنْهَا مَصْرِفًا (٥٣)
The guilty will sight the Fire and know that they are about to fall in it, for they will find no way to escape it. (53)
وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَا فِى هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ لِلنَّاسِ مِن كُلِّ مَثَلٍ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَكْثَرَ شَىْءٍ جَدَلًا (٥٤)
We have certainly interspersed this Quran with every kind of parable for the people. But man is the most disputatious of creatures. (54)
وَمَا مَنَعَ ٱلنَّاسَ أَن يُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ إِذْ جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن تَأْتِيَهُمْ سُنَّةُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ قُبُلًا (٥٥)
Nothing has kept these people from believing and pleading to their Lord for forgiveness when guidance came to them, except [their demand] that the precedent of the ancients come to pass for them, or that the punishment come to them, face to face. (55)
وَمَا نُرْسِلُ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ ۚ وَيُجَـٰدِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ لِيُدْحِضُوا۟ بِهِ ٱلْحَقَّ ۖ وَٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ ءَايَـٰتِى وَمَآ أُنذِرُوا۟ هُزُوًا (٥٦)
We do not send the apostles except as bearers of good news and as warners, but those who are faithless dispute fallaciously to refute thereby the truth, having taken My signs and what they are warned of in derision. (56)
وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن ذُكِّرَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِۦ فَأَعْرَضَ عَنْهَا وَنَسِىَ مَا قَدَّمَتْ يَدَاهُ ۚ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَن يَفْقَهُوهُ وَفِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَقْرًا ۖ وَإِن تَدْعُهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ فَلَن يَهْتَدُوٓا۟ إِذًا أَبَدًا (٥٧)
Who is a greater wrongdoer than he who is reminded of the signs of his Lord, whereat he disregards them and forgets what his hands have sent ahead? Indeed We have cast veils on their hearts lest they should understand it, and a deafness into their ears; and if you invite them to guidance they will never [let themselves] be guided. (57)
وَرَبُّكَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ذُو ٱلرَّحْمَةِ ۖ لَوْ يُؤَاخِذُهُم بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ لَعَجَّلَ لَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابَ ۚ بَل لَّهُم مَّوْعِدٌ لَّن يَجِدُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦ مَوْئِلًا (٥٨)
Your Lord is the All-forgiving dispenser of mercy. Were He to take them to task because of what they have committed, He would have surely hastened their punishment. But they have a tryst, [when] they will not find a refuge besides Him. (58)
وَتِلْكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ لَمَّا ظَلَمُوا۟ وَجَعَلْنَا لِمَهْلِكِهِم مَّوْعِدًا (٥٩)
Those are the towns that We destroyed when they were wrongdoers, and We appointed a tryst for their destruction. (59)

(Commentary)

The verses are connected to what preceded them, following the same course in pursuing the explanation that these outward causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) and the deceptive vanities of the world—which are the "adornment of life"—will soon vanish. It will then become clear to man that they possess for him neither benefit nor harm; rather, what remains for or against him is his deed, for which he shall be recompensed.

The verses first mention the commencement of the Hour and the arrival of man as an individual, having nothing with him but his deed. Then, they mention Iblis and his refusal to prostrate to Adam and his defiance (فسقه) of his Lord’s command, yet they take him and his progeny as guardians (أولياء) besides Allah, while they are enemies to them. Subsequently, the verses mention the Day of Resurrection, the summoning of them and their "associates," and the manifestation of the severance of all ties between them. This is followed by other verses regarding the promise and the threat. All of this, in accordance with the intended purpose, is connected to what preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ نُسَيِّرُ الْجِبالَ وَتَرَى الْأَرْضَ بارِزَةً وَحَشَرْناهُمْ فَلَمْ نُغادِرْ مِنْهُمْ أَحَداً — The day We shall move the mountains, and you will see the earth a leveled plain, and We shall muster them, and We will not leave out any one of them. (18:47). The adverbial phrase (الظرف) relates to an omitted verb, the estimation being: "And mention the day We move..." The moving of the mountains (تسيير الجبال) refers to their removal from their fixed places. The Glorified has expressed this with various descriptions, such as His saying: "and the mountains became like sand-dunes poured out" (73:14), and His saying: "and the mountains will be like carded wool" (101:5), and His saying: "and they became scattered dust" (56:6), and His saying: "and the mountains will be moved and become a mirage" (78:20).

What is understood from the context is that the "prominence" of the earth (بروز الأرض) is a consequence of the moving of the mountains. When the mountains and hills are removed, the earth is seen as a leveled plain (بارزة), where no part of it is hidden from another by any barrier or obstacle, and no region is concealed from another by a screen. It is also possible that it refers to what is mentioned in His saying: "And the earth will glow with the light of its Lord" (39:69).

And His saying: وَحَشَرْناهُمْ فَلَمْ نُغادِرْ مِنْهُمْ أَحَداً — and We shall muster them, and We will not leave out any one of them—means We did not leave behind any of them; thus, the gathering (الحشر) is universal for all.

His saying, the Exalted: وَعُرِضُوا عَلى رَبِّكَ صَفًّا لَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونا كَما خَلَقْناكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ — They will be presented before your Lord in ranks: ‘Certainly you have come to Us just as We created you the first time.’ (18:48). The context bears witness that the plural pronoun in His saying: عُرِضُوا (they will be presented), as well as the two plural pronouns in the preceding verse, refer to the polytheists (المشركين). They are those who felt secure in themselves and in the outward causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) to which their lives were tied, and who clung to the adornment of life as one clings to something permanent and eternal. This resulted in their severance from their Lord, their denial of returning to Him, and their indifference toward the deeds they performed—whether they pleased Allah or angered Him.

This remains their state as long as the foundation of the divine trial (الامتحان الإلهي) and the fleeting adornment are before them, and the outward causes surround them, before the matter reaches its term. Then, when the appointed time arrives, the causes are severed, hopes perish, and Allah makes whatever is upon the earth a barren dust, nothing remains for them then except their Lord, themselves, and the record of their deeds preserved against them. They are presented before their Lord—whom they did not regard as their Lord, for had they done so, they would have worshipped Him—in a single rank (صفاً واحداً). There is no superiority among them based on lineage, wealth, or worldly status for the final judgment. At that moment, it becomes clear to them that Allah is the Manifest Truth (الحق المبين), and that what they invoked besides Him and what their hearts were attached to—the adornment of life, the perceived independence of their selves, and the causes subjected to them—were nothing but illusions that avail them nothing against Allah. They were in error when they clung to them and turned away from the path of their Lord, failing to act according to what He willed for them. Rather, this occurred because they imagined there was no station where they would be made to stand and be held to account.

Through this explanation, it becomes clear that these four sentences: "They will be presented...", "Certainly you have come to Us...", "Rather you claimed...", and "The Book will be set..." are fundamental points selected from the details of what occurs that day between them and their Lord, from the moment they are gathered until they are judged. These were deemed sufficient for the sake of conciseness, as the purpose is achieved through them.

Thus, His saying: وَعُرِضُوا عَلى رَبِّكَ صَفًّا (They will be presented before your Lord in ranks) indicates, firstly, that they are compelled to return to their Lord and meet Him, being presented before Him without having chosen it for themselves. Secondly, it indicates that there is no honor for them in this meeting; this is suggested by His saying: عَلى رَبِّكَ (before your Lord). Had they been honored, it would have been said: "their Lord," as He said: "their reward with their Lord is the Gardens of Eden" (98:8), and "they shall indeed meet their Lord" (11:29). Or it would have been said: "presented before Us," following the previous first-person context. Thirdly, it indicates that the types of worldly superiority and honors fabricated by worldly illusions—such as lineage, wealth, and status—have fallen away from them. Thus, they are lined up in a single rank where there is no distinction between the high and the low, the rich and the poor, or the master and the slave. Distinction today is only by deed. At that point, it becomes clear to them that they missed the right mark in their worldly life and strayed from the path, so they are addressed with the likes of His saying: لَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونا (Certainly you have come to Us...).

His saying: لَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونا كَما خَلَقْناكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ (Certainly you have come to Us just as We created you the first time) is the content of the speech, the estimation being: "And it was said to them" or "We said to them: Certainly you have come to Us..." In this is an explanation of their error and misguidance in the world, as they clung to its adornment and vanity, which distracted them from traversing the path of Allah and adhering to His religion.

His saying: بَلْ زَعَمْتُمْ أَلَّنْ نَجْعَلَ لَكُمْ مَوْعِداً — ‘Rather you claimed that We would never appoint a tryst for you.’ (18:48) is similar in meaning to His saying: "Did you suppose that We created you aimlessly, and that you will not be brought back to Us?" (23:115). If the sentence is a digression (إضراب) from the preceding sentence—as the context suggests—then the estimation is something like: "The adornment of the world and your attachment to yourselves and outward causes distracted you from Our worship and traversing Our path; rather, you thought that We would never appoint for you a tryst (موعداً) in which you would meet Us and be held to account." In other words: your preoccupation with the world and attachment to its adornment, though it was a cause for turning away from Our remembrance and committing sins, there was a cause more primary and foundational, which is that you thought We would never appoint a tryst for you. Forgetting the Return (المعاد) is the root cause for abandoning the path and the corruption of deeds. The Exalted said: "Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because they forgot the Day of Reckoning" (38:26).

The reason for attributing the thought of denying the Return to them is that their total devotion to the world, their attachment to its adornment, and to those they invoked besides Allah, is the act of one who thinks the world is permanent and eternal for them and that they will not return to Allah. Thus, it is a situational and practical thought (ظن حالي عملي) on their part. It could also be a metaphor for their lack of concern for Allah's command and their belittling of what they were warned of, similar to His saying: "but you thought that Allah does not know much of what you do" (41:22).

It is also possible that His saying: بَلْ زَعَمْتُمْ أَلَّنْ نَجْعَلَ لَكُمْ مَوْعِداً is a digression from an assumed excuse on their part, such as ignorance or the like. Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: وَوُضِعَ الْكِتابُ فَتَرَى الْمُجْرِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا فِيهِ وَيَقُولُونَ يا وَيْلَتَنا — The Book will be set up. Then you will see the guilty apprehensive of what is in it. They will say, ‘Woe to us! (18:49) to the end of the verse. The "setting up" (وضع) of the Book means placing it so that judgment may be passed based upon it. "Apprehensive" (مشفقين) is from ishfaq (الإشفاق), the root of which is tenderness (الرقة). Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "Apprehension (الإشفاق) is care mixed with fear, because the one who is apprehensive loves the one he is apprehensive for and fears what might befall him. The Exalted said: 'and they are apprehensive (مشفقون) of the Hour.' When it is followed by the preposition min (من), the meaning of fear is more apparent, and when followed by fi (في), the meaning of care is more apparent. The Exalted said: 'Indeed, we used to be apprehensive (مشفقين) among our people before' and 'apprehensive (مشفقون) of it (منها).'" End quote.

Woe (الويل) is destruction (الهلاك). Invoking it at the time of calamity—as has been said—is a metaphor for the calamity being more severe than destruction itself; thus, one seeks help through destruction to be saved from the calamity, just as one might wish for death during a catastrophe. The Exalted said: "I wish I had died before this" (19:23).

His saying: وَوُضِعَ الْكِتابُ (The Book will be set up)—the context suggests it is a single Book set up for the reckoning of everyone's deeds. This does not contradict the setting up of a specific book for every individual. Quranic verses indicate that every human has a book, every nation has a book, and there is a book for all. The Exalted said: "We have fastened every man’s omen to his neck, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book" (17:13), and the discussion regarding it has preceded. He also said: "Every nation will be summoned to its book" (45:28), and: "This is Our book that speaks against you with the truth" (45:29). The discussion on these two verses will follow, Allah willing.

It has been said that "the Book" refers to the records of deeds, and the definite article (اللام) denotes exhaustiveness (الاستغراق), but the context does not support this.

His saying: فَتَرَى الْمُجْرِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا فِيهِ (Then you will see the guilty apprehensive of what is in it)—the branching of this sentence from the setting up of the Book and the mention of their apprehension of what is in it is evidence that it is the book of deeds or a book containing the deeds. Mentioning them by the description of "guilt" (الاجرام) is to indicate the cause of the judgment—that their apprehension of what is in it is because they are guilty. Thus, the judgment encompasses every guilty person, even if they were not a polytheist.

His saying: وَيَقُولُونَ يا وَيْلَتَنا ما لِهذَا الْكِتابِ لا يُغادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَلا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّا أَحْصاها — They will say, ‘Woe to us! What a book is this! It leaves out nothing, small or great, but has counted it.’ (18:49). "Small" and "great" are adjectives standing in place of their nouns, which are "sin," "disobedience," "lapse," or the like.

This saying of theirs is an expression of shock and terror at the authority of the Book in its enumeration of sins—or of events in general, including sins—in the form of an interrogative of wonderment. From this, the reason for mentioning the small before the great in His saying: صَغِيرَةً وَلا كَبِيرَةً (small or great) is known. Although it might outwardly seem that one should say "it leaves out nothing great or small" based on the fact that the speech is in the sense of affirmation, and the proper progression (الترقي) in it is to move from the great to the small. However, this is because the meaning—Allah knows best—is: it does not leave out a small matter due to its smallness and subtlety, nor a great matter due to its magnitude and clarity. The context is one of seeking to inspire terror in the form of wonderment, and the enumeration of a small matter despite its smallness and subtlety is closer to achieving that than anything else.

His saying: وَوَجَدُوا ما عَمِلُوا حاضِراً — They will find present whatever they had done (18:49). The context suggests that this sentence is a new point (تأسيس), not an explanatory apposition (عطف تفسير) for His saying: "It leaves out nothing, small or great..." Accordingly, what is "present" with them are the deeds themselves in their appropriate forms, not merely the writing of them. This is the apparent meaning of verses like His saying: "O you who faithless! Do not make any excuses today. You are only being requited for what you used to do" (66:7). This is supported by His saying afterward: وَلا يَظْلِمُ رَبُّكَ أَحَداً — and your Lord does not wrong anyone (18:49). For the negation of wrong (الظلم) is clearer based on the embodiment of deeds (تجسم الأعمال), because what they are requited with is only their own deed being returned to them and attached to them, with no one else having a hand in it. Understand this.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قُلْنا لِلْمَلائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ كانَ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِ — When We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis. He was one of the jinn, so he transgressed against his Lord’s command. (18:50). This is a second reminder to them of what occurred between Him, the Exalted, and Iblis when He commanded the angels to prostrate to their father Adam. They prostrated, except Iblis, who was of the jinn and rebelled against his Lord’s command.

That is: mention this event so it becomes clear to them that Iblis—who is of the jinn—and his progeny are enemies to them and do not wish them well. Therefore, it is not fitting for them to be seduced by what he and his progeny adorn for them of worldly pleasures and desires, nor to turn away from the remembrance of Allah, nor to obey them in the falsehood to which they invite them.

His saying: أَفَتَتَّخِذُونَهُ وَذُرِّيَّتَهُ أَوْلِياءَ مِنْ دُونِي وَهُمْ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ — ‘Will you then take him and his offspring for guardians besides Me, while they are your enemies?’ (18:50) is a consequence (تفريع) of the outcome of the event, and the interrogation is for denunciation (الإنكار). That is: it follows from the event that you should not take him and his progeny as guardians while they are enemies to you, the assembly of mankind. Based on this, what is meant by guardianship (الولاية) is the guardianship of obedience (ولاية الطاعة), where they obey him and his progeny in what they invite them to; thus, they have taken them as ones to be obeyed besides Allah. This is how the commentators have explained it.

It is not far-fetched that what is meant by guardianship is the guardianship of dominion and governance (ولاية الملك والتدبير), which is Lordship (الربوبية). For just as the idolaters worship the angels hoping for their good, they also worship the jinn to guard against their evil. He, the Glorified, explicitly states that Iblis is of the jinn and has progeny, and that the misguidance of man on the path of his felicity and the various miseries that bridle him are only through the seduction of Satan. Thus, the meaning is: Will you then take him and his progeny as gods and lords besides Me, worshipping them and seeking nearness to them, while they are your enemies?

This is supported by the following verse, for the fact that they were not made witnesses to the creation is only suitable for negating the guardianship of governance (ولاية التدبير) from them, not the guardianship of obedience. This is evident.

He concluded the verse by decrying their taking them as guardians besides Allah, which means taking Iblis as a substitute (بدلاً) for Him, the Glorified, saying: بِئْسَ لِلظَّالِمِينَ بَدَلاً — Evil is the substitute for the wrongdoers! (18:50). How evil that is! No one with any sense would commit such a thing. This is the secret behind the shift (الالتفات) in His saying: مِنْ دُونِي (besides Me); He did not say "besides Us" following the context of His saying: "When We said," in order to increase the clarity of the vileness. Similarly, this is the secret in the previous shift in His saying: عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِ (against his Lord’s command) rather than saying "against Our command."

The commentators have discussions here regarding the meaning of the command to the angels including Iblis, and the meaning of his being from the jinn. We have already presented some discussion on this in the commentary of Surah al-A'raf.

His saying, the Exalted: ما أَشْهَدْتُهُمْ خَلْقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلا خَلْقَ أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَما كُنْتُ مُتَّخِذَ الْمُضِلِّينَ عَضُداً — I did not make them witness the creation of the heavens and the earth, nor their own creation, nor do I take those who mislead as assistants. (18:51). The context clearly shows that the two plural pronouns refer to Iblis and his progeny. What is meant by "making witness" (الإشهاد) is bringing them present and informing them through direct vision (عياناً), just as "witnessing" (الشهود) is observation through presence. The upper arm (العضد) is the part of a human between the elbow and the shoulder, and it is used metaphorically for an assistant (المعين), like the "hand," and that is what is intended here.

The verse contains two arguments for negating the guardianship of governance from Iblis and his progeny. The first: the guardianship of governing the affairs of any thing depends on an encompassing knowledge (الإحاطة العلمية)—in the full sense of the term—of those affairs from the perspective they are governed, and of the internal and external links that thing and those affairs have with what they begin from, what they are concurrent with, and what they end at. Existential connection (الارتباط الوجودي) permeates all parts of the cosmos. These—Iblis and his progeny—Allah, the Glorified, did not make them witness the creation of the heavens and the earth, nor their own creation. They were not witnesses when He said to the heavens and the earth: "Be," and they were; nor when He said to them: "Be," and they were. Thus, they are ignorant of the reality of the heavens and the earth and the secrets of creation within the vessels of their existences, even the reality of their own making. How then can it be within their capacity to assume the governance of their affairs, or the governance of a portion of them, such that they would be gods and lords besides Allah, while they are ignorant of the reality of their creation and the creation of themselves?

As for the fact that they did not witness their creation, it is because each of them is a limited thing with no access to what is beyond itself. Thus, others are in a state of the Unseen (غيب) from it, with a veil drawn over them. This is clear and Allah, the Glorified, has informed of it in various places in His speech. Likewise, each of them is veiled from the state of the causes that preceded its existence and the consequences that will follow its existence.

This is a demonstrative argument (حجة برهانية), not a dialectical one, for one who has perfected his vision and delved deep into reflection, such that he does not confuse this false play we call "governance" with the cosmic governance (التدبير الكوني) which is never overtaken by error or misguidance. Likewise, he does not confuse the vain thoughts and claims we circulate and rely upon with the visionary knowledge (العلم العياني) which is the reality of knowledge, nor the knowledge of hidden matters by finding their majority signs with the knowledge of the Unseen (العلم بالغيب) through which the Unseen is transformed into witnessing.

The second argument: every species of creature is directed by its primordial nature (الفطرة) toward its specific perfection. This is necessary for one who tracks them and reflects deeply on their state. Thus, divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية) is universal for all, as He said: "He who gave everything its creation and then guided it" (20:50). The satans are evil, corruptors, and misleaders. Their undertaking the governance of anything in the heavens, the earth, or man—which could only be by permission from Allah, the Glorified—would lead to the nullification of the divine sunnah of universal guidance. That is, it would mean His, the Exalted, seeking recourse to reform through that whose nature is only corruption, and to guidance through that whose property is only misguidance, which is impossible.

This is the meaning of His saying, the Glorified: وَما كُنْتُ مُتَّخِذَ الْمُضِلِّينَ عَضُداً (nor do I take those who mislead as assistants), which indicates that His sunnah, the Exalted, is not to take the misleaders as assistants. Understand this.

In His saying: ما أَشْهَدْتُهُمْ (I did not make them witness) and His saying: وَما كُنْتُ (nor do I), rather than saying "they did not witness" or "they were not," there is an indication that He, the Glorified, is the Subduer (القاهر) and Overseer (المهيمن) over them in every state. Those who claim that the satans, the angels, or others are partners with Allah in the matter of governance do not claim their independence in that, but rather that the matter of governance is possessed by them through Allah's making them possess it, and delegated to them through His delegation, and that they are lords and gods while Allah is the Lord of lords and God of gods.

What has preceded regarding the meaning of the verse is based on taking "making witness" (الإشهاد) in its literal sense and returning the two pronouns in "I did not make them witness" and "their own" to Iblis and his progeny, as is the apparent and immediate sense from the context. Commentators have other views:

Among them is the view of some: that what is meant by "making witness" in their creation is "consultation" (المشاورة) metaphorically. For the lowest rank of guardianship over a thing is to be consulted in its affair. What is meant by negating "assistance" (الاعتضاد) is negating all other ranks of seeking help that lead to guardianship and authority over the one governed in some way. It is as if it were said: I did not consult them in the matter of their creation, nor did I seek help from them in any form of seeking help; so from where would they be guardians for them?

The objection to this is that there is no context for this metaphor, and there is nothing preventing taking it in its literal sense. Furthermore, there is no link between giving advice on a thing and guardianship over it, such that consultation would be counted as one of the ranks of appointing a guardian, or advice as one of the degrees of guardianship. Some have justified this meaning by saying that what is meant by "making witness" is consultation as a metonymy (كناية), and the necessary consequence of consultation is that He would create as they wished—that is, He would create them as they liked, meaning He would create them perfect. Thus, the negation of making the satans witness their own creation is the negation of their being perfect in creation such that they would be capable of the guardianship of governing affairs.

In addition to the objections raised against the previous view, it is also noted: first, that this involves using a term and intending its necessary consequence through five levels of necessity. Consultation is the consequence of making witness according to his claim; creating what the advisor wishes is the consequence of consultation; creating what he likes is the consequence of creating what he wishes; perfection of creation is the consequence of creating what he likes; and the validity of guardianship is the consequence of perfection of creation. Thus, using "making witness" and intending "perfection of creation" or "validity of guardianship" is like using a metonymy for the consequence of a meaning through four or five necessities. The Manifest Book is far above such riddles.

Second: if this were correct, it would only be correct regarding making them witness their own creation, not making them witness the creation of the heavens and the earth. This would necessitate a separation between the two instances of "making witness."

Third: its necessary consequence would be the validity of the guardianship of whoever was perfect in their creation, such as the Archangels. In this is an admission of the possibility of their guardianship and the permissibility of their lordship, while the Quran rejects that with the clearest explanation. Where is the possible being (الممكن), who is essentially impoverished (المفتقر لذاته) before Allah, the Glorified, from independence in governing itself or governing others? As for the likes of His saying, the Exalted: "by those who regulate the affair" (79:5), the clarification of its meaning will follow, Allah willing.

Among them is the view of some: that "making witness" is intended in its literal sense and the two pronouns refer to the satans, but what is meant by making them witness their own creation is making some of them witness the creation of others, not making each witness his own creation.

The objection is that the negation of making witness is intended to conclude the negation of guardianship. None of the polytheists claimed the guardianship of some satans over others, nor was the purpose concerned with negating that, such that the wording of the verse should be taken to mean making some of them witness the creation of others.

Among them is the view of some: that the first of the pronouns refers to the satans and the second to the disbelievers, or to them and other people. The meaning being: I did not make the satans witness the creation of the heavens and the earth, nor the creation of the disbelievers or people, such that they would be guardians for them.

The objection is that this involves a separation of the pronouns.

Among them is the view of some: that the two pronouns return to the disbelievers. Imam al-Razi said in his commentary: "What is closer in my view is their return—meaning the two pronouns—to the disbelievers who said to the Messenger (peace be upon him and his progeny): 'If you do not expel these poor people from your assembly, we will not believe in you.' It is as if He, the Exalted, said: 'Indeed, these who came with this corrupt proposal and vain obstinacy were not My partners in governing the world, as evidenced by the fact that I did not make them witness the creation of the heavens and the earth, nor their own creation, nor did I take them as assistants in governing the world and the Hereafter. Rather, they are like the rest of creation; so why did they venture this corrupt proposal?' Its parallel is that when someone makes grand proposals to you, you say to him: 'You are not the sultan of the land for us to accept these formidable proposals from you; so why did you venture them?' This is reinforced by the fact that the pronoun must return to the nearest mentioned, which in the verse are those disbelievers, because they are what is intended by 'the wrongdoers' in His saying, the Exalted: 'Evil is the substitute for the wrongdoers!'" End quote.

The objection is that this breaks the context by linking the content of the verse to what was addressed in His saying: "and do not obey him whose heart We have made oblivious to Our remembrance" by way of allusion twenty-three verses prior. The direction of the speech has shifted by turning back to the beginning of the Surah time and again through parable after parable and reminder after reminder. Thus, the meaning he suggests is extremely far-fetched.

Furthermore, what he mentioned regarding their proposal to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny): "If you do not expel these poor people from your assembly, we will not believe in you," is not a proposal that involves interference in the governance of the world's affairs such that it would be refuted with the likes of His saying: "I did not make them witness...", etc. Rather, it was a condition for their faith that those people be expelled, without it being based on a claim that would be refuted by such a statement. Yes, if it were said: "Expel these poor people from your assembly," and it were limited to that, there would be some merit to what he said.

It seems that awareness of this point led some to justify the meaning of the verse—on the assumption that the two pronouns return to the disbelievers—by saying the meaning is that they are ignorant of what the Pen has written in pre-eternity regarding the matter of felicity and misery, since they did not witness the creation; so how can they propose to you that you bring them near and expel the poor?

Similar to this is the view of others: that the meaning is: I did not inform them of the secrets of creation, nor were they singled out by Me with that which would distinguish them from others, such that they would be examples followed by people in believing in you; so do not hope for their support, for it is not fitting for Me to take the misleaders as assistants for My religion.

Both views are even further than what the Imam mentioned. Where does the verse indicate the meaning they have fabricated?

Among them is that the two pronouns refer to the angels, and the meaning is: I did not make the angels witness the creation of the world nor their own creation such that they should be worshipped besides Me. It should be added to this that His saying: "nor do I take those who mislead as assistants" also addresses the negation of the guardianship of the satans. The verse would then indicate, through its beginning and end, the negation of the guardianship of both groups together; otherwise, the end of the verse would refute it.

The objection is that the previous verse only addressed the disbelievers regarding their claim of the guardianship of the satans, then mentioned them with the plural pronoun in its saying: "while they are your enemies," and did not address anything regarding the matter of the angels. Thus, returning the two pronouns to the angels instead of the satans is a separation, and preoccupation with negating the guardianship of the angels is addressing something that the context did not necessitate nor the situation require.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ يَقُولُ نادُوا شُرَكائِيَ الَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ — The day He will say, ‘Call those whom you claimed to be My partners!’ (18:52) to the end of the verse. This is a third reminder in which the manifestation of the futility of the link between the polytheists and their "associates" on the Day of Resurrection is mentioned. This confirms that they are not what the polytheists claim them to be.

Thus, His saying: وَيَوْمَ يَقُولُ (The day He will say)—the pronoun refers to Him, the Exalted, as evidenced by the context. The meaning is: and mention to them the day Allah says to them: "Call My partners whom you claimed were My partners." So they called them, but they did not respond to them, and it became clear that they were not My partners; for had they been so, they would have responded.

His saying: وَجَعَلْنا بَيْنَهُمْ مَوْبِقاً — and We shall set a pit of destruction between them. (18:52). The pit of destruction (الموبق) with a kasrah on the ba is a noun of place from wabaqa (وبق), meaning he perished. The meaning is: We placed between the polytheists and their associates a place of destruction. The people have interpreted this "pit of destruction" or "place of destruction" as the Fire, or a place in the Fire where both groups—the polytheists and their associates—perish. However, reflection on His speech, the Exalted, does not support this. For the verse has used "associates" (الشركاء) generally, and among them—perhaps the majority—are the angels and some prophets and saints, and He referred to them with the pronoun for rational beings (أولي العقل) time and again. There is no evidence for restricting them to the rebellious jinn and humans, and the claim that placing the "pit of destruction" between them is evidence for this restriction is the very point in question.

Perhaps what is meant by placing a "pit of destruction" between them is the nullification and removal of the link from between them. They used to see in the world that between them and their associates there was a link of Lordship and servanthood, or cause and effect. This was expressed metaphorically by placing a "pit of destruction" between them in which the link and attachment perish, without the two parties themselves perishing. This is alluded to with a subtle indication by His expressing their calling them first as "shouting" (النداء) where He said: نادُوا شُرَكائِيَ (Call My partners); for shouting (النداء) is only for what is distant, so it is evidence of the distance between them.

A similar meaning is indicated by His saying, the Exalted, in another place in His speech: "We do not see with you your intercessors, those whom you claimed to be partners in [the management of] your [affairs]. Certainly all links between you have been cut, and what you used to claim has forsaken you" (6:94), and His saying, the Exalted: "Then We shall say to those who ascribed partners [to Allah], ‘Stay in your place, you and your partners!’ Then We shall set them apart from one another, and their partners will say, ‘It was not us that you used to worship’" (10:28).

His saying, the Exalted: وَرَأَى الْمُجْرِمُونَ النَّارَ فَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ مُواقِعُوها وَلَمْ يَجِدُوا عَنْها مَصْرِفاً — The guilty will see the Fire and realize that they are about to fall into it, and they will find no way out of it. (18:53). Using "the guilty" (المجرمون) in place of "the polytheists" indicates that the judgment is general for all people of guilt. What is meant by "thought" (الظن) is "certainty" (العلم)—as has been said—and it is evidenced by His saying: "and they will find no way out of it."

What is meant by "falling into the Fire" (مواقعة النار) is falling into it—as has been said. It is not far-fetched that what is meant is the occurrence of falling from both sides: they are falling into the Fire by entering it, and the Fire is falling upon them by burning them.

His saying: وَلَمْ يَجِدُوا عَنْها مَصْرِفاً (and they will find no way out of it)—the way out (المصرف) with a kasrah on the ra is a noun of place from sarf (الصرف), meaning they did not find a place to turn to and divert away from the Fire; there is no escape.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنا فِي هذَا الْقُرْآنِ لِلنَّاسِ مِنْ كُلِّ مَثَلٍ وَكانَ الْإِنْسانُ أَكْثَرَ شَيْءٍ جَدَلاً — Certainly We have elaborated in this Quran every [kind of] parable for mankind, but man is more contentious than anything. (18:54). The discussion on the parallel of the beginning of this verse has passed in Surah al-Isra, verse 89. Contention (الجدل) is speech by way of dispute and quarreling. The verse, up to the completion of six verses, is directed toward threatening with punishment after the previous reminders.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما مَنَعَ النَّاسَ أَنْ يُؤْمِنُوا إِذْ جاءَهُمُ الْهُدى وَيَسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّهُمْ — Nothing prevented the people from believing when guidance came to them and from pleading with their Lord for forgiveness (18:55). "And from pleading for forgiveness" is conjoined to His saying: "from believing." That is: nothing prevented them from faith and seeking forgiveness when guidance arrived.

His saying: إِلَّا أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمْ سُنَّةُ الْأَوَّلِينَ — except that the precedent of the ancients should befall them—means except the seeking that the sunnah current among the ancient nations should come to them, which is the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال). And His saying: أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ الْعَذابُ قُبُلاً — or that the punishment should come to them face to face (18:55) is conjoined to the preceding. That is: or the seeking that the punishment should come to them directly and visibly, at which point faith would not benefit them because it would be faith after witnessing the divine might. The Exalted said: "But their faith did not benefit them when they saw Our might—the precedent of Allah which has passed among His servants" (40:85).

The sum of the meaning is that the people do not seek a faith that benefits them. What they want is for the punishment of extirpation to seize them according to the sunnah of the ancients, so they perish and do not believe; or that the punishment should confront them visibly, so they believe out of necessity, and thus their faith does not benefit them.

This "prevention" and "requirement" in the verse is a rhetorical claim (أمر ادعائي) intended to mean that they are turning away from the truth due to the evil of their inner state. Thus, there is no utility in the lengthy discussions that occurred in the commentaries regarding the correctness of the aforementioned justification and estimation, in terms of objection and refutation.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما نُرْسِلُ الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ — We do not send the apostles except as bearers of good news and as warners (18:56) to the end. This is a consolation for the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) so that his breast should not be straitened by the denial of the deniers and their turning away from the remembrance of Allah. For the function of the messengers was nothing but bearing good news and warning, and they bear no burden beyond that. In this is a return to the likes of what passed in His saying at the beginning of the Surah: "You might perhaps fret yourself to death with grief on their account if they do not believe in this discourse" (18:6). In the verse, there is also a type of threat to the disbelievers who mock.

Perishing (الدحض) is destruction, and causing to perish (الإدحاض) is destroying and nullifying. Mockery (الهزوء): is derision, and the verbal noun is in the sense of the object (اسم المفعول). The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنْ ذُكِّرَ بِآياتِ رَبِّهِ فَأَعْرَضَ عَنْها وَنَسِيَ ما قَدَّمَتْ يَداهُ — Who is a greater wrongdoer than him who is reminded of the signs of his Lord, whereat he disregards them and forgets what his hands have sent ahead? (18:57). This is an aggrandizement and magnification of their wrongdoing. Wrongdoing (الظلم) becomes great and magnificent according to what it relates to; and if it relates to Allah, the Glorified, through His signs, then it is greater than any other wrongdoing.

What is meant by "forgetting what his hands have sent ahead" is his lack of concern for the turning away from the truth and mocking it that he commits, while he knows it is the truth. His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْنا عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَنْ يَفْقَهُوهُ وَفِي آذانِهِمْ وَقْراً — Indeed We have cast veils over their hearts lest they should understand it, and a deafness into their ears (18:57) is as if it were a justification for their turning away from the signs of Allah, or for both that and their forgetting what their hands sent ahead. The discussion on the meaning of placing veils (أكنة) over their hearts and deafness (وقراً) in their ears has preceded in the Book repeatedly.

His saying: وَإِنْ تَدْعُهُمْ إِلَى الْهُدى فَلَنْ يَهْتَدُوا إِذاً أَبَداً — and if you invite them to guidance, they will never be guided (18:57) is an expression of despair regarding their faith after Allah has struck the veil over their hearts and ears. It is not possible for them after that to be guided by themselves through understanding the truth, nor to be guided by the guidance of others through hearing and following. The evidence for this meaning is His saying: "they will never be guided," as it indicates the permanence of the negation and restricts it with His saying: إِذاً (then), which is a recompense and a response.

It was said in Ruh al-Ma'ani: "The Necessitarians (الجبرية) used this verse as evidence for their school of thought, and the Voluntarists (القدرية) used the verse before it. The Imam said: 'Rarely do you find in the Quran a verse for one of these two groups except that there is a verse for the other group with it. This is nothing but a severe trial from Allah, the Exalted, which Allah has cast upon His servants so that the firmly rooted scholars may be distinguished from the imitators.'" End quote.

I say: Both verses are true. The necessary consequence of that is the establishment of choice (الاختيار) for the servants in their deeds, and the extension of His, the Exalted, authority in His kingdom even over the deeds of the servants. This is the school of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them).

His saying, the Exalted: وَرَبُّكَ الْغَفُورُ ذُو الرَّحْمَةِ — Your Lord is the All-forgiving, possessor of mercy (18:58) to the end of the verse. The verses—as you have heard—are arranged to threaten them with punishment, for they are corrupt in their deeds with a corruption from which no reform is hoped. This necessitates the descent of punishment and that it be hastened without granting them respite, since there is no effect to their remaining except corruption. However, Allah, the Glorified, did not hasten the punishment for them, even though He decreed it with a final decree; rather, He delayed it to an appointed term (أجل مسمى) which He specified by His knowledge.

Thus, His saying: وَرَبُّكَ الْغَفُورُ ذُو الرَّحْمَةِ (Your Lord is the All-forgiving, possessor of mercy) began the verse containing the explicit decree in threatening them, so that it might balance—by virtue of its containing the two attributes: the All-forgiving, the possessor of mercy—that which necessitates hastened punishment. Thus, He decrees and executes the essence of the punishment as a fulfillment of the right of its necessitating cause, which is their deed, but He delays its occurrence because Allah is All-forgiving, possessor of mercy.

So the sentence, meaning His saying: "the All-forgiving, possessor of mercy," along with His saying: لَوْ يُؤاخِذُهُمْ بِما كَسَبُوا لَعَجَّلَ لَهُمُ الْعَذابَ — Were He to take them to task for what they have earned, He would have surely hastened the punishment for them (18:58), are like two disputing litigants appearing before a judge. And His saying: بَلْ لَهُمْ مَوْعِدٌ لَنْ يَجِدُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ مَوْئِلاً — But they have a tryst from which they will find no refuge (18:58)—meaning a shelter they can flee to from Him—is like the judgment issued by him, containing the satisfaction of both sides and the observation of both rights. Thus, the attribute of divine vengeance was given the essence of the punishment due to the requirement of what they earned; and the attribute of forgiveness and mercy was given that the punishment be delayed and not hastened. At that point, divine forgiveness began to erase the effect of the deed which is the hastening of punishment, and mercy began to pour upon them a temporary life.

The sum of the meaning: If your Lord were to take them to task, He would have hastened the punishment for them, but He did not hasten it because He is the All-forgiving, possessor of mercy. Rather, He made the punishment inevitable for them by appointing for them a tryst from which they have no refuge. Thus, His saying: بَلْ لَهُمْ مَوْعِدٌ (But they have a tryst), etc., is a word of decree (كلمة قضاء) and not a mere narration; otherwise, it would have been said: "rather, He made for them a tryst," etc. Understand that.

"The All-forgiving" (الغفور) is an intensive form (صيغة مبالغة) indicating the abundance of forgiveness. "Possessor of mercy" (ذو الرحمة)—with the definite article for the genus—is an attribute indicating the encompassment of mercy for everything. Thus, it is more comprehensive in meaning than "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن) and "the Most Merciful" (الرحيم), which indicate abundance or stability and continuity. So "the All-forgiving" is like a servant to the "possessor of mercy," for he prepares the subject for the possessor of mercy by erasing the stain of obstacles upon it. Once it is prepared, the possessor of mercy encompasses it. Thus, for the All-forgiving there is striving and abundance of action, and for the possessor of mercy there is extension and encompassment over that which has no obstacle. For this subtle point, "the All-forgiving" was used for forgiveness, which is an intensive form, and "possessor of mercy" was used for mercy, containing the genus of mercy. Understand this and leave aside the lengthy discussions they have made regarding the two names.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتِلْكَ الْقُرى أَهْلَكْناهُمْ لَمَّا ظَلَمُوا وَجَعَلْنا لِمَهْلِكِهِمْ مَوْعِداً — And [the people of] these towns—We destroyed them when they were wrongdoers, and We appointed a tryst for their destruction. (18:59). What is meant by "the towns" (القرى) is their people metaphorically, as evidenced by the pronouns returning to them. The destruction (المهلك) with a kasrah on the lam is a noun of time.

The meaning of the verse is clear. it is directed toward explaining that the delay of their destruction and its postponement is not an innovation from Us; rather, the divine sunnah among the past nations whom Allah destroyed when they did wrong was current upon that. Thus, Allah would destroy them and appoint a tryst for their destruction.

From here it becomes clear that the punishment and destruction contained in the verses is not the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, but rather a worldly punishment. It is the punishment of the Day of Badr if the intention is to threaten the leaders of the Quraysh, or the punishment of the end of time if the intention is to threaten the Ummah, as passed in the commentary of Surah Yunus.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: يا وَيْلَتَنا ما لِهذَا الْكِتابِ (Woe to us! What a book is this!), the verse: narrated from Khalid ibn Najih from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "When it is the Day of Resurrection, a person is given his book—then it is said to him: 'Read.'" I said: "Does he recognize what is in it?" He said: "He remembers it; there is not a moment, nor a word, nor a step taken, nor anything he did—except that he remembers it as if he had done it at that very hour. For that reason they said: 'Woe to us! What a book is this! It leaves out nothing, small or great, but has counted it.'"

I say: The narration, as you see, makes what the person remembers to be what he recognized from that Book. Thus, what he remembers is what is written in it. Had his deed not been present, the argument would not have been completed against him, and it would have been possible for him to deny it.

In Tafsir al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَلا يَظْلِمُ رَبُّكَ أَحَداً (and your Lord does not wrong anyone), he said: "They find everything they did written down."

In Tafsir al-Burhan: from Ibn Babuwayh with his chain from Abu Ma'mar al-Sa'dan from Ali (peace be upon him), who said: "His saying: وَرَأَى الْمُجْرِمُونَ النَّارَ فَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ مُواقِعُوها (The guilty will see the Fire and realize that they are about to fall into it)—meaning they were certain they were entering it."
