In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ahmad, Abu Ya'la, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Hibban, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny), who said: "The disbeliever will be made to stand on the Day of Resurrection for a duration of fifty thousand years—as he did not work in the world. And the disbeliever will see Hell—and think that it is falling upon him from a distance of forty years' journey."

I say: This supports what preceded, that the "falling" (المواقعة) in the verse is taken as being between two parties.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِفَتَىٰهُ لَآ أَبْرَحُ حَتَّىٰٓ أَبْلُغَ مَجْمَعَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ أَوْ أَمْضِىَ حُقُبًا (٦٠)
When Moses said to his lad, ‘I will go on [journeying] until I have reached the confluence of the two seas, or have spent a long time [travelling].’ (60)
فَلَمَّا بَلَغَا مَجْمَعَ بَيْنِهِمَا نَسِيَا حُوتَهُمَا فَٱتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُۥ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ سَرَبًا (٦١)
So when they reached the confluence between them, they forgot their fish, which found its way into the sea, sneaking away. (61)
فَلَمَّا جَاوَزَا قَالَ لِفَتَىٰهُ ءَاتِنَا غَدَآءَنَا لَقَدْ لَقِينَا مِن سَفَرِنَا هَـٰذَا نَصَبًا (٦٢)
So when they had passed on, he said to his lad, ‘Bring us our meal. We have certainly encountered much fatigue on this journey of ours.’ (62)
قَالَ أَرَءَيْتَ إِذْ أَوَيْنَآ إِلَى ٱلصَّخْرَةِ فَإِنِّى نَسِيتُ ٱلْحُوتَ وَمَآ أَنسَىٰنِيهُ إِلَّا ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَنْ أَذْكُرَهُۥ ۚ وَٱتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُۥ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ عَجَبًا (٦٣)
He said, ‘Did you see?! When we took shelter at the rock, indeed I forgot about the fish—and none but Satan made me forget to mention it!—and it made its way into the sea in an amazing manner!’ (63)
قَالَ ذَٰلِكَ مَا كُنَّا نَبْغِ ۚ فَٱرْتَدَّا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَارِهِمَا قَصَصًا (٦٤)
He said, ‘That is what we were after!’ So they returned, retracing their footsteps. (64)
فَوَجَدَا عَبْدًا مِّنْ عِبَادِنَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا وَعَلَّمْنَـٰهُ مِن لَّدُنَّا عِلْمًا (٦٥)
[There] they found one of Our servants whom We had granted a mercy from Ourselves, and taught him a knowledge from Our own. (65)
قَالَ لَهُۥ مُوسَىٰ هَلْ أَتَّبِعُكَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن تُعَلِّمَنِ مِمَّا عُلِّمْتَ رُشْدًا (٦٦)
Moses said to him, ‘May I follow you for the purpose that you teach me some of the probity you have been taught?’ (66)
قَالَ إِنَّكَ لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِىَ صَبْرًا (٦٧)
He said, ‘Indeed you cannot have patience with me! (67)
وَكَيْفَ تَصْبِرُ عَلَىٰ مَا لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِۦ خُبْرًا (٦٨)
And how can you have patience about something you do not comprehend?’ (68)
قَالَ سَتَجِدُنِىٓ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ صَابِرًا وَلَآ أَعْصِى لَكَ أَمْرًا (٦٩)
He said, ‘You will find me, God willing, to be patient, and I will not disobey you in any matter.’ (69)
قَالَ فَإِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَنِى فَلَا تَسْـَٔلْنِى عَن شَىْءٍ حَتَّىٰٓ أُحْدِثَ لَكَ مِنْهُ ذِكْرًا (٧٠)
He said, ‘If you follow me, do not question me concerning anything until I myself first mention it for you.’ (70)
فَٱنطَلَقَا حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا رَكِبَا فِى ٱلسَّفِينَةِ خَرَقَهَا ۖ قَالَ أَخَرَقْتَهَا لِتُغْرِقَ أَهْلَهَا لَقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْـًٔا إِمْرًا (٧١)
So they went on and when they boarded the boat, he made a hole in it. He said, ‘Did you make a hole in it to drown its people? You have certainly done a monstrous thing!’ (71)
قَالَ أَلَمْ أَقُلْ إِنَّكَ لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِىَ صَبْرًا (٧٢)
He said, ‘Did I not say that you cannot have patience with me?’ (72)
قَالَ لَا تُؤَاخِذْنِى بِمَا نَسِيتُ وَلَا تُرْهِقْنِى مِنْ أَمْرِى عُسْرًا (٧٣)
He said, ‘Do not take me to task for my forgetting, and do not be hard upon me.’ (73)
فَٱنطَلَقَا حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا لَقِيَا غُلَـٰمًا فَقَتَلَهُۥ قَالَ أَقَتَلْتَ نَفْسًا زَكِيَّةًۢ بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ لَّقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْـًٔا نُّكْرًا (٧٤)
So they went on until they came upon a boy, whereat he slew him. He said, ‘Did you slay an innocent soul, without [his having slain] anyone? You have certainly done a dire thing!’ (74)
۞ قَالَ أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكَ إِنَّكَ لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِىَ صَبْرًا (٧٥)
He said, ‘Did I not tell you that you cannot have patience with me?’ (75)
قَالَ إِن سَأَلْتُكَ عَن شَىْءٍۭ بَعْدَهَا فَلَا تُصَـٰحِبْنِى ۖ قَدْ بَلَغْتَ مِن لَّدُنِّى عُذْرًا (٧٦)
He said, ‘If I question you about anything after this, do not keep me in your company. You already have enough excuse on my part.’ (76)
فَٱنطَلَقَا حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَتَيَآ أَهْلَ قَرْيَةٍ ٱسْتَطْعَمَآ أَهْلَهَا فَأَبَوْا۟ أَن يُضَيِّفُوهُمَا فَوَجَدَا فِيهَا جِدَارًا يُرِيدُ أَن يَنقَضَّ فَأَقَامَهُۥ ۖ قَالَ لَوْ شِئْتَ لَتَّخَذْتَ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا (٧٧)
So they went on until they came to the people of a town. They asked its people for food, but they refused to extend them any hospitality. There they found a wall which was about to collapse, so he erected it. He said, ‘Had you wished, you could have taken a wage for it.’ (77)
قَالَ هَـٰذَا فِرَاقُ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنِكَ ۚ سَأُنَبِّئُكَ بِتَأْوِيلِ مَا لَمْ تَسْتَطِع عَّلَيْهِ صَبْرًا (٧٨)
He said, ‘This is where you and I shall part. I will inform you about the interpretation of that over which you could not maintain patience. (78)
أَمَّا ٱلسَّفِينَةُ فَكَانَتْ لِمَسَـٰكِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ فَأَرَدتُّ أَنْ أَعِيبَهَا وَكَانَ وَرَآءَهُم مَّلِكٌ يَأْخُذُ كُلَّ سَفِينَةٍ غَصْبًا (٧٩)
As for the boat, it belonged to some poor people who work on the sea. I wanted to make it defective, for behind them was a king seizing every ship usurpingly. (79)
وَأَمَّا ٱلْغُلَـٰمُ فَكَانَ أَبَوَاهُ مُؤْمِنَيْنِ فَخَشِينَآ أَن يُرْهِقَهُمَا طُغْيَـٰنًا وَكُفْرًا (٨٠)
As for the boy, his parents were faithful [persons], and We feared he would overwhelm them with rebellion and unfaith. (80)
فَأَرَدْنَآ أَن يُبْدِلَهُمَا رَبُّهُمَا خَيْرًا مِّنْهُ زَكَوٰةً وَأَقْرَبَ رُحْمًا (٨١)
So We desired that their Lord should give them in exchange one better than him in respect of purity and closer in mercy. (81)
وَأَمَّا ٱلْجِدَارُ فَكَانَ لِغُلَـٰمَيْنِ يَتِيمَيْنِ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ وَكَانَ تَحْتَهُۥ كَنزٌ لَّهُمَا وَكَانَ أَبُوهُمَا صَـٰلِحًا فَأَرَادَ رَبُّكَ أَن يَبْلُغَآ أَشُدَّهُمَا وَيَسْتَخْرِجَا كَنزَهُمَا رَحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۚ وَمَا فَعَلْتُهُۥ عَنْ أَمْرِى ۚ ذَٰلِكَ تَأْوِيلُ مَا لَمْ تَسْطِع عَّلَيْهِ صَبْرًا (٨٢)
As for the wall, it belonged to two boy orphans in the city. Under it there was a treasure belonging to them. Their father had been a righteous man. So your Lord desired that they should come of age and take out their treasure—as a mercy from your Lord. I did not do that out of my own accord. This is the interpretation of that over which you could not maintain patience.’ (82)

(Commentary)

The story of Moses and the learned man (العالم) whom he met at the junction of the two seas (مجمع البحرين), and who possessed knowledge of the inner reality (تأويل) of events, is mentioned by Allah, the Glorified, to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This is the fourth of the reminders occurring after what He commanded him at the beginning of the Surah regarding patience and perseverance in conveying His message, and offering solace for the aversion of the people from the remembrance of Allah and their turning toward the world. He clarified that what they are occupied with is a fleeting adornment (زينة معجلة) and a temporary enjoyment (متاع إلى حين). Therefore, he should not be distressed by the outward display of their enjoyment of life and their attainment of what they desire therein, for behind this outward appearance (الظاهر) lies an inner reality (باطن), and above their authority over their desires lies a Divine Sovereignty (سلطنة إلهية).

Thus, the reminder through the story of Moses and the learned man is as if to indicate that these occurrences and events, which proceed according to the desires of the people of the world, have an inner reality (تأويل) that will be manifested to them when the Decree (الكتاب) reaches its term. Then, Allah will permit them to awaken from the slumber of heedlessness (الغفلة) and they will be resurrected for a realm (نشأة) other than this realm, on the day when its fulfillment (تأويله) comes; those who had forgotten it before will say: "The apostles of our Lord had certainly brought the truth."

The Moses mentioned in the story is the son of Amram (ابن عمران), the Messenger, the Prophet, and one of the Arch-Prophets (أولي العزم) — the possessors of great resolve and constancy — (عليه‌السلام), according to what has been narrated in reports from both the Shi‘a and Sunni paths.

It has been said that he was one of the descendants of Joseph, son of Jacob (عليه‌السلام), namely Moses son of Manasseh (ميشا) son of Joseph, and he was among the prophets of the Children of Israel. This is rendered unlikely by the fact that the Quran mentions the name of Moses more than one hundred and thirty times, referring to the son of Amram (عليه‌السلام). Had someone else been intended in this story, a diverting context (قرينة صارفة) would have been attached to it.

It has also been said that the story is a symbolic legend (أسطورة تخييلية) depicted for the purpose of showing that the perfection of gnosis (المعرفة) brings man to the watering place of the Fountain of Life (عين الحياة) and gives him its water to drink, which is the eternal life (الحياة الخالدة) after which there is no death, and the everlasting felicity (السعادة السرمدية) above which there is no felicity. This view is an estimation without evidence, and the outward meaning of the Mighty Book refutes it. There is no mention in the story narrated by the Quran of this "Fountain of Life" except what has appeared in the sayings of some commentators and storytellers among the historians, without any Quranic basis to rely upon or any sensory perception (وجدان حسي) of a fountain with such attributes in any region of the earth.

As for the youth (الفتى) whom Allah mentioned and associated with Moses, it is said that he was Joshua son of Nun (يوشع بن نون), his successor (وصيه), as stated in the narrations. It is said he was called a "youth" (فتى) because he accompanied him in travel and residence, or because he served him.

The learned man (العالم) whom Moses met, and whom Allah described beautifully by saying: عَبْداً مِنْ عِبادِنا آتَيْناهُ رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِنا وَعَلَّمْناهُ مِنْ لَدُنَّا عِلْماً — "one of Our servants whom We had granted a mercy from Ourselves, and whom We had taught a knowledge from Our own presence" — but did not name, is identified in the narrations as al-Khidr (الخضر). He was a prophet among the prophets, contemporary with Moses (عليه‌السلام). In some reports, it is mentioned that Allah granted him longevity, so he is alive and has not yet died. There is no harm in this amount [of belief], as neither reason (عقل) nor definitive transmitted evidence (نقل قطعي) has established the contrary. Extensive research has been conducted regarding the personality of al-Khidr among scholars, as seen in the lengthy commentaries, and stories and anecdotes regarding sightings of him have multiplied. Nevertheless, these reports and stories are not free from fabricated or interpolated legends (أساطير موضوعة أو مدسوسة).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قالَ مُوسى لِفَتاهُ لا أَبْرَحُ حَتَّى أَبْلُغَ مَجْمَعَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ أَوْ أَمْضِيَ حُقُباً — When Moses said to his lad, ‘I will not give up until I reach the junction of the two seas, or [until] I spend a long time.’ (18:60). The adverbial phrase (الظرف) relates to an implied [verb]. The sentence is coordinated with the previous three reminders mentioned earlier. His saying: لا أَبْرَحُ (I will not give up) means "I will not cease," and it is one of the incomplete verbs (الأفعال الناقصة) whose predicate (خبر) has been omitted for brevity, as indicated by his saying: حَتَّى أَبْلُغَ (until I reach). The implied meaning is: "I will not cease walking or traveling." The junction of the two seas (مجمع البحرين) is said to be where the Roman Sea (Mediterranean) ends on the eastern side and the Persian Sea (Red Sea/Gulf) ends on the western side. Hukub (حُقُب) refers to an age or a long period of time, and its indefinite form (تنكيره) indicates a deleted adjective; the implied meaning is "a long age."

The meaning — and Allah knows best — is: And remember when Moses said to his youth: "I shall not cease traveling until I reach the junction of the two seas, or I pass a long age."

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا بَلَغا مَجْمَعَ بَيْنِهِما نَسِيا حُوتَهُما فَاتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُ فِي الْبَحْرِ سَرَباً — But when they reached the junction between the two, they forgot their fish, and it took its way into the sea, burrowing [into it]. (18:61). It appears that His saying: مَجْمَعَ بَيْنِهِما (the junction between the two) is an instance of adding the attribute to the described (إضافة الصفة إلى الموصوف), and its origin is "between the two seas described as their junction."

His saying: نَسِيا حُوتَهُما (they forgot their fish): the following two verses indicate that it was a salted or roasted fish they carried for sustenance during the journey. It was not alive, but it came to life there and took its way into the sea. The youth saw it while it was alive, diving into the sea, but he forgot to mention that to Moses, and Moses forgot to ask him where it was. Based on this, the meaning of نَسِيا حُوتَهُما (they forgot their fish), by attributing the forgetting to both of them, is: they forgot the state of their fish. Moses forgot its presence in the basket (المكتل) and thus did not check on it, and the youth forgot it in that he did not inform Moses of the wondrous thing he saw regarding it. This is what they [the commentators] have mentioned.

Know that the verses are not explicit regarding the fish coming to life after being dead. Rather, the outward meaning of His saying: نَسِيا حُوتَهُما (they forgot their fish), as well as His saying: نَسِيتُ الْحُوتَ (I forgot the fish), suggests that they had placed it in a spot on the rock overlooking the sea, and it fell into the sea or the sea took it by a tide or the like, so it disappeared in it and sank into its depths in a wondrous manner, like entering a burrow (سرب). This is supported by what is in some narrations that the sign (العلامة) was the loss of the fish, not its coming to life. And Allah knows best.

His saying: فَاتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُ فِي الْبَحْرِ سَرَباً (and it took its way into the sea, burrowing): Sarab (سرب) is a path or a course. A sarab or a tunnel (نفق) is a path dug into the earth with no exit. It is as if the path the fish took inside the water was likened to a burrow (سرب) that a traveler enters and disappears within.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا جاوَزا قالَ لِفَتاهُ آتِنا غَداءَنا لَقَدْ لَقِينا مِنْ سَفَرِنا هذا نَصَباً — When they had passed on, he said to his lad, ‘Bring us our meal. We have certainly encountered much fatigue on this journey of ours.’ (18:62). It is said in al-Majma‘: Nasab (نَصَب), wasab (وَصَب), and ta‘ab (تَعَب) are synonyms, referring to the weakness that results from toil. What is meant by ghada’ (غداء) is the meal eaten at midday, which indicates that this occurred during the day.

The meaning is: When they passed the junction of the two seas, Moses commanded his youth to bring the midday meal, which was the fish they had carried to eat, for they had encountered exhaustion from their journey.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ أَرَأَيْتَ إِذْ أَوَيْنا إِلَى الصَّخْرَةِ — He said, ‘Did you see, when we took refuge on the rock...’ (18:63) to the end of the verse. He refers to the time they reached the junction of the two seas and stayed there, for the rock was there. The evidence for this is his saying: وَاتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُ (and it took its way...), and it was mentioned previously that this was at the junction of the two seas. He says to Moses: "We have no meal to eat, for our meal—the fish—came to life, entered the sea, and went away when we reached the junction of the two seas and took refuge at the rock that was there, and I forgot to inform you of that."

So his saying: أَرَأَيْتَ إِذْ أَوَيْنا إِلَى الصَّخْرَةِ (Did you see, when we took refuge on the rock) reminds him of the state of their taking refuge at the rock and stopping there to rest a little. His saying: فإني نَسِيتُ الْحُوتَ (for I forgot the fish) means: "I forgot the state of the fish which I witnessed, so I did not mention it to you." The evidence for this meaning—as has been said—is His saying: وَما أَنْسانِيهُ إِلَّا الشَّيْطانُ أَنْ أَذْكُرَهُ (and none but Satan made me forget to mention it), for أَنْ أَذْكُرَهُ (to mention it) is an appositive (بدل) for the pronoun in أَنْسانِيهُ (made me forget it).

The implied meaning is: "None but Satan made me forget the mentioning of the fish to you." Thus, he did not forget the fish itself, but rather forgot to mention the state he witnessed of it to Moses.

There is no harm in the youth attributing his forgetting to an act of Satan, even assuming he was Joshua son of Nun, the Prophet. Prophets are under Divine protection (عصمة) from Satan in that they are infallible (معصومون) regarding anything that leads to sin (معصية). As for the absolute harm or annoyance (إيذاء) from Satan in matters not relating to sin, there is no evidence to prevent it. The Exalted said: وَاذْكُرْ عَبْدَنا أَيُّوبَ إِذْ نادى رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الشَّيْطانُ بِنُصْبٍ وَعَذابٍ — "And remember Our servant Job, when he called out to his Lord, ‘The Satan has visited me with hardship and torment’" (38:41).

His saying: وَاتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُ فِي الْبَحْرِ عَجَباً (and it took its way into the sea wondrously): meaning "a wondrous taking" (اتخاذاً عجباً), so "wondrously" (عجباً) is an adjective standing in place of its described noun as a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق). It has been said that وَاتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُ فِي الْبَحْرِ (and it took its way into the sea) is the speech of the youth, and عَجَباً (wondrously!) is the speech of Moses, but the context refutes this.

Know that the possibility mentioned previously regarding His saying: نَسِيا حُوتَهُما (they forgot their fish) etc., applies here as well. And Allah knows best.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ ذلِكَ ما كُنَّا نَبْغِ فَارْتَدَّا عَلى آثارِهِما قَصَصاً — He said, ‘That is what we were seeking!’ So they returned, retracing their footsteps. (18:64). Baghy (البغي) is seeking (الطلب). Artidad (الارتداد) is returning to the starting point. What is meant by athar (الآثار) is their footprints. Qasas (القصص) is following the tracks. The meaning is: Moses said, "That which occurred regarding the fish is exactly what we were seeking." So they returned upon their footprints, retracing them and following them closely.

His saying: ذلِكَ ما كُنَّا نَبْغِ فَارْتَدَّا (That is what we were seeking! So they returned) reveals that Moses had been commanded through revelation (الوحي) to meet the learned man at the junction of the two seas, and the sign (العلامة) of the place where he would find and meet him was what occurred regarding the fish—either specifically the matter of its coming to life and going into the sea, or in a vague and general sense such as the loss of the fish, its coming to life, or the dead returning to life, and the like. Therefore, when Moses heard from his youth what he heard regarding the fish, he said what he said, and they returned to the place they had left, where they found "a servant..." etc.

His saying, the Exalted: فَوَجَدا عَبْداً مِنْ عِبادِنا آتَيْناهُ رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِنا — [There] they found one of Our servants whom We had granted a mercy from Ourselves... (18:65) etc. Every blessing (نعمة) is a mercy (رحمة) from Him, the Exalted, to His creation. However, some blessings are mediated by cosmic causes (الأسباب الكونية) which act upon them, such as all types of outward blessings. Others are not mediated by any of these, such as inward blessings (النعم الباطنية) like prophethood (النبوة) and guardianship (الولاية) with their various branches and stations. Restricting the mercy with the phrase مِنْ عِنْدِنا (from Ourselves)—which suggests it is from His direct bestowal (موهبته) with no role for others—indicates that it belongs to the second category, namely inward blessings. Furthermore, the fact that guardianship (الولاية) in its true essence is exclusive to Him, the Exalted, as He said: فَاللهُ هُوَ الْوَلِيُّ — "it is Allah who is the Guardian" (42:9), while prophethood is something in which the noble angels have a role, such as revelation (الوحي) and the like, supports the view that the intended meaning of رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِنا (a mercy from Ourselves)—where the "nun" of grandeur (نون العظمة) is used and He did not say "from Me"—is prophethood rather than guardianship. This supports the interpretation of those who explained the word as prophethood. And Allah knows best.

As for His saying: وَعَلَّمْناهُ مِنْ لَدُنَّا عِلْماً (and whom We had taught a knowledge from Our own presence), it is also like the mercy from Him: a knowledge (علم) in which ordinary causes (الأسباب العادية) like the senses and thought have no role, such that it would be acquired through effort (الاكتساب). The evidence for this is His saying: مِنْ لَدُنَّا (from Our own presence). Thus, it is a bestowed knowledge (علم وهبي), not an acquired one (اكتسابي), which He reserves for His friends (أولياءه). The end of the verses indicates that it was knowledge of the inner reality (تأويل) of events.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ لَهُ مُوسى هَلْ أَتَّبِعُكَ عَلى أَنْ تُعَلِّمَنِ مِمَّا عُلِّمْتَ رُشْداً — Moses said to him, ‘May I follow you on [the condition] that you teach me some of the rectitude you have been taught?’ (18:66). Rushd (الرشد) is the opposite of error (الغي), and it means attaining what is correct (إصابة الصواب). In the verse, it is either a causal object (مفعول له) or a direct object (مفعول به). The meaning is: Moses said to him, "May I follow you with a following based on this foundation: that you teach me from what you have been taught so that I may be guided (أرشد) by it, or that you teach me from what you have been taught a matter possessing rectitude (رشد)."

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِيَ صَبْراً — He said, ‘Indeed you cannot have patience with me.’ (18:67). This is an emphasized negation of his (عليه‌السلام) patience regarding anything he would witness from him during the course of instruction. The evidence for this is the emphasis of the speech with إِنَّ (indeed), and the use of "patience" (صَبْراً) as an indefinite noun in the context of negation, which indicates intended generality (العموم). Furthermore, the negation of patience is through the negation of "capacity" (الاستطاعة), which is power (القدرة); this is more emphatic than saying "you will not be patient." The use of the negation لَنْ (never/cannot) instead of "you do not have patience" is significant, as an action depends on power. Thus, it is a negation of the action by negating one of its causes. Then, the negation of patience is further explained by the negation of the cause of the power for it, which is the comprehensive knowledge (إحاطة الخبر) and understanding of the reality of the event and its inner meaning (تأويل), such that one knows it must proceed as it did.

He negated his patience regarding the manifestations of his knowledge in events when he said: لَنْ تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِيَ (you cannot [have patience] with me), but he did not negate his patience regarding the knowledge itself. He did not say: "You will not be patient with what I know" or "You will not bear it." Moses (عليه‌السلام) did not change his attitude toward him when he informed him of the inner reality (تأويل) of what he saw from him; rather, he changed when witnessing the acts themselves which he showed him during the course of instruction. Thus, knowledge has one ruling, and its manifestations have another. A similar case is when Moses (عليه‌السلام) returned from the appointed time (الميقات) to his people and saw that they had worshipped the calf after him; he was filled with rage, threw down the tablets, and seized his brother by the head, dragging him toward himself. Yet Allah had already informed him of that while he was at the appointed time, but he did not do any of those things then, even though the word of Allah is truer than sensory perception. The story is in Surah al-A‘raf.

So His saying: إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِيَ (Indeed you cannot [have patience] with me) etc., is an announcement that he would not be able to endure the path he would take in his instruction if he followed him, not that he could not bear the knowledge itself.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَيْفَ تَصْبِرُ عَلى ما لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِ خُبْراً — ‘How can you have patience about something you have not encompassed in [your] knowledge?’ (18:68). Khubr (الخبر) is knowledge (العلم), and it is a specification (تمييز). The meaning is: your knowledge does not encompass it.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ صابِراً وَلا أَعْصِي لَكَ أَمْراً — He said, ‘You will find me, Allah willing, to be patient, and I will not disobey you in any matter.’ (18:69). He promised him patience but conditioned it upon the Divine Will (المشية); thus, he did not lie when he failed to be patient. His saying: وَلا أَعْصِي (and I will not disobey) etc., is coordinated with صابِراً (patient) because of the verbal meaning it carries. Thus, the lack of disobedience he promised is also conditioned upon the Divine Will, and he did not break the promise when he did not stop himself from questioning despite the prohibition.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ فَإِنِ اتَّبَعْتَنِي فَلا تَسْئَلْنِي عَنْ شَيْءٍ حَتَّى أُحْدِثَ لَكَ مِنْهُ ذِكْراً — He said, ‘If you follow me, do not question me about anything until I mention it to you.’ (18:70). It appears that مِنْهُ (of it) relates to His saying: ذِكْراً (a mention). "Initiating a mention" (إحداث الذكر) of something means starting it without prior prompt. The meaning is: "If you follow me, do not ask me about anything you witness of my affairs that is difficult for you to witness, until I myself initiate a mention of it." In this is an indication that he would witness things difficult for him to see, and he [the learned man] would explain them to him, but it is not appropriate for Moses to initiate the questioning and inquiry; rather, he should be patient until he [the learned man] initiates the explanation.

Moses (عليه‌السلام) displayed a wondrous degree of character (الخلق) and exquisite etiquette (الأدب البارع) befitting a student seeking benefit before al-Khidr—as these verses recount—and he is the Interlocutor of Allah (كليم الله), Moses son of Amram, the Messenger, the Prophet, one of the Arch-Prophets (أولي العزم), and the possessor of the Torah.

His speech is built upon humility (التواضع) from beginning to end. He first showed etiquette with him by not presenting his request for instruction in the form of a command, but rather in the form of an inquiry, out of self-effacement (هضماً لنفسه). He called his companionship "following" (اتباعاً) him. Then, he did not present the instruction in the form of a condition, but said: "on [the condition] that you teach me" etc. Then, he considered himself a learner. Then, he magnified the status of his knowledge by attributing it to an unknown source (مبدإ غير معلوم) which he did not specify by name or attribute, saying: عُلِّمْتَ (you have been taught) and did not say "you learned." Then, he praised it by saying: رُشْداً (rectitude). Then, he made what he would learn only a part of his knowledge, saying: مِمَّا عُلِّمْتَ (some of what you have been taught) and did not say "what you have been taught." Then, he raised his status by making what he would suggest to him a "command" (أمر) and considered himself a "disobedient" (عاصياً) one if he opposed him in what he commanded. Then, he did not indulge in an explicit promise, but alluded to it with the like of his saying: سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللهُ صابِراً وَلا أَعْصِي لَكَ أَمْراً (You will find me, Allah willing, to be patient, and I will not disobey you in any matter).

Al-Khidr also showed etiquette with him, as he did not explicitly reject him at first, but alluded to it by negating his capacity for patience. Then, when Moses promised him patience "Allah willing," he did not command him to follow, but left the choice to him, saying: فَإِنِ اتَّبَعْتَنِي (If you follow me). Then, he did not forbid him from questioning with an absolute prohibition in the form of pure lordship (المولوية المحضة), but conditioned it upon his following, saying: فَإِنِ اتَّبَعْتَنِي فَلا تَسْئَلْنِي (If you follow me, do not question me), so as to indicate that he was not imposing the prohibition arbitrarily, but rather it was a matter necessitated by the following itself.

His saying, the Exalted: فَانْطَلَقا حَتَّى إِذا رَكِبا فِي السَّفِينَةِ خَرَقَها قالَ أَخَرَقْتَها لِتُغْرِقَ أَهْلَها لَقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْئاً إِمْراً — So they went on until, when they boarded the ship, he made a hole in it. He said, ‘Did you make a hole in it to drown its people? You have certainly done a monstrous thing!’ (18:71). Imr (إِمْر), with a kasra on the hamza, refers to a great calamity (الداهية العظيمة). His saying: فَانْطَلَقا (So they went on) is a consequence (تفريع) of what preceded it. The two who went on were Moses and al-Khidr, which clearly shows that Moses did not take his youth with him in his journey with al-Khidr. The "lam" in His saying: لِتُغْرِقَ أَهْلَها (to drown its people) is for the consequence (للغاية); for although drowning was a consequence of the hole and al-Khidr certainly did not intend it, a necessary consequence is sometimes treated as an intended goal for the sake of clarity, as one might say: "Do you do such-and-such to destroy yourself?" The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ أَلَمْ أَقُلْ إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِيَ صَبْراً — He said, ‘Did I not say, ‘‘Indeed you cannot have patience with me’’?’ (18:72). This is a disapproval of Moses's question and a reminder of what he had said before: إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَسْتَطِيعَ (Indeed you cannot...) etc.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ لا تُؤاخِذْنِي بِما نَسِيتُ وَلا تُرْهِقْنِي مِنْ أَمْرِي عُسْراً — He said, ‘Do not take me to task for what I forgot, and do not be hard on me.’ (18:73). Rahaq (الرهق) is being overcome by force, and irhaq (الإرهاق) is imposing a burden. The meaning is: "Do not take me to task for my forgetting the promise and my heedlessness of it, and do not impose a difficulty upon me in my affair." Sometimes "forgetting" (النسيان) is interpreted as "abandoning" (الترك), but the first is more apparent. The speech is an apology in any case.

His saying, the Exalted: فَانْطَلَقا حَتَّى إِذا لَقِيا غُلاماً فَقَتَلَهُ قالَ أَقَتَلْتَ نَفْساً زَكِيَّةً بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ لَقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْئاً نُكْراً — So they went on until they met a boy, and he killed him. He said, ‘Did you kill an innocent soul, without [his having killed] anyone? You have certainly done a horrible thing!’ (18:74). There is some omission in the speech for brevity; the implied meaning is: "So they left the ship and went on."

In His saying: حَتَّى إِذا لَقِيا غُلاماً فَقَتَلَهُ قالَ (until they met a boy, and he killed him. He said...) etc., فَقَتَلَهُ (and he killed him) is coordinated with the condition (الشرط) by the "fa" of consequence (فاء التفريع), and قالَ (He said) is the apodosis (جزاء) of إِذا (when), as is apparent from the speech. By this, it becomes clear that the main point in the speech is the mention of Moses's objection, not the mention of the killing. A similar case is the subsequent verse: فَانْطَلَقا حَتَّى إِذا أَتَيا أَهْلَ قَرْيَةٍ (So they went on until they came to the people of a town) — until His saying — قالَ لَوْ شِئْتَ (He said, ‘If you wished...’) etc. This is unlike the previous verse: فَانْطَلَقا حَتَّى إِذا رَكِبا فِي السَّفِينَةِ خَرَقَها قالَ (So they went on until, when they boarded the ship, he made a hole in it. He said...), for the apodosis of إِذا therein is خَرَقَها (he made a hole in it), and His saying قالَ (He said) is a separate, resumed (مستأنف) speech.

Accordingly, the verses are narrated in the form of a single story in which Moses objected to al-Khidr (عليه‌السلام) three times, one after another, not in the form of three separate stories in which he made three objections. It is as if it were said: "Such and such happened, so he objected to him, then he objected, then he objected." Thus, the story is the story of his objections; it is one story, not a story of the acts of one and the objections of the other such that they would be three.

From here, the reason for the difference between the three verses becomes clear: why خَرَقَها (he made a hole in it) was made the answer to idha in the first verse, while فَقَتَلَهُ (and he killed him) and فَوَجَدا (and they found) or فَأَقامَهُ (and he set it upright) were not made the answers in the second and third, but rather parts of the condition coordinated with it. Understand this.

His saying: أَقَتَلْتَ نَفْساً زَكِيَّةً (Did you kill an innocent soul?): Zakiyyah (الزكية) means pure (الطاهرة). What is meant is its purity from sins due to not having reached maturity, as suggested by His saying: غُلاماً (a boy). The inquiry is for disapproval (للإنكار), and the speaker is Moses.

His saying: بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ (without [his having killed] anyone): meaning without his having killed a soul in a manner that would permit his killing as retaliation (قصاص) or blood-wit (قود), for one who has not reached maturity cannot commit a killing that necessitates retaliation. It might be inferred from His saying بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ that he was a mature youth (شاباً بالغاً), and there is no contradiction in calling him a "boy" (غلام) even if he were mature, for "boy" is applied to both the mature and the immature. So the meaning is: "Did you kill, without retaliation, a soul innocent of the sins that necessitate killing?" for nothing had appeared to them from the boy that would necessitate it.

His saying: لَقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْئاً نُكْراً (You have certainly done a horrible thing!): meaning something abominable (منكراً) that nature finds repulsive and society does not recognize. He considered the making of a hole in the ship إِمْراً (monstrous)—meaning a calamity that results in misfortunes that had not yet occurred—and the killing of a soul نُكْراً (horrible) or abominable, which is more heinous and tragic to people than the hole which usually necessitates the loss of lives, but not through direct action at that moment.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ أَلَمْ أَقُلْ لَكَ إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِيَ صَبْراً — He said, ‘Did I not say to you, ‘‘Indeed you cannot have patience with me’’?’ (18:75). Its meaning is clear, and the addition of لَكَ (to you) is a type of reprimand (تقريع) to him for not heeding his advice, and an allusion to the fact that it was as if he had not heard his saying to him the first time: إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِيَ صَبْراً (Indeed you cannot have patience with me), or he heard it but thought it did not concern him but rather someone else. It is as if he is saying: "By my saying 'Indeed you cannot,' I meant you specifically and no one else."

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ إِنْ سَأَلْتُكَ عَنْ شَيْءٍ بَعْدَها فَلا تُصاحِبْنِي قَدْ بَلَغْتَ مِنْ لَدُنِّي عُذْراً — He said, ‘If I question you about anything after this, do not keep my company. You have already received enough excuses from me.’ (18:76). The pronoun in بَعْدَها (after this) refers to "this time" or "this question." Meaning: "If I ask you after this time or this question, do not keep my company," i.e., it is permissible for you not to keep my company.

His saying: قَدْ بَلَغْتَ مِنْ لَدُنِّي عُذْراً (You have already received enough excuses from me): meaning "you have reached an excuse and found it existing from my side," as your excuse has reached its limit from me.

His saying, the Exalted: فَانْطَلَقا حَتَّى إِذا أَتَيا أَهْلَ قَرْيَةٍ اسْتَطْعَما أَهْلَها — So they went on until they came to the people of a town. They asked its people for food... (18:77) to the end of the verse. The discussion regarding His saying: فَانْطَلَقا (So they went on), فَأَبَوْا (but they refused), فَوَجَدا (and they found), and فَأَقامَهُ (and he set it upright) is like the discussion regarding His saying in the previous verse: فَانْطَلَقا (So they went on) and فَقَتَلَهُ (and he killed him).

His saying: اسْتَطْعَما أَهْلَها (They asked its people for food) is an attribute (صفة) for "town" (قرية). He did not say "they asked them for food" because of the awkwardness of saying "a town they asked them for food," unlike saying "he came to a town" with the intention of "he came to the people of a town," because the town itself has a share in the "coming," so it is permissible to place it in the position of its people metaphorically. This is not the case with "asking for food," for that is specifically for its people. Accordingly, His saying أَهْلَها (its people) is not an instance of placing the manifest noun in the position of the pronoun.

He did not say "until when they came to a town, they asked its people for food" because the "town" would then be restricted to its literal meaning. The main purpose—as you have known—relates to the apodosis, namely His saying: قالَ لَوْ شِئْتَ لَاتَّخَذْتَ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً (He said, ‘If you wished, you could have taken a wage for it’). In this is the mention of taking a wage, which is only from its people, not from the town itself. So His saying: أَتَيا أَهْلَ قَرْيَةٍ (they came to the people of a town) is evidence that the setting upright of the wall was in the presence of some of the people of the town, which made it unnecessary to say "If you wished, you could have taken a wage for it from them" or "from its people." Understand this.

What is meant by istat‘ama (اسْتَطْعَما) is asking for food as hospitality (بالإضافة); therefore, He said: فَأَبَوْا أَنْ يُضَيِّفُوهُما (but they refused to entertain them). His saying: فَوَجَدا فِيها جِداراً يُرِيدُ أَنْ يَنْقَضَ (and they found in it a wall which was about to collapse): Inqidad (الانقضاض) is falling. The "will" (إرادة) of collapsing is a metaphor for being on the verge of falling and ruin. His saying: فَأَقامَهُ (and he set it upright): meaning al-Khidr stabilized it by repairing its condition. The Glorified did not mention how he set it upright—whether through a miraculous manner (خرق العادة) or through building or placing a support? However, Moses's saying: لَوْ شِئْتَ لَاتَّخَذْتَ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً (If you wished, you could have taken a wage for it) suggests that it was through a non-miraculous act, for the custom of taking a wage is for ordinary acts.

His saying: قالَ لَوْ شِئْتَ لَاتَّخَذْتَ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً (He said, ‘If you wished, you could have taken a wage for it’): takhidha (تخذ) and akhadha (أخذ) have the same meaning. The pronoun in عَلَيْهِ (for it) refers to the "setting upright" (الإقامة) understood from فَأَقامَهُ (and he set it upright), which is a verbal noun (مصدر) that allows both possibilities. The context testifies that they were hungry, so Moses mentioned taking a wage for his work, for had he taken a wage, they could have bought some food with it to satisfy their hunger.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ هذا فِراقُ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنِكَ سَأُنَبِّئُكَ بِتَأْوِيلِ ما لَمْ تَسْتَطِعْ عَلَيْهِ صَبْراً — He said, ‘This is the parting between me and you. I will inform you of the interpretation of that which you could not bear patiently.’ (18:78). The indication in هذا (this) is to Moses's speech, meaning "this speech is the cause of parting between me and you," or to the time, meaning "this time is the time of parting between me and you," as has been said. It is also possible that the indication is to the parting itself, meaning "this parting has arrived," as if it were an absent matter that arrived at Moses's saying: لَوْ شِئْتَ لَاتَّخَذْتَ (If you wished, you could have taken...) etc. His saying: بَيْنِي وَبَيْنِكَ (between me and you) and not "between us" is for emphasis. Al-Khidr only said this after the third objection because Moses had previously been apologizing, as in the first instance, or asking for a reprieve, as in the second. As for the parting after the third objection, Moses had already provided the excuse for it when he said after the second objection: إِنْ سأَلْتُكَ عَنْ شَيْءٍ بَعْدَها فَلا تُصاحِبْنِي (If I question you about anything after this, do not keep my company) etc. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمَّا السَّفِينَةُ فَكانَتْ لِمَساكِينَ — ‘As for the ship, it belonged to some poor people...’ (18:79) etc. This is the beginning of the detailed explanation of what he promised generally by saying: سَأُنَبِّئُكَ (I will inform you) etc. His saying: أَنْ أَعِيبَها (that I should damage it) means "make it defective." This is a context (قرينة) showing that what is meant by "every ship" is every non-defective ship.

His saying: وَكانَ وَراءَهُمْ مَلِكٌ (and there was a king behind them): Wara’ (وراء) means behind (الخلف), which is the spatial direction (الظرف) opposite to the direction a person faces, called "front" (قدام) or "ahead" (أمام). However, it is sometimes applied to the direction of which a person is heedless, and in which there is someone who intends him harm or something disliked, even if it is in front of him; or it is used for that which a person turns away from, or that which occupies a person with himself away from others, as if the person has turned his face in a direction opposite to it. The Exalted said: فَمَنِ ابْتَغى وَراءَ ذلِكَ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ العادُونَ — "But whoever seeks beyond that—they are the transgressors" (23:7). And He said: وَما كانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُكَلِّمَهُ اللهُ إِلَّا وَحْياً أَوْ مِنْ وَراءِ حِجابٍ — "It is not [given] to any human that Allah should speak to him except by revelation or from behind a curtain" (42:51). And He said: وَاللهُ مِنْ وَرائِهِمْ — "and Allah is behind them" (85:20).

The summary of the meaning is: The ship belonged to a number of poor people who worked with it in the sea and earned their living by it. There was a tyrannical king there who had ordered the seizure of ships. So I intended by making a hole in it to create a defect in it, so that the tyrant would not covet it and would leave it for them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمَّا الْغُلامُ فَكانَ أَبَواهُ مُؤْمِنَيْنِ فَخَشِينا أَنْ يُرْهِقَهُما طُغْياناً وَكُفْراً — ‘As for the boy, his parents were faithful [persons], and we feared lest he should overwhelm them with rebellion and unfaith.’ (18:80). What is more apparent from the context of the verse and what will follow in His saying: وَما فَعَلْتُهُ عَنْ أَمْرِي (and I did not do it of my own accord) is that the intended meaning of "fear" (الخشية) is "caution" (التحذر) out of compassion and mercy metaphorically, not its literal meaning which is a specific psychological state (التأثر القلبي) that is negated for Him, the Exalted, and for His prophets, as He said: وَلا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَداً إِلَّا اللهَ — "and they do not fear anyone except Allah" (33:39). Also, the intended meaning of His saying: أَنْ يُرْهِقَهُما طُغْياناً وَكُفْراً (lest he should overwhelm them with rebellion and unfaith) is that he would cover them with that, i.e., lead his parents to rebellion and unfaith through misguidance and spiritual influence due to their intense love for him. However, His saying in the following verse: وَأَقْرَبَ رُحْماً (and more affectionate) is not without support for the view that "rebellion and unfaith" (طُغْياناً وَكُفْراً) are specifications (تمييز) for the "overwhelming" (الإرهاق), i.e., attributes of the boy rather than his parents.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَرَدْنا أَنْ يُبْدِلَهُما رَبُّهُما خَيْراً مِنْهُ زَكاةً وَأَقْرَبَ رُحْماً — ‘So we desired that their Lord should give them in exchange one better than him in purity and more affectionate.’ (18:81). What is meant by being "better than him in purity" (زكاة) is being better than him in righteousness and faith, by the context of its being contrasted with "rebellion and unfaith" in the previous verse. The root of zakah (الزكاة), as has been said, is purity (الطهارة). What is meant by being "closer in kinship" (أَقْرَبَ رُحْماً) is being more observant of the ties of kinship and relationship, so he would not overwhelm them. As for interpreting it as being "more merciful" to them, the word أَقْرَبَ (closer) does not suit it as well. This—as you have known—supports the view that the intended meaning of يُرْهِقَهُما طُغْياناً وَكُفْراً (overwhelm them with rebellion and unfaith) in the previous verse is his overwhelming them with his own rebellion and unfaith, not his burdening them with rebellion and unfaith and covering them with it.

The verse—in any case—alludes to the fact that the faith of his parents was of value before Allah and called for a faithful, righteous child who would maintain their kinship ties. Since it was decreed that the boy would be otherwise, Allah commanded al-Khidr to kill him so that He might exchange him for them with one better than him in purity and closer in kinship.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمَّا الْجِدارُ فَكانَ لِغُلامَيْنِ يَتِيمَيْنِ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ وَكانَ تَحْتَهُ كَنْزٌ لَهُما وَكانَ أَبُوهُما صالِحاً — ‘As for the wall, it belonged to two orphan boys in the city, and there was a treasure under it belonging to them. Their father was a righteous man...’ (18:82). It is not unlikely to infer from the context that the "city" (المدينة) mentioned in this verse is other than the "town" (القرية) in which they found the wall and he set it upright. For had it been the same, there would not have been much need to mention that the two orphan boys were "in it." Thus, the concern was directed toward indicating that they and whoever was in charge of their affairs were not present in the town.

The mention of the orphanhood of the two boys, the existence of a treasure for them under the wall—which, had it collapsed, would have appeared and been lost—and the fact that their father was righteous, are all a preparation and preamble for His saying: فَأَرادَ رَبُّكَ أَنْ يَبْلُغا أَشُدَّهما وَيَسْتَخْرِجا كَنزَهُما (so your Lord desired that they should come of age and take out their treasure). His saying: رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ (as a mercy from your Lord) is the reason (تعليل) for the desire.

So His mercy, the Exalted, was the cause for the desire that they reach maturity and extract their treasure. This depended on the wall remaining upright, so al-Khidr set it upright. The cause for the arousal of mercy was the righteousness of their father, who had passed away, leaving the two boys as orphans and leaving a treasure for them.

There has been long discussion regarding reconciling the righteousness of their father with the existence of a treasure for them under the wall—which suggests that their father was the one who hoarded it—based on the condemnation of hoarding (الكنز), as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يَكْنِزُونَ الذَّهَبَ وَالْفِضَّةَ وَلا يُنْفِقُونَها فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ فَبَشِّرْهُمْ بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ — "And those who treasure up gold and silver, and do not spend it in the way of Allah, inform them of a painful punishment" (9:34).

However, the verse does not state more than that there was a treasure for them under it, without indicating that their father was the one who buried and hoarded it. Furthermore, the description of their father as "righteous" (صالحاً) is evidence that this treasure, whatever it was, was not something condemned, even assuming the father was the hoarder. Moreover, it is possible that their righteous father hoarded it for them for an interpretation (تأويل) that justified it; this is no greater than the making of a hole in the ship or the killing of a respected soul mentioned in the story, both of which were justified by an interpretation based on a Divine command. There are some narrations here that will reach you in the upcoming traditional discussion, Allah willing.

In the verse is an indication that the righteousness of a person may bequeath a beautiful effect to his children and result in felicity and good for them. Thus, this verse, on the side of good, is similar to His saying, the Exalted: وَلْيَخْشَ الَّذِينَ لَوْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ خَلْفِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّةً ضِعافاً خافُوا عَلَيْهِمْ — "Let those fear who, if they were to leave behind them weak offspring, would be concerned for them" (4:9).

His saying: وَما فَعَلْتُهُ عَنْ أَمْرِي (and I did not do it of my own accord) is an allusion to the fact that he only did what he did by the command of another, namely Allah, the Glorified, not by a command his own self gave him.

His saying: ذلِكَ تَأْوِيلُ ما لَمْ تَسْطِعْ عَلَيْهِ صَبْراً (That is the interpretation of that which you could not bear patiently): tas-ti‘ (تَسْطِعْ) is from as-ta‘a (اسطاع), meaning is-ta-ta‘a (استطاع). It was mentioned at the beginning of the commentary on Surah Al ‘Imran that "interpretation" (التأويل) in the usage of the Quran is the reality (الحقيقة) that a thing contains, to which it returns, and upon which it is built—like the interpretation of a dream, which is its explanation; the interpretation of a ruling, which is its rationale (ملاك); the interpretation of an act, which is its benefit and true goal; the interpretation of an event, which is its true cause; and so on.

So His saying: ذلِكَ تَأْوِيلُ ما لَمْ تَسْطِعْ (That is the interpretation of that which you could not...) etc., is an indication from him that what he mentioned for the three events and his actions therein is their true cause, not what Moses had assumed from the apparent titles (العناوين المترائية) of his actions, such as causing the destruction of people in the making of a hole in the ship, killing without a justifying cause in the killing of the boy, and poor management of livelihood in the setting upright of the wall.

Some have mentioned that among the beautiful etiquette al-Khidr used with his Lord in his speech is that those actions which were not free from some defect, he attributed to himself, such as his saying: "so I intended to damage it" (فأردت أن أعيبها). Those which could be attributed to both his Lord and himself, he expressed with the first-person plural, such as his saying: "so we desired that their Lord should give them in exchange" (فَأَرَدْنا أَنْ يُبْدِلَهُما رَبُّهُما) and "we feared" (فَخَشِينا). Those which are exclusive to Him, the Exalted, because of their relation to His Lordship and the management of His kingdom, he attributed to Him, such as his saying: "so your Lord desired that they should come of age" (فَأَرادَ رَبُّكَ أَنْ يَبْلُغا أَشُدَّهما).

Historical Discussion in Two Sections

1 - The Story of Moses and al-Khidr in the Quran: Allah, the Glorified, revealed to Moses that there was a servant among His servants who possessed knowledge that Moses did not have. He informed him that if he traveled to the junction of the two seas, he would find him there, at the place where the dead fish would come to life (or where the fish would be lost).

Moses resolved to meet the learned man and learn from him some of what he possessed, if possible. He informed his youth of what he had resolved, and they set out toward the junction of the two seas, carrying a dead fish with them. They traveled until they reached the junction of the two seas, having become exhausted. There was a rock there on the seashore, so they took refuge at it to rest for a while, having forgotten their fish while occupied with other things.

Suddenly, the fish stirred and fell into the sea alive, or fell into it while dead and sank into it. The youth witnessed this and wondered at it, but he forgot to mention it to Moses until they left the place and traveled on. When they had passed the junction of the two seas and were fatigued, Moses said to him: "Bring us our meal. We have certainly encountered much fatigue on this journey of ours." Then the youth remembered what he had witnessed regarding the fish and said to Moses: "When we took refuge on the rock, the fish came to life and fell into the sea, swimming in it until it sank. I wanted to mention its matter to you, but Satan made me forget it (or: I forgot the fish at the rock, and it fell into the sea and sank in it)."

Moses said: "That is what we were seeking and searching for! Let us return there." So they returned, retracing their footsteps, and found a servant among the servants of Allah, to whom Allah had granted a mercy from Himself and taught a knowledge from His own presence. Moses presented himself to him and asked to follow him so that he might teach him something possessing rectitude (رشد) from what Allah had taught him. The learned man said: "Indeed you cannot have patience with me regarding what you will witness of my actions, for you have no knowledge of their inner reality (تأويل). And how can you have patience about something you have not encompassed in your knowledge?" Moses promised him that he would be patient and would not disobey him in any matter, Allah willing. The learned man said, based on what he had requested and promised: "If you follow me, do not question me about anything until I mention it to you."

So Moses and the learned man went on until they boarded a ship with other passengers. Moses was unaware of the learned man's intention. The learned man made a hole in the ship such that drowning could not be ruled out. This astonished Moses and made him forget what he had promised, so he said to the learned man: "Did you make a hole in it to drown its people? You have certainly done a monstrous thing!" The learned man said to him: "Did I not say, 'Indeed you cannot have patience with me'?" Moses apologized to him, saying he had forgotten his promise of patience: "Do not take me to task for what I forgot, and do not be hard on me."

They went on and met a boy, and the learned man killed him. Moses could not restrain himself from changing his attitude and denounced that, saying: "Did you kill an innocent soul, without [his having killed] anyone? You have certainly done a horrible thing!" The learned man said to him a second time: "Did I not say to you, 'Indeed you cannot have patience with me'?" Moses had nothing with which to apologize or prevent his parting, though his soul was not pleased with it. He requested a final period of companionship with another question; if he asked it, he would accept the parting. He asked for a reprieve, saying: "If I question you about anything after this, do not keep my company. You have already received enough excuses from me." The learned man accepted.

They went on until they came to a town, having become very hungry. They asked its people for food, but no one among them would entertain them. Suddenly, there was a wall in it about to collapse, which people were wary of, so the learned man set it upright. Moses said to him: "If you wished, you could have taken a wage for your work from them, so we could use it to satisfy our hunger, for we are in need of it and the people will not entertain us."

The learned man said to him: "This is the parting between me and you. I will inform you of the interpretation of that which you could not bear patiently." Then he said: "As for the ship, it belonged to some poor people who worked in the sea and earned their living by it. There was a king behind them who seized every ship by force, so I made a hole in it so it would be defective and he would not desire it."

"As for the boy, he was a disbeliever and his parents were believers. Had he lived, he would have overwhelmed them with his disbelief and rebellion. Thus, Divine mercy encompassed them, and He commanded me to kill him so that He might exchange him for them with a child better than him in purity and closer in kinship. So I killed him."

"As for the wall, it belonged to two orphan boys in the city, and there was a treasure for them under it. Their father was a righteous man, so Divine mercy encompassed them due to the righteousness of their father. He commanded me to set it upright so it would remain stable until they reach maturity and extract their treasure. Had it collapsed, the matter of the treasure would have appeared and people would have plundered it."

He said: "I did not do what I did of my own accord, but by a command from Allah. And its interpretation (تأويل) is what I have informed you of." Then he parted from Moses.

2 - The Story of al-Khidr (عليه السلام): He is not mentioned in the Quran except in the story of Moses's journey to the junction of the two seas. None of his general attributes are mentioned except what is in His saying, the Exalted: فَوَجَدا عَبْداً مِنْ عِبادِنا آتَيْناهُ رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِنا وَعَلَّمْناهُ مِنْ لَدُنَّا عِلْماً — "There they found one of Our servants whom We had granted a mercy from Ourselves, and whom We had taught a knowledge from Our own presence" (18:65). What can be gathered from the Prophetic narrations or those reported through the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt regarding his story is as follows: In a narration from Muhammad ibn ‘Amarah from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام): "Al-Khidr was a sent prophet (نبياً مرسلاً). Allah, Blessed and Exalted, sent him to his people, and he called them to His monotheism (توحيده), and to the acknowledgment of His prophets, messengers, and books. His sign (آيته) was that he would not sit on a dry piece of wood or a barren land except that it would bloom green. He was named al-Khidr (the Green One) for that reason. His name was Talia son of Malik son of ‘Abir son of Arpachshad son of Shem son of Noah..." the tradition. What was mentioned regarding the reason for his naming is supported by what is in al-Durr al-Manthur, from several compilers of traditions from Ibn ‘Abbas and Abu Hurayrah from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), who said: "Al-Khidr was only named al-Khidr because he prayed on a white fur (فروة بيضاء), and it shook and turned green."

In some reports—as narrated by al-‘Ayyashi from Burayd from one of the two [Imams al-Baqir or al-Sadiq] (عليهما‌السلام): "Al-Khidr and Dhu al-Qarnayn were two learned men and were not prophets..." the tradition. However, the verses revealed regarding his story with Moses are not without an apparent indication of his being a prophet. How could it be otherwise, when they mention the descent of judgment (الحكم) upon him?

It appears from scattered reports from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) that he is alive and has not yet died. It is not difficult for Allah, the Glorified, to grant some of His servants a long life until a distant term, nor is there any rational proof (برهان عقلي) indicating the impossibility of that.

Regarding the cause of this, it has been mentioned in some narrations from the paths of the general public [Sunnis] that he is a son of Adam from his own loins, and his term was deferred for him so that he might expose the lie of the Dajjal. In some, it is said that Adam (عليه‌السلام) prayed for his survival until the Day of Resurrection. In several narrations from the paths of both groups, it is said that he drank from the Fountain of Life (عين الحياة) which is in the Darkness (الظلمات) when Dhu al-Qarnayn entered it seeking it. Al-Khidr was in his vanguard, so al-Khidr was granted it while Dhu al-Qarnayn was not. These and their like are isolated reports (آحاد) that are not definitive, and there is no way to authenticate them through the Book, definitive Sunnah, or reason.

Stories and anecdotes, as well as narrations, have multiplied regarding al-Khidr, including things that no person of intellect would rely upon, such as the narration of Khasif: "Four of the prophets are alive: two in heaven, Jesus and Idris; and two on earth, al-Khidr and Elias. As for al-Khidr, he is in the sea, and as for his companion, he is on land."

And the narration of al-‘Uqayli from Ka‘b, who said: "Al-Khidr is on a pulpit between the Upper Sea and the Lower Sea. The creatures of the sea have been commanded to listen to him and obey him, and the souls are presented to him morning and evening."

And the narration of Ka‘b al-Ahbar: "Al-Khidr son of ‘Amil rode with a group of his companions until he reached the Indian Sea, which is the Sea of China. He said to his companions: 'O my companions, lower me down.' So they lowered him into the sea for days and nights. Then he ascended, and they said: 'O Khidr, what did you see? For Allah has honored you and preserved your soul in the depths of this sea.' He said: 'An angel among the angels met me and said to me: "O sinful human, to where? And from where?" I said: "I intended to see the depth of this sea." He said to me: "How? When a man from the time of David (عليه‌السلام) has been falling and has not reached a third of its depth until this hour; and that was three hundred years ago."'" This is among other narrations containing rare stories.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Ibn Babuwayh with his chain of narration from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad ibn ‘Amarah from his father from Imam Ja‘far ibn Muhammad (عليه‌السلام) in a tradition: "When Allah spoke to Moses directly, and sent down the Torah to him, and wrote for him in the tablets an admonition of everything and an explanation for everything, and made a sign in his hand and his staff, and in the flood, the locusts, the lice, the frogs, and the blood, and the parting of the sea, and Allah drowned Pharaoh and his hosts—humanity [i.e., human nature] acted within him until he said to himself: 'I do not see that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has created a creature more learned than me.' So Allah revealed to Gabriel: 'Reach My servant before he perishes, and say to him: "At the meeting place of the two seas is a worshipping man; follow him and learn from him."'"

"So Gabriel descended to Moses with what his Lord, the Mighty and Majestic, had commanded him. Moses knew that this was because of what his soul had whispered to him. So he and his youth, Joshua son of Nun, went on until they reached the meeting place of the two seas. They found al-Khidr there worshipping Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, as Allah said in His Book: فَوَجَدا عَبْداً مِنْ عِبادِنا آتَيْناهُ رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِنا وَعَلَّمْناهُ مِنْ لَدُنَّا عِلْماً..." the tradition.

I say: The tradition is long, mentioning his companionship with al-Khidr and what occurred between them as Allah mentioned in His Book in the story.

Al-‘Ayyashi narrated the story in his Tafsir through two paths, and al-Qummi in his Tafsir through two paths, both supported and interrupted (مسنداً ومرسلاً). It was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through many paths from the compilers of traditions such as al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Nasa’i, al-Tirmidhi, and others, from Ibn ‘Abbas from Ubayy ibn Ka‘b from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

The traditions agree on the meaning of what we quoted from the beginning of the tradition of Muhammad ibn ‘Amarah, and that the fish they carried came to life at the rock and took its way into the sea burrowing. However, they differ in many matters added to what is in the Quran regarding the core of the story.

Among them is what is gathered from the narration of Ibn Babuwayh and al-Qummi: that the junction of the two seas was in the land of the Levant (الشامات) and Palestine, by the context of their mention that the town they entered was Nazareth—to which the Christians (النصارى) are attributed. In some, it is said the land was Azerbaijan. This agrees with what is in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Suddi: that the two seas are the Kura and the Aras where they pour into the sea, and that the town was called Bajrawan and its people were mean. It was narrated from Ubayy that it was Africa; from al-Qurazi that it was Tangier; and from Qatadah that it was the meeting place of the Roman and Persian seas.

Among them is what is in some narrations that the fish was roasted, while in most of them it was salted.

Among them is what is in the interrupted (مرسلة) report of al-Qummi and the narrations of the two Shaykhs [al-Bukhari and Muslim], al-Nasa’i, al-Tirmidhi, and others: that there was a Fountain of Life (عين الحياة) at the rock—even in the narration of Muslim and others that the water was the Water of Life; whoever drinks from it becomes eternal, and no dead thing approaches it except that it comes to life. So when they stopped and the fish touched the water, it came to life.

The tradition continues: In others, it is said the youth of Moses performed ablution (توضأ) from the water and a drop of it fell on the fish, so it came to life. In others, he drank from it though he was not supposed to, so al-Khidr took him and placed him in a ship and left it in the sea, so he is among its waves until the Hour begins. In some, it is said that at the rock was the Fountain of Life from which al-Khidr used to drink, while the rest of the narrations are devoid of its mention.

Among them is what is in the narration of the four Sahihs and others: "The fish fell into the sea and took its way into the sea burrowing—so Allah held back the flow of the water from the fish, so it became like an arch (طاق) over it..." the tradition. In some of these narrations, after Moses returned, he saw the track of the fish, so they began walking on the water following the track of the fish until they reached one of the islands of the Arabs. In the tradition of al-Tabari from Ibn ‘Abbas regarding the story: "So he returned—meaning Moses—until he came to the rock and found the fish. The fish began striking in the sea and Moses followed it, putting his staff forward to part the water for it and following the fish. The fish did not touch anything of the sea except that it turned dry until it became a rock..." the tradition. Some are devoid of that.

Among them is what is in most of them that Moses met al-Khidr at the rock. In some, it is said he went through the burrow of the fish or on the water until he found him on one of the islands of the sea. In some, he found him sitting or reclining on the surface of the water.

Among them is their difference as to whether the youth accompanied them or they left him and went on.

Among these differences are the variations regarding the manner in which the ship was breached, how the youth was slain, how the wall was erected, and the nature of the treasure (الكنز) — the hidden wealth or knowledge — that lay beneath it. However, the majority of narrations state that it was a golden tablet inscribed with words of wisdom. Regarding the "righteous father" (الأب الصالح), the apparent sense of most narrations is that he was their immediate father. Yet, some reports suggest he was their tenth forefather, others say the seventh, some state there were seventy fathers between them, and others claim there was a span of seven hundred years between them, among other points of divergence.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, narrated from Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Bilal, from Yunus, regarding a letter written to Imam al-Rida عليه‌السلام: They asked him about the learned man (العالم) whom Musa approached—which of them was more knowledgeable? And is it permissible for there to be a Proof (حجة) — a divine authority and guide — over Musa during his own time? He wrote in reply: "Musa came to the learned man and found him on one of the islands of the sea—either sitting or reclining. Musa offered him the greeting of peace (السلام), but the man did not recognize the greeting, as there was no greeting of peace in that land."

He said: "Who are you?" He replied: "I am Musa ibn 'Imran." He said: "Are you the Musa ibn 'Imran to whom Allah spoke directly?" He replied: "Yes." He asked: "What is your need?" He said: "I have come so that you may teach me of the right guidance (رشد) — the divine leading toward truth and the straight path — that you have been taught." He said: "I have been entrusted with a matter that you cannot bear, and you have been entrusted with a matter that I cannot bear..." to the end of the narration.

I say: This meaning is narrated in other reports from the traditions of both schools (الفريقين) — the Shi'a and the Sunni.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Hakim extracted and authenticated a report from Ubayy that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "When Musa met al-Khidr, a bird came and dipped its beak into the water. Al-Khidr said to Musa: 'Do you know what this bird is saying?' He asked: 'And what does it say?' He replied: 'It says: My knowledge and your knowledge compared to the knowledge of Allah is naught but as much as my beak has taken from the water.'"

I say: The story of this bird is mentioned in most narrations of this account.

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, narrated from Hisham ibn Salim, from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام, he said: "Musa was more knowledgeable than al-Khidr."

In the same source, from Abu Hamzah, from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام, he said: "The successor (وصي) of Musa was Yusha' ibn Nun, and he was the youth (فتاه) mentioned in His Book."

In the same source, from Abdullah ibn Maymun al-Qaddah, from Abu Abdillah, from his father عليه‌السلام, he said: "While Musa was sitting among a gathering of the Children of Israel, a man said to him: 'I do not see anyone more knowledgeable of Allah than you.' Musa replied: 'I do not see [anyone either].' Then Allah revealed to him: 'Nay, My servant al-Khidr [is].' So he asked for the way to reach him, and the fish (الحوت) was a sign (آية) for him if he were to lose it. Then there occurred of his affair what Allah has narrated."

I say: The difference in the narrations regarding their respective knowledge should be understood as a difference in the type of knowledge.

In the same source, from Abu Basir, from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام: Regarding His saying: فَخَشِينا "So we feared" [Qarai: 18:81], he said: "He feared that if the youth reached maturity, he would call his parents to faithlessness (الكفر), and they would comply out of their excessive love for him."

In the same source, from 'Uthman, from a man, from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام: Regarding the saying of Allah: فَأَرَدْنَا أَن يُبْدِلَهُمَا رَبُّهُمَا خَيْرًا مِّنْهُ زَكَاةً وَأَقْرَبَ رُحْمًا "So we desired that their Lord should give them in exchange one better than him in purity and closer in affection" [Qarai: 18:81], he said: "A daughter was born to them, who then gave birth to a son who became a prophet."

I say: In most narrations, it is stated that seventy prophets were born from her lineage; the intent is to establish the mediation (الواسطة) — the causal link through which divine blessings flow.

In the same source, from Ishaq ibn 'Ammar, he said: I heard Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام saying: "Indeed, Allah rectifies the affairs of a believer's child and grandchild through his own righteousness (صلاح), and He protects him in his small dwelling and the dwellings around him; they remain under the protection of Allah due to his honor (كرامة) before Allah." Then he mentioned the two youths and said: وَكَانَ أَبُوهُمَا صَالِحًا "and their father had been a righteous man" [Qarai: 18:82]. "Have you not seen how Allah rewarded the righteousness of their parents for their sake?"

In the same source, from Mas'adah ibn Sadaqah, from Ja'far ibn Muhammad, from his fathers عليهم‌السلام, that the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Indeed, Allah succeeds the righteous servant after his death in his family and wealth—even if his family were people of evil." Then he recited this verse to its end: وَكَانَ أَبُوهُمَا صَالِحًا "and their father had been a righteous man" [Qarai: 18:82].

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Jabir that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Indeed, Allah rectifies the affairs of a righteous man's child, his grandchild, and the people of the dwellings around him; they remain under the protection of Allah as long as he is among them."

I say: The narrations regarding this meaning are numerous and widely transmitted (مستفيضة).

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission from Safwan al-Jammal, he said: I asked Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَأَمَّا الْجِدَارُ فَكَانَ لِغُلَامَيْنِ يَتِيمَيْنِ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ وَكَانَ تَحْتَهُ كَنزٌ لَّهُمَا "As for the wall, it belonged to two orphan boys in the city, and there was a treasure beneath it belonging to them" [Qarai: 18:82]. He said: "As for that, it was neither gold nor silver; rather, it was four statements: 'There is no god but Allah'; 'Whoever is certain of death does not laugh [excessively]'; 'Whoever is certain of the Reckoning (الحساب), his heart does not rejoice [in vanity]'; and 'Whoever is certain of the Decree (القدر) fears none but Allah.'"

I say: Narrations have multiplied from both the Shi'a and Sunni paths stating that the treasure (الكنز) which was beneath the wall was a tablet inscribed with these words. In most of them, it is stated that it was a tablet of gold. This does not contradict his saying in this narration, "it was neither gold nor silver," because the intent is to negate its being currency like the dinar or dirham, as that is what first comes to mind (المتبادر). The narrations differ in specifying the words that were inscribed on the tablet, but most of them agree on the word of monotheism (التوحيد) and the two issues of death and the Decree.

Some narrations combine the two testimonies of faith (الشهادتين), as narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Bayhaqi in Shu'ab al-Iman, from Ali ibn Abi Talib: Regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَكَانَ تَحْتَهُ كَنزٌ لَّهُمَا "and there was a treasure beneath it belonging to them" [Qarai: 18:82], he said: "It was a golden tablet inscribed with: 'There is no god but Allah, Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah. I wonder at the one who mentions that death is a reality (حق), how can he rejoice? I wonder at the one who mentions that the Fire is a reality, how can he laugh? I wonder at the one who mentions that the Decree is a reality, how can he grieve? And I wonder at the one who sees the world and its shifting of its people from state to state, how can he find tranquility in it?'"

Ayahs

وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَن ذِى ٱلْقَرْنَيْنِ ۖ قُلْ سَأَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْهُ ذِكْرًا (٨٣)
They question you concerning Dhul Qarnayn. Say, ‘I will relate to you an account of him.’ (83)
إِنَّا مَكَّنَّا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ سَبَبًا (٨٤)
Indeed We had granted him power in the land and given him the means to all things. (84)
فَأَتْبَعَ سَبَبًا (٨٥)
So he directed a means. (85)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ مَغْرِبَ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَجَدَهَا تَغْرُبُ فِى عَيْنٍ حَمِئَةٍ وَوَجَدَ عِندَهَا قَوْمًا ۗ قُلْنَا يَـٰذَا ٱلْقَرْنَيْنِ إِمَّآ أَن تُعَذِّبَ وَإِمَّآ أَن تَتَّخِذَ فِيهِمْ حُسْنًا (٨٦)
When he reached the place where the sun sets, he found it setting over a warm sea, and by it he found a people. We said, ‘O Dhul Qarnayn! You will either punish them, or treat them with kindness.’ (86)
قَالَ أَمَّا مَن ظَلَمَ فَسَوْفَ نُعَذِّبُهُۥ ثُمَّ يُرَدُّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ فَيُعَذِّبُهُۥ عَذَابًا نُّكْرًا (٨٧)
He said, ‘As for him who is a wrongdoer, we will punish him. Then he shall be returned to his Lord and He will punish him with a dire punishment. (87)
وَأَمَّا مَنْ ءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلَهُۥ جَزَآءً ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۖ وَسَنَقُولُ لَهُۥ مِنْ أَمْرِنَا يُسْرًا (٨٨)
But as for him who has faith and acts righteously, he shall have the best reward, and we will assign him easy tasks under our command.’ (88)
ثُمَّ أَتْبَعَ سَبَبًا (٨٩)
Thereafter he directed another means. (89)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ مَطْلِعَ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَجَدَهَا تَطْلُعُ عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ لَّمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُم مِّن دُونِهَا سِتْرًا (٩٠)
When he reached the place where the sun rises, he found it rising on a people for whom We had not provided any shield against it. (90)
كَذَٰلِكَ وَقَدْ أَحَطْنَا بِمَا لَدَيْهِ خُبْرًا (٩١)
So it was, and We were fully aware of whatever [means] he possessed. (91)
ثُمَّ أَتْبَعَ سَبَبًا (٩٢)
Thereafter he directed another means. (92)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ بَيْنَ ٱلسَّدَّيْنِ وَجَدَ مِن دُونِهِمَا قَوْمًا لَّا يَكَادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ قَوْلًا (٩٣)
When he reached [the place] between the two barriers, he found between them a people who could hardly understand a word [of his language]. (93)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰذَا ٱلْقَرْنَيْنِ إِنَّ يَأْجُوجَ وَمَأْجُوجَ مُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَهَلْ نَجْعَلُ لَكَ خَرْجًا عَلَىٰٓ أَن تَجْعَلَ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَهُمْ سَدًّا (٩٤)
They said, ‘O Dhul Qarnayn! Indeed Gog and Magog are causing disaster in this land. Shall we pay you a tribute on condition that you build a barrier between them and us?’ (94)
قَالَ مَا مَكَّنِّى فِيهِ رَبِّى خَيْرٌ فَأَعِينُونِى بِقُوَّةٍ أَجْعَلْ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُمْ رَدْمًا (٩٥)
He said, ‘What my Lord has furnished me is better. Yet help me with some strength, and I will make a bulwark between you and them. (95)
ءَاتُونِى زُبَرَ ٱلْحَدِيدِ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا سَاوَىٰ بَيْنَ ٱلصَّدَفَيْنِ قَالَ ٱنفُخُوا۟ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَعَلَهُۥ نَارًا قَالَ ءَاتُونِىٓ أُفْرِغْ عَلَيْهِ قِطْرًا (٩٦)
Bring me pieces of iron!’ When he had levelled up between the flanks, he said, ‘Blow!’ When he had turned it into fire, he said, ‘Bring me molten copper to pour over it.’ (96)
فَمَا ٱسْطَـٰعُوٓا۟ أَن يَظْهَرُوهُ وَمَا ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ لَهُۥ نَقْبًا (٩٧)
So they could neither scale it, nor could they make a hole in it. (97)
قَالَ هَـٰذَا رَحْمَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّى ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّى جَعَلَهُۥ دَكَّآءَ ۖ وَكَانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّى حَقًّا (٩٨)
He said, ‘This is a mercy from my Lord. But when the promise of my Lord is fulfilled, He will level it; and my Lord’s promise is true.’ (98)
۞ وَتَرَكْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَمُوجُ فِى بَعْضٍ ۖ وَنُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَجَمَعْنَـٰهُمْ جَمْعًا (٩٩)
That day We shall let them surge over one another, the Trumpet will be blown, and We shall gather them all, (99)
وَعَرَضْنَا جَهَنَّمَ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَرْضًا (١٠٠)
and on that day We shall bring hell into view visibly for the faithless. (100)
ٱلَّذِينَ كَانَتْ أَعْيُنُهُمْ فِى غِطَآءٍ عَن ذِكْرِى وَكَانُوا۟ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَمْعًا (١٠١)
—Those whose eyes were blind to My remembrance and who could not hear. (101)
أَفَحَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَن يَتَّخِذُوا۟ عِبَادِى مِن دُونِىٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۚ إِنَّآ أَعْتَدْنَا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ نُزُلًا (١٠٢)
Do the faithless suppose that they have taken My servants for guardians in My stead? Indeed, We have prepared hell for the hospitality of the faithless. (102)

(Commentary)

The verses contain the story of Dhū al-Qarnayn, and within them are some of the prophecies (ملاحم) of the Quran:

His saying, the Exalted: «وَيَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنْ ذِي الْقَرْنَيْنِ قُلْ سَأَتْلُوا عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْهُ ذِكْراً» — They ask you about Dhū al-Qarnayn. Say, ‘I will let you into something of his story.’ (18:83). That is, they ask you about the state and affairs of Dhū al-Qarnayn. The evidence for this is that His answer to the question was by mentioning his affairs, not by defining his person, to the extent that He sufficed with his title and did not go beyond it to mention his name.

The word "remembrance" (الذكر) is either a verbal noun (مصدر) used in the sense of the object (مفعول), meaning: "Say, I shall recite to you from him—meaning from Dhū al-Qarnayn—something mentioned (مذكوراً)"; or what is meant by "remembrance" is the Quran—as Allah has named it "the Remembrance" (الذكر) in several places of His speech—and the meaning would be: "Say, I shall recite to you from him—meaning regarding Dhū al-Qarnayn—or from Allah, a Quranic recitation," which is what follows this verse from His saying: «إِنَّا مَكَّنَّا لَهُ» — Indeed We established him... until the end of the story. The second meaning is more apparent.

His saying, the Exalted: «إِنَّا مَكَّنَّا لَهُ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَآتَيْناهُ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ سَبَباً» — Indeed We established him in the land and gave him the means to all things. (18:84). Establishment (التمكين) means the granting of power (الإقدار). It is said: "I established him" (مكنته) or "I established for him" (مكنت له), meaning I gave him power.
