The State of Ma'in, its capital being Qarnaw. Scholars have estimated that the traces of the Ma'in state begin from the 14th century BC to the 7th or 8th century BC. They have found some kings of this state, twenty-six in number, such as "Ab-Yada'" and "Ab-Yada' Yathi'," meaning the Savior.

The State of Saba'. They were from the Qahtanites. They were first "Dhus" (أذواء) then "Qayls" (أقيال). The one among them who rose to prominence was "Saba'," the master of the Sarwah palace east of Sana'a, who seized control of all. Their kingdom began from 850 BC to 115 BC. Known among their kings are twenty-seven, fifteen of whom were called Mukarrib, such as the Mukarrib "Yatha' Amar" and the Mukarrib "Dhamar Ali," and twelve of whom were called only "King," such as King "Dharah" and King "Yarim Ayman."

The State of the Himyarites. They consist of two layers: the first being the kings of Saba' and Raydan from 115 BC to 275 AD, and these were kings only. The second layer being the kings of Saba', Raydan, Hadramaut, and others; these were fourteen kings, most of whom were Tababi'ah. The first was "Shammar Yar'ish," the second "Dhu al-Qarnayn," and the third "Amr," the husband of Bilqis—and it ends with Dhu Jadan. Their kingdom began from 275 AD to 525 AD.

He then said: Thus, the connection of the title "Dhu" with the kings of Yemen has become apparent. We do not find among others, such as the Roman kings for example, anyone titled with "Dhu." So Dhu al-Qarnayn is from the kings of Yemen, and there has preceded among their kings one named Dhu al-Qarnayn. But is this the Dhu al-Qarnayn mentioned in the Quran?

We say: No. Because this one is mentioned among kings very close to our time, and that was not narrated about them, except perhaps in narrations mentioned by storytellers in history, such as that "Shammar Yar'ish" reached the lands of Iraq, Persia, Khorasan, and Sogdia, and built the city of Samarkand—its origin being "Shammar-kand"—and that As'ad Abu Karib invaded Azerbaijan, sent his son Hassan to Sogdia, his son Ya'fur to Rome, and his nephew to Persia, and that some Himyarites remained in China to this day after that king's invasion of it.

Ibn Khaldun and others dismissed these reports as lies, labeling them as exaggerated, and refuted them with geographical and historical evidence.

Therefore, Dhu al-Qarnayn is from the Arab nation, but in an ancient history before the known history. (End of summary).

And it is said: Dhu al-Qarnayn is Cyrus (كورش), one of the Achaemenid Persian kings (539–560 BC). He is the one who founded the Iranian Empire, united the kingdoms of Persia and Media, conquered Babylon, permitted the return of the Jews from Babylon to Jerusalem, assisted in building the Temple, conquered Egypt, then crossed into Greece and defeated them, reaching the West. Then he traveled to the farthest inhabited regions in the East.

This was mentioned by someone near our era, and then some Indian researchers in recent times exerted effort in clarifying and making it plausible. The summary of this is that the description of Dhu al-Qarnayn mentioned in the Quran applies to this great king. He was a believer in Allah according to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد), just toward his subjects, traveling with compassion, gentleness, and benevolence, and firm with the people of oppression and aggression. Allah gave him the means to all things, so he combined religion, intellect, moral virtues, preparation, strength, wealth, and power, with all means being subservient to him.

He traveled, as Allah mentioned in His Book, once toward the West until he seized Lydia and its surroundings. Then he traveled a second time toward the East until he reached the rising-place of the sun and found there a nomadic, barbaric people living in the wilderness. Then he built the barrier, which, according to evidence, is the barrier constructed in the Darial Pass between the Caucasus Mountains near the city of Tbilisi. This is the summary of the discourse, and here is the detail:

His faith in Allah and the Last Day: This is indicated by what is in the books of the Old Testament, such as the Book of Ezra (chapter 1), the Book of Daniel (chapter 6), and the Book of Isaiah (chapters 44 and 45), regarding his glorification and sanctification, even calling him in the Book of Isaiah "the Lord's shepherd." It says in the forty-fifth chapter: "This is what the Lord says to his anointed, to Cyrus, whose right hand I take hold of to subdue nations before him and to strip kings of their armor, to open doors before him so that gates will not be shut: I will go before you and will level the mountains; I will break down gates of bronze and cut through bars of iron. I will give you hidden treasures, riches stored in secret places, so that you may know that I am the Lord, the God of Israel, who summons you by name... I bestow on you a title of honor, though you do not acknowledge me."

Even if one disregards its being revelation, the Jews, despite their religious fanaticism, would not consider a polytheistic, Magian, or pagan man—if Cyrus were such—as a divine messiah, guided, supported, and a shepherd for the Lord.

Furthermore, the inscriptions and writings in cuneiform script inherited from Darius the Great—with only eight years separating them—speak of him being a monotheist, not a polytheist. It is not conceivable that what Cyrus was upon would change in this short time.

As for his psychological virtues, it suffices to refer to the preserved reports of his life and conduct, and how he dealt with the tyrants and despots who rebelled against him or whom he fought, such as the kings of Media, Lydia, Babylon, Egypt, and the nomadic tyrants on the fringes of Bactria (which is Balkh) and others. Whenever he prevailed over a people, he pardoned their criminals, honored their noble ones, had mercy on their weak, and disciplined their corrupt and treacherous.

The books of the Old Testament praised him, and the Jews respect him with the greatest respect because he saved them from the Babylonian captivity, returned them to their lands, spent wealth for them to renew the building of the Temple, and returned to them the precious items of the Temple that had been looted and stored in the treasuries of the kings of Babylon. This in itself is another support for Dhu al-Qarnayn being Cyrus, for the question about Dhu al-Qarnayn was prompted by the Jews, according to the narrations.

The ancient Greek historians, such as Herodotus and others, could not help but describe him with chivalry, gallantry, magnanimity, generosity, forgiveness, lack of greed, mercy, and compassion, praising him with the finest praise.

As for his being named Dhu al-Qarnayn: Although histories are devoid of anything indicating this, the recent discovery of his stone statue in Mashhad Murghab in southern Iran removes doubt regarding his being described as "Dhu al-Qarnayn." For he is depicted there with two horns growing from the top of his head from a single base; one horn inclines forward and the other takes the backward direction. This is close to the saying of the ancients regarding the reason for the name Dhu al-Qarnayn: that he had a crown or helmet with two horns.

In the Book of Daniel, there is mention of a vision he saw regarding Cyrus in the form of a two-horned ram. It says:

"In the third year of King Belshazzar’s reign, I, Daniel, had a vision, after the one that had already appeared to me. In my vision I saw myself in the citadel of Susa in the province of Elam; in the vision I was beside the Ulai Canal. I looked up, and there before me was a ram with two horns, standing beside the canal, and the horns were long. One of the horns was longer than the other but grew up later. I watched the ram as it charged toward the west and the north and the south. No animal could stand against it, and none could rescue from its power. It did as it pleased and became great."

"As I was thinking about this, suddenly a goat with a prominent horn between its eyes came from the west, crossing the whole earth without touching the ground. It came toward the two-horned ram I had seen standing beside the canal and charged at it in great rage. I saw it attack the ram furiously, striking the ram and shattering its two horns. The ram was powerless to stand against it; the goat knocked it to the ground and trampled on it, and none could rescue the ram from its power. The goat became very great..."

Then it mentions after the completion of the vision that Gabriel appeared to him and interpreted his vision, in which the two-horned ram applies to Cyrus, and its two horns are the kingdoms of Persia and Media, and the goat with one horn applies to Alexander the Great.

As for his journey toward the West and East: his journey toward the West was to repel the tyrant of Lydia, who had marched with his armies toward Cyrus in oppression and transgression without any excuse. So he marched toward him, fought him, and defeated him, then pursued him until he besieged him in his capital, then conquered it and captured him. Then he pardoned him and all his supporters, honored them, and treated them well, though he could have disciplined or destroyed them. The application of the story to the saying of the Exalted: حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ مَغْرِبَ الشَّمْسِ وَجَدَها تَغْرُبُ فِي عَيْنٍ حَمِئَةٍ "until when he reached the setting-place of the sun, he found it setting in a muddy spring" (18:86)—perhaps the western coast of Asia Minor—وَوَجَدَ عِنْدَها قَوْماً قُلْنا يا ذَا الْقَرْنَيْنِ إِمَّا أَنْ تُعَذِّبَ وَإِمَّا أَنْ تَتَّخِذَ فِيهِمْ حُسْناً "and he found a people near it. We said, ‘O Dhul Qarnayn! Either punish them, or treat them with kindness’" (18:86), is due to their deserving punishment for their transgression, oppression, and corruption.

Then he traveled toward the great desert in the East around Bactria to extinguish the fire of sedition ignited by nomadic, barbaric tribes who had risen there for attack and corruption. The application of the saying of the Exalted: حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ مَطْلِعَ الشَّمْسِ وَجَدَها تَطْلُعُ عَلى قَوْمٍ لَمْ نَجْعَلْ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِها سِتْراً "until when he reached the rising-place of the sun, he found it rising upon a people for whom We had provided no shelter from it" (18:90), to him is apparent.

As for his building the barrier: The barrier exists in the pass of the Caucasus Mountains extending from the Caspian Sea to the Black Sea. The pass is called "Darial," and it is located between the city of Tbilisi and Vladikavkaz.

This barrier is located in a pass between two towering mountains that extend from its sides. It is the only opening connecting the southern and northern sides of the mountains. The mountains, along with the Caspian Sea and the Black Sea, form a natural barrier thousands of kilometers long, separating southern Asia from its north.

In those ages, evil nations from the inhabitants of northeastern Asia would attack from the Caucasus mountain pass to the regions south of it, raiding Armenia, then Iran, even Assyria and Chaldea. They attacked around the seventh century BC, spreading killing, captivity, and looting throughout the lands until they reached Nineveh, the capital of Assyria. This was approximately in the century preceding the era of Cyrus.

Ancient historians like Herodotus the Greek mentioned Cyrus's journey to northern Iran to extinguish the fires of sedition that had ignited there. It appears that he built the barrier in the Darial Pass during this journey at the request of the people of the north and their complaint. He built it with stones and iron, and it is the only rampart in which iron was used, and it is between two mountain barriers. The application of the saying of the Exalted: فَأَعِينُونِي بِقُوَّةٍ أَجْعَلْ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُمْ رَدْماً آتُونِي زُبَرَ الْحَدِيدِ "‘So assist me with strength, and I will make a barrier between you and them. Bring me blocks of iron...’" (18:95-96) to it is apparent.

Among what supports this claim is the existence of a river near it called "Cyrus," which is the name of Cyrus among Westerners, and another river passing through Tbilisi called "Kur." This barrier was mentioned by Josephus, the Jewish historian, when mentioning his journey to northern Caucasus. It is not the wall existing in Bab al-Abwab on the coast of the Caspian Sea, for history attributes its construction to Khosrow Anushirvan, and Josephus was before him.

Furthermore, the barrier of Bab al-Abwab is certainly not the barrier of Dhu al-Qarnayn mentioned in the Quran, as iron was never used in it.

As for Gog and Magog: Research into the developments governing languages guides us to the fact that they are the Mongols. For the word in Chinese pronunciation is "Mankuk" or "Manjuk." The terms "Yajuj" and "Majuj" seem to be Hebrew transliterations, and in the Greek translations and others of the Old Testament, they are "Gog" and "Magog." The complete similarity between "Magog" and "Mankuk" dictates that the word developed from the Chinese pronunciation "Mankuk," just as "Mongol" and "Mughal" were derived from it. In the field of linguistic evolution, there are countless parallels to this.

So Gog and Magog are the Mongols. This nation, inhabiting northeastern Asia from the most ancient times, was a large, attacking nation that would raid China at times, and at other times through the Darial Pass of the Caucasus into Armenia, northern Iran, and elsewhere. At another time—after the building of the barrier—they raided Northern Europe and were called Scythians (سيت) by them. Among them was the nation that attacked Rome, leading to the fall of the Roman Empire in that instance. It was also previously mentioned that what is derived from the books of the Old Testament is that this corrupting nation was from the inhabitants of the far north.

This is the sum of what we have summarized from his discourse. Although it is not free from some objections in certain aspects, it is more clearly applicable to the verses and closer to acceptance than others.

G - Among what has been said regarding Dhu al-Qarnayn is what I heard from some of my teachers: that he was from the people of some cycles (أدوار) preceding this human cycle. This is strange, and perhaps it was to validate what has been reported in the traditions regarding the wondrous states of Dhu al-Qarnayn and the strange events that occurred from him, such as his dying and coming to life time after time, his being raised to heaven and descending to earth, the clouds being made subservient to him to carry him to the West and East, and the subservience of light, darkness, thunder, and lightning to him. It is well known that the history of this cycle preserves nothing of that, so it must have been in some preceding cycles. This view was based on an excessive good opinion of those traditions.

4 - Exegetes and historians have delved deeply into the research surrounding the story and have spoken extensively on its various aspects. Most of them agree that Gog and Magog are a large nation in Northern Asia. A group of them applied what the Quran informed regarding their emergence at the end of time and their corruption in the land to the Mongol (Tatar) invasion in the first half of the seventh century AH upon Western Asia. They were excessive in destroying crops and progeny by demolishing cities, annihilating souls, looting wealth, and committing atrocities that no predecessor had surpassed.

They first subjugated China, then marched to Turkestan, Iran, Iraq, the Levant, and the Caucasus into Asia Minor. They annihilated everything that resisted them of cities, towns, and fortresses, such as Samarkand, Bukhara, Khwarazm, Merv, Nishapur, Ray, and others. A city would wake up with thousands of souls in it and by evening not a single person would remain to light a fire, nor a stone upon a stone of its buildings.

Then they returned to their lands, then came back and attacked the Russians, destroyed the people of Poland and the lands of Hungary, and attacked the Romans, forcing them to pay tribute—all in atrocities whose description would be lengthy.

However, they neglected to research the matter of the barrier from the perspective of their emergence from it and solving its problem. For the saying of the Exalted: فَمَا اسْطاعُوا أَنْ يَظْهَرُوهُ وَمَا اسْتَطاعُوا لَهُ نَقْباً قالَ هذا رَحْمَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّي فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي جَعَلَهُ دَكَّاءَ وَكانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي حَقًّا وَتَرَكْنا بَعْضَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَمُوجُ فِي بَعْضٍ "So they could not scale it, nor could they dig a hole in it. He said, ‘This is a mercy from my Lord. But when the promise of my Lord comes to pass, He will level it, and the promise of my Lord is true.’ On that day We shall leave them to surge against one another..." (18:97-99), its apparent meaning—according to their interpretation—is that this corrupting nation is imprisoned behind it, with no exit to the rest of the earth as long as it remains standing, until when the promise of Allah the Glorified comes, He will make it leveled or collapsed, and they will emerge from it to the people and spread corruption and evil.

Based on this, they ought to have established the description of this barrier (السد). If the mentioned nation refers to the Tatars, who marched from northern China to Iran, Iraq, the Levant, and the Caucasus, and then into Asia Minor, then where was this barrier described in the Quran—which they supposedly trampled over before emerging into these lands and turning them upside down?

And if it was not the Tatars or any other invading nations throughout history, then where is this barrier constructed of iron (السد المشيد بالحديد)? Its description is such that it has confined a massive nation for thousands of years, preventing them from attacking the rest of the world’s regions, with no exit to the rest of the world except through the barrier struck before them. Today, the various regions of the earth have become interconnected via land, sea, and air routes. No natural barrier—be it a mountain or a sea—nor any man-made one—be it a dam, a wall, or a trench—prevents one nation from reaching another. What meaning, then, remains for a people being blocked from the world by a barrier between two mountains, regardless of how it is described or how its existence is posited?

What I hold regarding the resolution of this problem—and Allah knows best—is that His saying: دَكَّاءَ (leveled) is derived from الدك (leveling/humbling) in the sense of "humiliation" or "insignificance" (الذلة). It is stated in Lisan al-Arab: "A leveled mountain (جبل دك) is one that is humbled (ذليل)." End quote. The meaning of making the barrier "leveled" (دكاء) is making it insignificant—such that its presence is no longer reckoned with, nor is it of any use, due to the expansion of communication routes and the diversification of means of movement and transport by land, sea, and air.

Thus, the reality of this promise is a promise of the advancement of human society in its civilization (مدنيته), and the drawing together of its various nations to a point where no barrier can block them, nor any wall encompass them to prevent movement from any corner of the earth to another, nor stop them from attacking or marching upon whichever people they wish.

This meaning is supported by the context of the saying of the Exalted in another place where He mentions the onslaught of Gog and Magog:

حَتَّى إِذَا فُتِحَتْ يَأْجُوجُ وَمَأْجُوجُ وَهُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ حَدَبٍ يَنْسِلُونَ "Until when Gog and Magog are let loose, and they gently flow down from every tier." (21:96)

Here, He expressed it as the "opening" (فتح) of Gog and Magog and did not mention the barrier.

Furthermore, الدك (leveling) has another meaning, which is "burying with earth" (الدفن بالتراب). It is stated in al-Sihah: "I leveled the well (دككت الركي)—meaning I buried it with earth." End quote. It has yet another meaning: a mountain becoming a mound of clay. It is stated in al-Sihah: "The mountains were leveled (تدكدكت الجبال)—meaning they became mounds of clay; the singular is dakka’ (دكاء)." End quote. Thus, it is possible to assume that the barrier is among the structures of ancient eras that have become buried under the earth due to violent winds, or submerged by the shifting of seas or the expansion of some of them, as established by geological research (الأبحاث الجيولوجية). By this, the problem is resolved; however, the previous explanation is more plausible, and Allah knows best.

Ayahs

قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُم بِٱلْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمَـٰلًا (١٠٣)
Say, ‘Shall we inform you who are the biggest losers in their works? (103)
ٱلَّذِينَ ضَلَّ سَعْيُهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعًا (١٠٤)
Those whose efforts are misguided in the life of the world, while they suppose they are doing good.’ (104)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَلِقَآئِهِۦ فَحَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فَلَا نُقِيمُ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَزْنًا (١٠٥)
They are the ones who deny the signs of their Lord and encounter with Him. So their works have failed. On the Day of Resurrection We will not give them any weight. (105)
ذَٰلِكَ جَزَآؤُهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ بِمَا كَفَرُوا۟ وَٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ ءَايَـٰتِى وَرُسُلِى هُزُوًا (١٠٦)
That is their requital—hell—because of their unfaith and for deriding My signs and apostles. (106)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ كَانَتْ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتُ ٱلْفِرْدَوْسِ نُزُلًا (١٠٧)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, they shall have the gardens of Firdaws for abode, (107)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا لَا يَبْغُونَ عَنْهَا حِوَلًا (١٠٨)
to remain in them [forever]; they will not seek to leave it for another place. (108)

(Commentary)

These six verses serve as a conclusion to what has preceded in the Surah, which explained how the polytheists (المشركون) were infatuated with the glitter of the worldly life (زينة الحياة الدنيا), how they felt secure with guardians (أولياء) besides Allah, and how they were afflicted with a veil over their eyes and deafness in their ears, followed by an evil end. They also serve as a prelude to what is to come in His saying at the end of the Surah: قُلْ إِنَّمَا أَنَا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ "Say, ‘I am just a human being like you...’" (18:110).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُم بِالْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمَالًا "Say, ‘Shall we inform you who are the biggest losers in their works?" (18:103). The apparent context indicates that the address is to the polytheists, and it is phrased as an allusion (كناية), for they are the ones intended by this description. This will approach explicit statement in His saying: أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآيَاتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَلِقَائِهِ "They are those who deny the signs of their Lord and the encounter with Him" (18:105); for those who deny prophethood (النبوة) and the Resurrection (المعاد) are the polytheists.

It has been said: He did not say "the biggest loser in work" (بالأخسرين عملاً) in the singular, even though the rule for the specification (التمييز) is to be singular and the verbal noun (المصدر) covers both the little and the much. This was done to signal the diversity of their works and to intend that loss (الخسران) encompasses all of them.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ ضَلَّ سَعْيُهُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعًا "Those whose endeavour goes awry in the life of the world, while they think they are doing good." (18:104). This is the information regarding "the biggest losers in their works." They are the ones whom He offered to inform the polytheists about in the previous verse. He identified them as those whose effort (السعي) has gone astray in the life of the world. The straying of effort is loss (خسران). He then followed it with His saying: "while they think they are doing good," and by this, their being the "biggest losers" was completed.

The explanation for this is: Loss (الخسران) and ruin (الخسار) in trades and efforts undertaken for the purpose of seeking profit only occur when the trade or effort fails to hit its mark, resulting in a decrease in capital or the wasting of the effort. This is what the verse expresses as the "straying of effort" (ضلال السعي), as if the effort lost its way and the journey ended in the opposite of its intended goal. A person might lose in his trade or effort due to a lack of training, ignorance of the path, or other accidental factors; this is a loss whose removal can be hoped for, as it is expected that the person will become aware, restart the work, and recover what was lost. However, sometimes one loses while being convinced he is profiting, and suffers harm while believing he is benefiting, seeing nothing else. This is the most severe loss, for there is no hope of its removal.

Furthermore, man in his worldly life has no concern other than striving for his happiness (السعادة), and has no interest beyond that. If he takes the path of truth and hits the mark—which is true happiness—then he has succeeded. If he misses the path while unaware of his error, he is a loser in effort, but his salvation (النجاة) may still be hoped for. But if he misses the path, hits upon something other than the truth, and finds comfort in it—such that whenever a glimmer of truth appears to him, his soul strikes a veil of turning away over it, and his arrogance and jahiliyyah-driven prejudice (عصبية الجاهلية) beautify his current state—then he is the biggest loser in work and the most failed in effort. This is because it is a loss whose removal cannot be hoped for, and there is no expectation that it will one day turn into happiness. This is His saying, the Exalted, in interpreting "the biggest losers in their works": "Those whose endeavour goes awry in the life of the world, while they think they are doing good."

Their reckoning of their work as good—despite the truth being manifest and the falsehood of their deeds being clear to them—is only due to their souls being attracted to the ornaments and trappings of the world and their immersion in desires. This prevents them from inclining toward following the truth or listening to the caller of truth and the herald of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being. The Exalted said: وَجَحَدُوا بِهَا وَاسْتَيْقَنَتْهَا أَنفُسُهُمْ "And they impugned them—though they were convinced of them in their hearts" (27:14), and He said: وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُ اتَّقِ اللَّهَ أَخَذَتْهُ الْعِزَّةُ بِالْإِثْمِ "And when he is told, ‘Be wary of Allah,’ he is seized by a pride [that drives him] to sin" (2:206). Thus, their following of their whims and their persistence in turning away from the truth out of stubbornness and arrogance, while being immersed in the desires of the soul, is nothing but their satisfaction with their state and their deeming their own conduct as good.

His saying, the Exalted: أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآيَاتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَلِقَائِهِ "They are those who deny the signs of their Lord and the encounter with Him" (18:105). This is a second identification and an explanation after an explanation for "the biggest losers in their works." What is meant by the signs (الآيات)—as required by the absolute nature of the word—are His signs in the horizons and within the souls, and the miracles brought by the prophets and messengers to support their missions. Thus, disbelieving in the signs is disbelief in prophethood (النبوة), especially since the Prophet himself is among the signs. What is meant by the "encounter with Allah" (لقاء الله) is the return to Him, which is the Resurrection (المعاد).

Thus, the definition of "the biggest losers in their works" boils down to those who deny prophethood and the Resurrection, which is a characteristic of the idolaters.

His saying, the Exalted: فَحَبِطَتْ أَعْمَالُهُمْ فَلَا نُقِيمُ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ وَزْنًا "so their works have failed, and on the Day of Resurrection We will not set up any scale for them." (18:105). The reason for the nullification (الحبط) of their works is that they do not perform any deed for the sake of Allah, nor do they desire the reward of the Abode of the Hereafter and the happiness of its life, nor is the motive for their work the remembrance of the Day of Reckoning. A discussion on nullification (الحبط) has already passed in the inquiries regarding deeds in the second volume of this book.

His saying: "and on the Day of Resurrection We will not set up any scale for them" is a derivative of the nullification of their deeds. The weighing (الوزن) on the Day of Resurrection is by the heaviness of righteous deeds, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: وَالْوَزْنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُّ ۚ فَمَن ثَقُلَتْ مَوَازِينُهُ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ وَمَن خَفَّتْ مَوَازِينُهُ فَأُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنفُسَهُم "The weighing on that day is a truth: as for those whose deeds weigh heavy in the scales, it is they who are the felicitous. As for those whose deeds weigh light in the scales, it is they who have ruined their souls" (7:8-9). Since there is no righteous deed due to nullification, there is no weight, and thus no scale is set.

His saying, the Exalted: ذَٰلِكَ جَزَاؤُهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ بِمَا كَفَرُوا وَاتَّخَذُوا آيَاتِي وَرُسُلِي هُزُوًا "That is their recompense—hell—because they were faithless and took My signs and My apostles in derision." (18:106). The word "That" (ذلك) refers to the description mentioned of them. The demonstrative pronoun is the predicate of a hidden subject, the estimation being: "The matter is that," meaning their condition is as we described; this is for emphasis. His saying: "their recompense is hell" is a new sentence informing of the end of their affair. His saying: "because they were faithless and took My signs and My apostles in derision" means because they disbelieved and increased in disbelief by mocking My signs and My messengers.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ كَانَتْ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتُ الْفِرْدَوْسِ نُزُلًا "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, they shall have the gardens of Firdaws for abode" (18:107). Firdaws (الفردوس) is used as both masculine and feminine. It is said to be "garden" in Roman; it is said to be "vineyard" in Nabataean, originally firdasa; it is said to be "garden of grapes" in Syriac; it is said to be "garden" in Ethiopic; and it is said to be Arabic, referring to a garden dense with trees, predominantly grapevines.

Some have inferred from the fact that the gardens of Firdaws are called an "abode" (نزلاً)—just as hell was previously called an abode for the disbelievers—that beyond Paradise and Hell there are rewards and punishments that have not been described. This might be supported by verses such as His saying: لَهُم مَّا يَشَاءُونَ فِيهَا وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ "There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more" (50:35), and His saying: فَلَا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ مَّا أُخْفِيَ لَهُم مِّن قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ "No one knows what has been kept hidden for them of the delight of the eyes" (32:17), and His saying: وَبَدَا لَهُم مِّنَ اللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يَكُونُوا يَحْتَسِبُونَ "and there will appear to them from Allah what they had never reckoned" (39:47).

His saying, the Exalted: خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا لَا يَبْغُونَ عَنْهَا حِوَلًا "to abide in them forever, and they will not desire to leave them for any other place." (18:108). Al-Baghy (البغي) is seeking, and al-Hiwal (الحول) is transformation or moving away. The rest is clear.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu al-Tufayl who said: I heard Ali bin Abi Talib (عليه السلام) when Ibn al-Kawwa asked him about: هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُم بِالْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمَالًا "Shall we inform you who are the biggest losers in their works?" He replied: "The wicked of Quraysh."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Imam bin Rab'i who said: Ibn al-Kawwa said to the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام): "Inform me about the saying of Allah: قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُم — until His saying — صُنْعًا." He replied: "Those are the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب); they disbelieved in their Lord and introduced innovations into their religion, so their works failed. And the people of al-Nahrawan (the Kharijites) are not far from them."

I say: It is also narrated that they are the Christians, by al-Qummi from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), and by al-Tabarsi in al-Ihtijaj from Ali (عليه السلام) that they are the People of the Book. In al-Durr al-Manthur, from Ibn al-Mundhir and Ibn Abi Hatim from Abu Khamisah Abdullah bin Qays from Ali (عليه السلام): "They are the monks who confined themselves in the cloisters."

All these narrations are of the nature of "application" (الجري) — the principle that Quranic verses apply to subsequent similar cases beyond their original context. The two verses occur in a connected context where the discourse is with the polytheists. The third verse, أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآيَاتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَلِقَائِهِ "They are those who deny the signs of their Lord and the encounter with Him," which interprets the second, applies more clearly to the idolaters than to others, as previously mentioned. Therefore, what was narrated by al-Qummi in his Tafsir under this verse—that it was revealed concerning the Jews and applied to the Kharijites—is not correct.

In Tafsir al-Burhan, from Muhammad bin al-Abbas with his chain from al-Harith from Ali (عليه السلام) who said: "Everything has a summit, and the summit of Paradise is Firdaws, and it is for Muhammad and the Progeny of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله)."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Bukhari, Muslim, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Abu Hurayrah that the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "When you ask Allah, ask Him for Firdaws, for it is the middle of Paradise and the highest part of Paradise; above it is the Throne of the All-Beneficent, and from it the rivers of Paradise gush forth."

In al-Majma', Ubadah bin al-Samit narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) who said: "Paradise has a hundred levels; between every two levels is like the distance between the heaven and the earth. Firdaws is the highest of them in level; from it the four rivers of Paradise gush forth. So when you ask Allah, the Exalted, ask Him for Firdaws."

I say: There are other narrations to this effect.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, from Ja'far bin Ahmad from Ubaydullah bin Musa from al-Hasan bin Ali bin Abi Hamzah from his father from Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام) in a tradition, he said: I asked about His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ كَانَتْ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتُ الْفِرْدَوْسِ نُزُلًا. He replied: "It was revealed concerning Abu Dharr, Salman, al-Miqdad, and Ammar bin Yasir; Allah made the gardens of Firdaws an abode for them, meaning a refuge and a dwelling."

I say: This should be understood as "application" (الجري), or that it was revealed concerning the true believers, and these four were mentioned because they are among the clearest instances. Otherwise, the Surah is Meccan, and Salman (رضي الله عنه) was among those who believed in Medina. Furthermore, the chain of this tradition is not free from weakness.

Ayah

قُل لَّوْ كَانَ ٱلْبَحْرُ مِدَادًا لِّكَلِمَـٰتِ رَبِّى لَنَفِدَ ٱلْبَحْرُ قَبْلَ أَن تَنفَدَ كَلِمَـٰتُ رَبِّى وَلَوْ جِئْنَا بِمِثْلِهِۦ مَدَدًا (١٠٩)
Say, ‘If the sea were ink for the words of my Lord, the sea would be spent before the words of my Lord are finished, though We replenish it with another like it.’ (109)

(Commentary)

The verse is an independent exposition of the vastness of the words of Allah (كلمات الله) — the creative decrees and existential signs that manifest His will and attributes — and their immunity to exhaustion. It is not improbable that it was revealed in isolation rather than as part of the sequence of the Surah’s verses; however, if it were revealed within the sequence, it would be connected to everything the Surah has discussed.

This is because the Surah pointed out at its beginning that there are divine realities. It first mentioned, by way of consoling the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) for his grief over their turning away from the Remembrance (الذكر), that the masses are in a slumber, heedless of perceiving these realities, and that they shall soon awaken from their sleep. In this regard, it cited the story of the People of the Cave. Then it mentioned matters in the course of which it cited the story of Musa and al-Khidr, where Musa witnessed acts performed by the latter that possessed an inner interpretation (تأويل) which Musa failed to perceive; their outward appearance distracted him from their inward reality until al-Khidr explained them to him, at which point his agitation subsided. Then it cited the story of Dhu al-Qarnayn and the barrier he erected by the command of Allah against the corrupters from among Gog and Magog, preventing them from entering what lay beyond it and spreading corruption therein.

These matters—as you can see—are affairs beneath which lie realities and secrets. In truth, they are "words" (كلمات) that disclose divine purposes and expositions that announce hidden truths to which the Wise Remembrance (الذكر الحكيم) invites the people. The verse—and Allah knows best—announces that these matters, which are His words (كلماته) that signify His purposes, do not exhaust. In its occurrence after the Surah has exhausted its various expositions, the verse is like the statement of a speaker who, after a long discourse, says: "There is no end to this discourse, so let us suffice with what we have presented."

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لَوْ كانَ الْبَحْرُ مِداداً لِكَلِماتِ رَبِّي Say, ‘If the sea were ink for the words of my Lord, the sea would be spent before the words of my Lord are spent, even if We brought another like it for replenishment.’ (18:109). The term "word" (الكلمة) is applied to a complete sentence just as it is applied to a single term. From this is His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ تَعالَوْا إِلى كَلِمَةٍ سَواءٍ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمْ أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا اللهَ Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you: that we will worship no one but Allah...’ (3:64). It has been used frequently in the Noble Quran for that which Allah has spoken or decreed, such as His saying: وَتَمَّتْ كَلِمَتُ رَبِّكَ الْحُسْنى عَلى بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ بِما صَبَرُوا And your Lord’s kindly word was fulfilled for the Children of Israel because of their patience... (7:137), and His saying: كَذلِكَ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَةُ رَبِّكَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ فَسَقُوا أَنَّهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ Thus was the word of your Lord fulfilled against the transgressors that they shall not believe. (10:33), and His saying: وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ ...and were it not for a prior decree from your Lord, it would have been decided between them... (10:19), among many other verses.

It is well known that the Exalted does not speak through the parting of lips; rather, His speech is His action and the existence He emanates, as He said: إِنَّما قَوْلُنا لِشَيْءٍ إِذا أَرَدْناهُ أَنْ نَقُولَ لَهُ كُنْ فَيَكُونُ All that We say to a thing, when We will it, is to say to it ‘Be!’ and it is. (16:40). It is only called a "word" (كلمة) because it is a sign (آية) pointing to the Exalted. From here, the Messiah was called a "word" in His saying: إِنَّمَا الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ اللهِ وَكَلِمَتُهُ ...The Messiah, Jesus son of Mary, was only an apostle of Allah, and His Word... (4:171).

From this, it becomes clear that there is no entity (عين) that exists nor any event (واقعة) that occurs except that it is—insofar as it is a sign (آية) pointing to Him—a "word" from Him. However, in the usage of the Quran, this term is specifically applied to that whose indication is manifest, containing no obscurity, falsehood, or change, as He said: وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ ...and I speak the truth. (38:84), and He said: ما يُبَدَّلُ الْقَوْلُ لَدَيَّ The word [of decree] is not changed with Me... (50:29). This is exemplified by the Messiah (peace be upon him) and instances of the decisive decree (القضاء المحتوم).

Thus, it becomes evident that interpreting "the words" (الكلمات) in the verse as His knowledge, His objects of power, or His promises to the people of reward and punishment—among other things mentioned by the commentators—is not sound.

So His saying: قُلْ لَوْ كانَ الْبَحْرُ مِداداً لِكَلِماتِ رَبِّي means: if the words were recorded and established in terms of their indication by using that sea as ink (مداداً), the sea would be exhausted before the words of my Lord were exhausted.

And His saying: وَلَوْ جِئْنا بِمِثْلِهِ مَدَداً means: even if We reinforced it with another sea, it too would be exhausted before the words of my Lord were exhausted.

Some have mentioned that what is meant by "its like" (بمثله) is the genus of "like" rather than a single equivalent. This is because any "like," when added to the original, remains finite (متناهي), whereas His words—meaning His objects of knowledge—are infinite (غير متناهية), and the finite cannot encompass the infinite. (End of summary).

What he mentioned is true, but not because of the discussion of finitude and infinity—even if the words are indeed infinite—but rather because the realities pointed to and the words, in terms of their indication, overwhelm all measures. How could it be otherwise? Every atom among the atoms of the sea, no matter what is hypothesized, does not suffice to record its own indication during the span of its existence regarding the Beauty and Majesty of the Exalted that it points toward; so how then if others were added to it?

In the repetition of "the sea" (البحر) in the verse by its explicit noun, as well as "my Lord" (ربي), there is a placement of the explicit noun where a pronoun would suffice. The subtle point (النكتة) in this is for reinforcement and emphasis, as is the case in specifying "the Lord" (الرب) for mention and annexing it to the first-person pronoun, which also carries an honorific (تشريف) for the one to whom it is annexed.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: أخبرك أن كلام الله ليس له آخر ولا غاية ولا ينقطع أبدا — "I inform you that the speech of Allah has no end, no limit, and it never ceases."

I say: In his interpretation of "the words" (الكلمات) as "speech" (الكلام), there is a support for what we have previously established.

Ayah

قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ أَنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ فَمَن كَانَ يَرْجُوا۟ لِقَآءَ رَبِّهِۦ فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلًا صَـٰلِحًا وَلَا يُشْرِكْ بِعِبَادَةِ رَبِّهِۦٓ أَحَدًۢا (١١٠)
Say, ‘I am just a human being like you. It has been revealed to me that your God is the One God. So whoever expects to encounter his Lord—let him act righteously, and not associate anyone with the worship of his Lord.’ (110)

(Commentary)

This verse is the conclusion of the Surah and summarizes the objective of the discourse within it. It has gathered the three foundations of religion (أصول الدين): monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts, prophethood (النبوة), and the return (المعاد) — the resurrection and final judgment. Monotheism is contained in His saying: أَنَّما إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ "that your God is the one God"; prophethood is in His saying: إِنَّما أَنَا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُوحى إِلَيَّ "I am only a mortal like you; it has been revealed to me," and His saying: فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلاً صالِحاً "let him act righteously" etc.; and the return is in His saying: فَمَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ رَبِّهِ "So whoever expects to encounter his Lord."

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُوحى إِلَيَّ أَنَّما إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ "Say, ‘I am only a mortal like you; it has been revealed to me that your God is the one God.’" The first restriction (القصر) limits him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to a humanity identical to the humanity of other people; he does not exceed them in anything, nor does he claim such for himself. This was in response to what they used to imagine—that if he claimed prophethood, he was claiming a divine nature (كينونة إلهية) or a supernatural power (قدرة غيبية). For this reason, they used to propose demands to him regarding things that none knows but Allah and none is capable of but Allah. However, he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله denied all of that from himself by the command of Allah, and established nothing for himself except that it is revealed to him.

The second restriction limits the God who is their God to being one God, which is monotheism (التوحيد) declaring that the God of all is one God.

And His saying: فَمَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ رَبِّهِ فَلْيَعْمَلْ "So whoever expects to encounter his Lord, let him act..." etc., contains a summary of the religious call (الدعوة الدينية).

This consists of righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) performed for the sake of Allah alone, without any partner. He has made this contingent upon the hope of encountering the Lord, the Exalted, which is the return (الرجوع) to Him. For if it were not for the reckoning (الحساب) and the recompense (الجزاء), there would be no motive calling one to adopt the religion or to commit to belief and action, as the Exalted said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسابِ "Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because they forgot the Day of Reckoning" (38:26).

He has arranged righteous deeds and the absence of polytheism (الإشراك) in the worship of the Lord upon the belief in the return. This is because belief in oneness (الوحدانية) alongside polytheism in action are two contradictory things that cannot coexist; if the God, the Exalted, is one, then He is one in all His attributes, including being the sole object of worship (المعبودية), having no partner therein.

He has based the adoption of religion upon the "hope" (رجاء) of the return rather than "certainty" (القطع) of it, because the mere possibility (احتمال) of it is sufficient for the obligation of taking precaution against it, based on the principle of the necessity of averting potential harm (دفع الضرر المحتمل). It has also been said that "encounter" (اللقاء) refers to the encounter of honor (لقاء الكرامة), which is hoped for but not definitively guaranteed.

He has made the hope of encountering Allah contingent upon His saying: أَنَّما إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ "that your God is the one God," because the return of the servants to Allah, the Glorified, is part of the complete meaning of divinity (الألوهية). To Him, the Exalted, belongs every sought perfection and every beautiful attribute, among which are acting with truth (فعل الحق) and judging with justice (الحكم بالعدل). Both of these necessitate the return of His servants to Him and the passing of judgment between them. The Exalted said: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما باطِلاً ذلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنَ النَّارِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ "We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is the opinion of those who are faithless. So woe to the faithless from the Fire! Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious?" (38:27-28).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mandah, Abu Nu’aym in al-Sahaba, and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated via al-Suddi al-Saghir, from al-Kalbi, from Abu Salih, from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "Jundub ibn Zuhayr, whenever he prayed, fasted, or gave charity and was mentioned favorably for it, he would feel pleased by it and increase in those acts due to the talk of the people. So Allah censured him, and regarding this, there was revealed: فَمَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ رَبِّهِ فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلاً صالِحاً وَلا يُشْرِكْ بِعِبادَةِ رَبِّهِ أَحَداً 'So whoever expects to encounter his Lord, let him act righteously, and not associate anyone with the worship of his Lord.'"

I say: Something similar has been mentioned in several other narrations without mentioning the name. It should be understood as the application (انطباق) of the verse to the case, for it is unlikely that the conclusion of one of the Surahs would be revealed solely for a specific personal reason.

In the same source, from Abu Hatim, from Sa’id ibn Jubayr regarding the verse, he said: "The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Indeed your Lord says: I am the best of partners (خير شريك). Whoever associates anyone from My creation with Me in his deed, I leave the entire deed to that partner, and I do not accept except what was purely for Me.' Then the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited: فَمَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ رَبِّهِ فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلاً صالِحاً وَلا يُشْرِكْ بِعِبادَةِ رَبِّهِ أَحَداً 'So whoever expects to encounter his Lord, let him act righteously, and not associate anyone with the worship of his Lord.'"

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from ‘Ali ibn Salim, from Abu ‘Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, said: 'I am the best of partners. Whoever associates [another] with Me in his deed, I do not accept it except what was purely for Me.'"

Al-Ayyashi said: In another narration from him عليه‌السلام, he said: "Indeed Allah says: 'I am the best of partners. Whoever acts for Me and for another, then it is for the one he acted for, and not for Me.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, Ibn Abi al-Dunya, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and al-Bayhaqi narrated from Shaddad ibn Aws, who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saying: 'Whoever prays to show off (يرائي) has committed polytheism (أشرك); whoever fasts to show off has committed polytheism; and whoever gives charity to show off has committed polytheism.' Then he recited: فَمَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ رَبِّهِ 'So whoever expects to encounter his Lord...' the verse."

In Tafsir al-Ayyashi, from Zurarah and Humran, from Abu Ja’far and Abu ‘Abdillah عليهما السلام, they both said: "If a servant performs a deed seeking thereby the mercy of Allah and the Abode of the Hereafter, but then introduces into it the pleasure of any person, he is a polytheist (مشرك)."

I say: The narrations in this chapter from the paths of both the Shi’a and the Sunnis are beyond counting. What is meant by polytheism (الشرك) in them is the hidden polytheism (الشرك الخفي) which does not contradict the essence of faith (أصل الإيمان), but rather its perfection. The Exalted said: وَما يُؤْمِنُ أَكْثَرُهُمْ بِاللهِ إِلَّا وَهُمْ مُشْرِكُونَ "And most of them do not believe in Allah without ascribing partners to Him" (12:106). Thus, the verse includes this by its inner meaning (باطنها), not by its literal occasion of revelation (تنزيلها).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Tabarani and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hakim, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'If nothing had been revealed to my nation except the conclusion of Surah al-Kahf, it would have sufficed them.'"

I say: The reason for this was previously mentioned in the preceding commentary.

Completed, and praise be to Allah.
